### 2. 7444748978140798976

- URL: https://www.linkedin.com/posts/patrickspychalski_i-dont-think-fully-autonomous-ai-sdrs-will-activity-7444748978140798976-2LCe
- Author: Patrick Spychalski (@patrickspychalski)
- Posted: 2026-03-31T14:14:33.136Z

#### Verbatim Content

I don’t think fully autonomous AI SDRs will replace real people any time soon. Here’s why:

This may be an opinion I get asked to defend more than any in my professional day-to-day, and I think it’s one worth talking about. I have been vehemently against fully autonomous outbound tools since they were released, and I’m convinced that they are bound to fail if they are truly “push button”.

The core reasoning is that AI output is truly the embodiment of “regression to the mean”. In other words, the models are specifically trained to answer a question based on the average output of human expression on a given subject.
In GTM, specifically top of funnel, regression to the mean poses a nearly guaranteed failure for any mature or commoditized market. Sending the message that everyone has already received 100 times is a surefire way to get ignored when sending outreach. This is the main reason “GTM Alpha” is centered around sending the message that nobody else has gotten before.

You could then say, well, why don’t you just prompt a model to draft a message that’s wild, different, and will capture attention? Well, this output will probably liken to a 55-year old trying to relate to their 18-year old daughter by quoting Tik-Toks- they may be using the right combination of words, but it just won't “feel right”. People have an uncanny way of sniffing out disingenuity. They’ll react the same way I do when I see a LinkedIn post with an em dash.

The linguistic nuance and research required to write a top 1% outbound message to a prospect is so precise that by the time you’ve optimized the research, tonality, and cadence necessary to write the perfect message, you’ve already written the perfect message. AI is no longer required.

As a result, the core of any GTM system we attempt to build at The Kiln is centered around our clients finding that perfect message, and us working to scale it. We use AI to write variable outputs for what is already defined, not to write entire messages. Plus, you can’t easily scale messaging when every message is completely different- there’s no way to measure what works and what doesn’t.

The only case where AI drafting copy will certainly work is when you've run dozens of campaigns for an offer that have gotten results, and the AI is trained on all of them. In other words, unless you're 🦾Eric Nowoslawski, don't do it. 

I truly do think the human touch still prevails in the majority of outbound use cases. From what we’ve seen with our most successful clients, I recommend not cutting corners on the message drafting front. Then, when you build an effective scaling mechanism, the thing actually works.

In conclusion- don't fire all of your SDRs and replace them with agents. Just build systems that make them more effective and have them focus on high-leverage work like cold calling. Or, hire a legendary copywriter/outbound strategist and pair them with a GTM engineer. Either way, don't get the AI SDR tool 🫡

### 3. 7425593840549658624

- URL: https://www.linkedin.com/posts/patrickspychalski_some-great-news-to-end-your-week-clay-finally-activity-7425593840549658624-QBGz

Some great news to end your week- Clay finally dropped delayed runs. 

Gone are the days where you have to create complex chron jobs in Clay just to delay an integration. If there was a category of features in the tool called "how does this not exist yet", this was at the top of the list. 

This is particularly exciting for any workflows where you want to create a "non-automated" vibe- sending automated emails the second a trigger is picked up can have a negative effect sometimes.

### 4. 7453451010296922113

- URL: https://www.linkedin.com/posts/patrickspychalski_the-day-has-come-clay-just-launched-an-activity-7453451010296922113-9ODU
- Author: Patrick Spychalski (@patrickspychalski)
- Posted: 2026-04-24T14:33:19.278Z

#### Verbatim Content

The day has come - Clay just launched an MCP 🔌 

It may not be the MCP you expected, though. Here's a rundown on what it does:

This is not an MCP you can use to build Clay tables in Claude Code. Instead, it's a rep-facing tool that allows sales team members to use the workflows you build much easier. 

Clay has always maintained that the platform shouldn't be used by reps, and the workflows within Clay should be connected to something much more user-friendly. I think the most natural progression of this thinking is, "what if we could call upon any workflow using an LLM like OpenAI or Anthropic? That's exactly what this tool does. 

Here's how it works:
1. Connect the Clay app to your LLM of choice (currently supports OpenAI and Claude)
2. Build a function in Clay to serve a specific task- enrichment, qualification, normalization, pushing to CRM, etc. 
3. Enable that function to be used by the MCP
4. Use it to your heart's content in the command line! 

I think the most exciting application of this could actually be pushing to CRM, sequencers, and data warehouse. I don't know about you, but I don't trust an agent to play around in my CRM just yet, but I do trust a pre-built Clay table to. This provides the best of both worlds- agentically prospect, then push to something that enriches, normalizes, and knows all of your system rules. 

Interested to hear people's thoughts on this, and what other capabilities they'd like this MCP to have!

### 7. 7457059537251078144

- URL: https://www.linkedin.com/posts/patrickspychalski_job-postings-have-more-hidden-gold-than-any-activity-7457059537251078144-U8OJ
- Author: Patrick Spychalski (@patrickspychalski)
- Posted: 2026-05-04T13:32:19.147Z

#### Verbatim Content

Job postings have more hidden gold than any other signal, and almost nobody is using them properly. 

Most people see job listings as a binary signal - the presence of a job listing, or lack thereof, signals whether a company has need for something. For example, if a company is hiring a person in RevOps, maybe they're changing tech stack. 

While I'm sure this can be somewhat helpful, what those same people miss is what's actually in the job descriptions. The job descriptions are a GOLD MINE. For large companies, you can essentially dissect everything that's happening within a business from job descriptions. Internal expansions, new initiatives, technology changes, overall hiring plans, and so much more. If you combine insights from several listings into one analysis, you pretty much have an x-ray into an org. 

I got on a call with Rafael Guerreiro earlier this week to chat through how to extract these insights. With powerful LLMs now able to comb through these and make broader assumptions he built Sentrion to analyze these job listings. The result is one of the cooler tools I've seen in a while. This is not a sponsored post, I genuinely thought this was so cool and had to share with people. 

Video walkthrough below ⬇️

### 9. 7442198305620643840

- URL: https://www.linkedin.com/posts/patrickspychalski_claude-code-enrichment-has-gotten-scarily-activity-7442198305620643840-j258
- Author: Patrick Spychalski (@patrickspychalski)
- Posted: 2026-03-24T13:19:05.418Z

#### Verbatim Content

Claude Code enrichment has gotten scarily good. Here are a few learnings for people just getting started:

We've been experimenting over at The Kiln on what use cases Claude currently do well - not just for indie hackers, but for enterprise clients looking to use it in their GTM stack. One use case that has become clearly a frontrunner is enrichment. A few thoughts-

1. The success of enrichment in Claude Code is still fully contingent on the tools you connect to it. 

While Claude Code is an incredible tool, it can't make company and person data appear out of thin air. For the large majority of use cases, you need to connect data apis to do anything meaningful. I've been using BlitzAPI for a lot of our LinkedIn enrichment- it's $500/mo for unlimited data, which is absurdly cheap and while most of the data is a flat file, it's generally very recent and reliable. Combine that with an email validator, and you can source great lists for the fraction of a tool like ZoomInfo. 

2. Writing your data to a database is amazing for solving context bloat. 

When you're enriching massive lists of companies and people, one thing you need to avoid is context bloat- giving the model that you're prompting a ton of information to ingest, decreasing performance. Connecting Claude to something like Supabase, so the model can read your database and reduce bloat, is absolutely necessary during large scale enrichment. Plus, it's a great way to visualize and gut check outputs. 

3. You need to be super specific on your desired output and process when using agents during enrichment. 

This isn't anything new, but is specifically important in the context of enrichment in Claude Code. If you're going to send an agent off to retrieve data for you, be very specific about where it should find this data and how the data should be formatted when it returns in the column of your database. Otherwise, you'll get left with a ton of false and unstructured information. Restructuring that data is going to take up time and you don't want to wait around for another 45 minutes tweaking a column. Even if the output looks good for the sample runs, it can still hallucinate a lot at scale without the right specification. 

Hope this is helpful! Would love to hear tips from others about what they've found from experimenting. 

P.S - if you're building in the data for Claude Code space, DM me! We're always looking for great partners with great data to work with :)

The Kiln has been acquired by 2X! 

When we started The Kiln 2.5 years ago, we did so with the goal of providing best in class service quality for the budding and quickly-growing GTM Engineering space. 

I truly feel like we have achieved this initial goal, and we've been able to work with some incredible companies along the way. However, we were missing one thing- the infrastructure to truly be the enterprise choice for large companies to reap the benefits of tools like Clay. 

2X provides this, and more- they're a team of 1200+ who have worked with large enterprise firms and have the resources to bring GTM Engineering to the next level. We truly feel that every enterprise B2B company should have GTM Engineers in their toolkit, and our hope is to be the group that does it. 

On a personal note, I'd like to thank God for all He has done to support me throughout this journey, The Kiln, Clay, 2X, Insight, and Recognize teams for their partnership, and for everybody who has supported us up to this point. 

Incredibly pumped for our next chapter together. 🚀

### 11. 7465071946846834689

- URL: https://www.linkedin.com/posts/patrickspychalski_we-gave-our-team-a-budget-to-buy-new-tools-activity-7465071946846834689-kS5Q
- Author: Patrick Spychalski (@patrickspychalski)
- Posted: 2026-05-26T16:10:46.458Z

#### Verbatim Content

We gave our team a budget to buy new tools without having to ask us. Here’s why you should do it too:

We’re a high ticket agency, and one of the expectations that comes with charging a lot is being at the cutting edge of GTM consistently.

A fact about the cutting edge is that it inherently isn’t widely adopted yet; the most revolutionary tools are often found deep in the crevices of group chats, edge cases, and slack channels working to find niche solutions to problems few people have to think about.

Who are the proverbial cave divers in GTM? The easy answer: not managers. Individual contributors are the people seeking out, testing, and validating these tools. We truly feel that tool discovery shouldn’t be top-down, and we should listen to the people actually doing the thing every day.

You may be thinking, well, I don’t work at an agency, so this isn’t relevant. However, I’d argue that finding alpha in tools that your competitors aren’t using is a concept just as salient, if not more, to non-agency companies.

Many of the tools we use today - Deepline, BlitzAPI, Watt, Recraft, Firecrawl, OutboundSync, and Superset just to name a few - were found by team members simply being creative to find a solution. Giving team members some money to test these tools using their personal emails can pay huge dividends.

So, if you’re a manager reading this, consider giving your team some budget to try new things. If you’re an IC, use this post as evidence it works. 

(And if one of those tools shows up on the company credit card statement as “Delta Flight to Turks and Caicos”, then assume your team member found a super important tool there and the trip was purely for business purposes 🏖️ )

### 12. 7465391361685483521

- URL: https://www.linkedin.com/posts/patrickspychalski_waterfall-enrichment-in-claude-code-has-arrived-activity-7465391361685483521-n61D
- Author: Patrick Spychalski (@patrickspychalski)
- Posted: 2026-05-27T13:20:00.890Z

#### Verbatim Content

Waterfall enrichment in Claude Code has arrived, and it has the potential to change the way we do sourcing. 

The team has been playing around with Deepline for the past few weeks, and it has the potential to be a great sourcing plugin for GTM Engineers working in Claude. 

The concept is pretty simple- you prompt Claude to source a list of companies or people in natural language, and Deepline puts the list together for you. 

What’s more interesting is that you can then enrich those leads for basic data points like phone numbers and emails using several different providers. 

What’s even MORE interesting is that it uses tools like Exa to perform agentic research tasks and write those to the table. 

You can then view it in a table format via their session id, which makes it easy to export, or you can just use their native Snowflake/Salesforce/HubSpot connections to write the information there. 

GTM is getting more and more agentic- curious as to whether people have used Deepline or similar tech in their sourcing yet!

### 15. 7442956077559795713

Claude Code and Google Sheets are a dangerous duo for lead enrichment ⚡ 

While most people see Google Sheets as a data input tool, it's actually a super powerful development environment. You can inject code, write complex formulas, make API calls, and more all from the free sheets tool. 

This used to take somebody technical to do, but with Claude Code, anybody can use it as the dynamic dev tool it was meant to be! Combine that with data APIs, it becomes a super powerful enrichment tool for GTM. 

Our Head of Education Elias Stråvik just shipped Rowbound, a free way to easily use the sheets CLI to create super robust GTM workflows within Google Sheets. This means that all of your formulas live within Sheets and not your Claude instance, making it SO much more effective than using the generic Sheets CLI. 

The below video is Elias and I talking about the implications of using Google Sheets with Claude Code, why it's such a great option for GTM specifically, and how to use it to supercharge workflows in the tool. 

It was a 30 minute convo and LinkedIn only allows 15, but I'll link the full video in the comments, as well as a link to Rowbound ⬇️  

Would love to hear people's thoughts or experience using Sheets with Claude so far!

### 18. 7403839377694490625

- URL: https://www.linkedin.com/posts/patrickspychalski_want-to-stop-getting-cold-emailed-heres-activity-7403839377694490625-HVmw
- Author: Patrick Spychalski (@patrickspychalski)
- Posted: 2025-12-08T16:54:24.326Z

#### Verbatim Content

Want to stop getting cold emailed? Here's how to prevent it:

I was chatting with Valentin Wallyn, founder of Findymail (one of the top email enrichment tools on the market), at the Paris Clay club last week. I was picking his brain on how these email tools are so accurate at finding valid work emails consistently. 

He uses a TON of different methods, but one of the core strategies is essentially pattern matching. In other words, guessing several different combinations using the person's name and their company's domain, and running each of these combinations against a validator to figure out which of these guesses is actually their email. Once you get a match, you have their email. 

This led me to ask- "so, if you use a super unconventional pattern for your email, like patspy456@thekiln.com, your odds of getting found are significantly lower, right?"

To my surprise, he said it does! Tools like Findymail usually guess the most common patterns when finding an email, and

Is is far less aesthetically pleasing than a normal email? 100%. Will it reduce your odds of getting outbounded to? Also yes. So, if you complain about getting cold emailed to and you don't have a weirdly structured email address, I'll know you don't hate them enough 🗣️ 

Insights like these only come from organic conversations at events like Clay Clubs, so if you haven't gone to the one in your city, I highly recommend it!

### 21. 7417638863994671106

- URL: https://www.linkedin.com/posts/patrickspychalski_weve-hired-a-lot-of-gtm-engineers-at-the-activity-7417638863994671106-ZXes
- Author: Patrick Spychalski (@patrickspychalski)
- Posted: 2026-01-15T18:48:38.242Z

#### Verbatim Content

We've hired a lot of GTM Engineers at The Kiln. Here are 3 great personas to look for when hiring one:

1. The software/forward deployed eng looking for a career pivot 

Why you should hire them: They're super technical, aren't constrained by no-code tools, and understand the code-level roots of everything they're building, which allows for creative ways to solve problems. 

When you should hire them: If you plan to build more than just outbound campaigns and crm enrichments- you want to build super robust, cutting edge automations that you're not even sure are possible yet

What to look out for: You will have to carry the burden of GTM strategy yourself. You should look to them for technical implementation, not ask them to write copy for you.


2. The ex-RevOps person who learned Clay to level up their game

Why you should hire them: They have deep understanding of the tools that connect to Clay (Salesforce, Outreach, Gong, etc.) and will eliminate a lot of headache when integrating them to Clay. 

When you should hire them: When you have an advanced CRM instance that requires a LOT of Clay-CRM integration, and/or if you don't have a dedicated RevOps person to work with your GTME.

What to look out for: Many people in this persona group stick to a few tools and don't want to change, which reduces flexibility in tooling to solve a problem. They're great for orgs that want to stick with their tooling outside of Clay, not iterate often. 


3. The ex-salesperson who wants to get into the technical weeds

Why you should hire them: They have a fundamental understanding of GTM strategy and know what sales teams want when building automations. They're also amazing at being client-facing and interacting with stakeholders.

When to hire them: You don't have a resident expert on sales strategy, or the majority of your workflows involve interaction with SDRs, BDRs, AEs, etc. 

What to look out for: Generally the least technical of the group, so there is often a learning curve when building out new workflows. Also limits use cases. 


BONUS PERSONA: A person new to GTM obsessed with automation and loves building with new AI tools. I try to talk to as many of these as I can- they're usually on the cutting edge and build the coolest stuff I've seen in the space. 


Interested to hear people's thoughts on these- we have all of these personas at The Kiln, A 2X Company and the combination is what makes everyone stronger!

### 22. 7421580230424702976

- URL: https://www.linkedin.com/posts/patrickspychalski_heres-how-to-automate-90-of-your-proposal-activity-7421580230424702976-gMxc
- Author: Patrick Spychalski (@patrickspychalski)
- Posted: 2026-01-26T15:50:13.226Z

#### Verbatim Content

Here's how to automate 90% of your proposal generation with Clay and Google Slides:

I made a video a few months ago where I used publicly available company info to auto-generate slide decks with Gamma. However, I had two small qualms with it- it was using zero first-party data, and the deck looked a little different every time. 

Luckily Akansh Gupta went ahead and fixed this for me with a v2. Here's what the table does:
1. Ingests a call transcript, and then enriches the person and company involved in the call
2. Uses the transcript to fill variables required by a Google Slides template we created
3. Sends the variables to a Google App Script, which duplicates our template proposal and fills it with the variable data 
4. Voila, proposal complete! 

For anyone using Gong or other call recording software with a good API, I highly recommend giving a variant of this a try. Quick disclaimer- this was meant as an example table, it will likely look a bit different in practice as you'll want to use the webhook from the call recorder as the table source, not a "find people" integration!

Quick video explainer below ⬇️

### 23. 7417987497797853184

#### Verbatim Content

Most companies essentially give up after an unanswered cold call. Here's how to keep the thread going automatically:

Our team member Akansh Gupta was chatting with a friend who was finding great success sending a WhatsApp message and voice note to prospects who didn't pick up his calls. So, he made a Clay workflow that:

1. Ingested all of the call data from a cold call tool (for example, Orum)
2. Enriches the prospect for more information
3. Drafts a WhatsApp message to the prospect based on their company data
4. Generates a voice message using ElevenLabs 
5. Sends both messages to their WhatsApp using Unipile

This is an incredible way to easily increase conversions on cold calls without much additional work! This is particularly helpful for locales where WhatsApp is prevalent (so, not the US), so if you're reaching out to people in other countries would highly recommend checking this out. 

A quick walkthrough below ⬇️

Paid Resources:

### 28. 7415053515137425409

- URL: https://www.linkedin.com/posts/patrickspychalski_ive-talked-to-so-many-people-who-love-using-activity-7415053515137425409-RwHk
- Author: Patrick Spychalski (@patrickspychalski)
- Posted: 2026-01-08T15:35:23.041Z

#### Verbatim Content

I've talked to so many people who love using Clay but have a hard time selling it internally. Here are a few ways we do it at The Kiln:

1. It's a better data provider than whatever you're using right now, and often less expensive. 

Clay simply can't be beat on data quality in 95% of cases, the only edge cases being niche private directories that don't integrate with Clay and contain data not available online. If you're using tools like Zoominfo, Cognism, or Apollo, then you're getting data from one provider and hoping that it's accurate. You're also likely getting the basic data points- emails, funding, websites, etc. that everybody has. Clay aggregates several data providers for each key point, and can find far more niche info than their competitors because of their agentic enrichment capabilities. Better data = more informed outreach, better decision making, and more closed-won. 

2. It's easily the best GTM workflow tool on the market.

Prospecting without automation is like trying to be an NFL wide receiver with both hands tied behind your back (sorry European readers, Google it). Clay is the best automation tool on the market- it has the best data, the most flexibility (essentially a dev environment for GTM Engineers), integrations with every major (and many minor) data providers, and enterprise scale. We're a tool-agnostic agency and still end up using Clay for 90% of the stuff we build because it's just the best solution for most problems that come to us. 

3. It creates massive efficiency gains.

If you work at an org with 50 reps, and you create an automated research workflow that saves each rep 1 hour of manual work per day, you have generated 6 additional people worth of work, saving 500k+ in the process. This is not a novel statement obviously, but I would venture to say any good GTM engineer could find at least that amount of efficiency delta in a traditional sales org and fix it. Most sales orgs at that size are a free-for-all, and a well-crafted series of Clay workflows with proper adoption can save a lot of time. 

4. You're not buying a tool, you're buying something to run your tools for you. 

Imagine that you have Zoominfo, Apollo, and People Data Labs for prospecting. You have Outreach, Orum and LinkedIn Sales Navigator for outbound. You have 6Sense, Common Room, and another random tool for signal aggregation. You're in Salesforce tracking all of your activity. That is SO many tools to be using during one work day, and realistically you don't need to be in most of them. Clay orchestrates all of these in one seamless interface and cuts down the amount of tools you actually need to operate in significantly. For the average rep, they should be in the outreach tools and that's about it. 


Hopefully this is helpful for the next time you plan to yell at your boss to buy Clay! 

I had more, but I hit my character limit on LI so maybe I'll make a part 2 🤔

### 30. 7463235490612146176

- URL: https://www.linkedin.com/posts/patrickspychalski_replacing-all-of-your-workflows-with-agents-activity-7463235490612146176-NnPF
- Author: Patrick Spychalski (@patrickspychalski)
- Posted: 2026-05-21T14:33:21.172Z

#### Verbatim Content

Replacing all of your workflows with agents is a bad idea. Here's why:

Listen, I'm as Claude-pilled as the rest of you are. It will fundamentally change the way we do work. However, having agents perform every task is a waste of money and often returns worse results than if you didn't. 

This point boils down to a core question- what should be agentic, and what should be deterministic? 

Well, agentic tasks should require some level of nuanced thought to perform. For example, assigning a score to a company based on their LinkedIn description should certainly be agentic. It's intaking a description and making a judgment call on how well it fits a heuristic model for scoring. This simply couldn't be done without the help of an LLM, or at least not nearly as effectively. 

On the deterministic side, if you decided to score a company using a set rubric (if revenue > $100m, then score it as x, if industry contains software, score it as x), this does not require an agent. It could be built for essentially free in a tool like n8n, or you could even have Claude write the code that eventually embeds itself in your workflow. 

The reason this is so important to distinguish between is that the cost for agentic tasks is almost always significantly higher. When applied at scale, it can get scary. Building an "army of agents" to handle everything for you may be burning through usage when it doesn't have to. 

My suggestion: break down each workflow into what has to be solved with an agent vs what doesn't. For anything deterministic, build a system that can handle it consistently at low to no cost, and have Claude act as a switchboard. In that scoring example, use Claude to spin up a program once that calls a few data providers and does the math instead of having an agent think about it each time. 

I highly recommend auditing your workflows for this, it may even free up another hour of your Max plan :)

### 31. 7420235468107128832

- URL: https://www.linkedin.com/posts/patrickspychalski_have-you-ever-wonder-how-data-providers-find-activity-7420235468107128832-1FDN
- Author: Patrick Spychalski (@patrickspychalski)
- Posted: 2026-01-22T22:46:36.905Z

#### Verbatim Content

Have you ever wonder how data providers find a company's tech stack? I asked experts so you don't have to:

At The Kiln, we've always found it important to figure out where the data comes from when we're working with clients. One that always perplexed me was tech stack- how the heck did they know what tools a company used? I was always interested to figure out why certain tools could be found, while others couldn't, and how data providers were seemingly looking under the hood at some very large companies. 

What I found was super impressive, and honestly, it made me realize why HG Insights' Clay enrichment cost that much. 

In short, they scrape a LOT of publicly available data. Like, millions and millions of datapoints to reveal a complete list of what tools a company uses. Examples of data they scrape include:
- Their website metadata (tool pixels often reveal themselves there)
- Job listings that mention certain tools
- Disclosures from publicly available companies
- Social posts from team members 
- News articles
... and I'm sure much, much more. 

Many of these companies use NLP models to identify any mention of a tool, then classify it under an existing list that they have internally. Then, they use a verifier to ensure that the tool callout actually alludes to a company using that software, not just talking about it (example- a company posting about their competitor in a blog post). 

This, imo, is very important to take into account when using tech stack tools, because it shows that the list of companies using x tool is not complete whatsoever. 

If you're sourcing a list of companies that use Clay, it will NOT be all-inclusive, so you should use it as a positive signal, but never exclusionary. Just because a tech stack tool doesn't find it, doesn't mean the company doesn't use it. 

It's also helpful to know before you spend money on a tech stack tool. Some tools will not be mentioned very much- therefore, it's likely not worth spending money on technographic data about it. So, before you buy one, ask the provider about the tool's coverage that you care about. 

I hope this was valuable!

Varun Anand and I have hit three Clay Clubs on our Euro tour so far, and it has been a blast! Here are some takeaways:

1. Europe is even more hype about Clay and GTM Engineering than I previously thought

We've had awesome turnouts to the clubs (with some of them even having waitlists) and the talks have been SO engaging. It's been so interesting learning about use cases we would have never considered, the most notable being a lead scoring system for yacht sales (check out Varun's LinkedIn for a post on this 🛥️ ). 

2. Europe-based Clay agencies are thriving. 

I've always said that carving our your niche in the Clay agency space is the best way to win early on. For many agencies in Europe, their location gives them a MASSIVE advantage. Native language, time zone, knowledge of data privacy laws, and other location-based advantages all allow for differentiation from US-based businesses. I've talked to a ton of agency owners absolutely crushing it, and it's been really insightful to trade notes with them.

3. There is Europe-specific GTM Alpha that makes Clay uniquely helpful to companies based there. 

I was left in a state of shock at our Stockholm club when I found out that you can look up EVERY person in Sweden and find essentially every piece of information about them, from their salary to their address. This data, combined with Clay enrichment and scaled data application, unlocks unreal possibilities. I highly recommend that every company (both in Europe and elsewhere) look into what data is accessible in their jurisdiction. 

Really appreciate the amazing organizers of the Lisbon (Olivier 🧪 Tytgat Rafael Guerreiro 🚀), Amsterdam (Jack Cane Davy Guijt Wouter Harink Wesley van Duin), and Stockholm (Axel Hägg Jesper Petersson 🚀) Clay Clubs for putting these events together! They have all been so well put together and it's been amazing meeting everyone :)

Next stop: planning to be in London from the 29th to the 3rd!

### 36. 7421968127535607808

- URL: https://www.linkedin.com/posts/patrickspychalski_clay-is-giving-away-50k-to-the-winner-of-activity-7421968127535607808-aTAL
- Author: Patrick Spychalski (@patrickspychalski)
- Posted: 2026-01-27T17:31:35.104Z

#### Verbatim Content

Clay is giving away $50k to the winner of their next Clay Cup. Here's how to train for the W:

1. Memorize every one of Clay's native integrations.

This might sound crazy, but it's impossible to finish a puzzle if you throw away some of the pieces. I credit a lot of my Clay learning to the early days when I was working there; Matt Maiale had me create a demo video for every integration (there were ~112 at the time) that Clay had. It allowed me to have full knowledge of every tool in its arsenal- how else are you supposed to know there was enrichment to find the distance between two locations?

2. Watch Clay videos from experts and try to replicate their tables. 

The event is timed- you only have 20 minutes to build a table. The more you need to strategize, the less time you have to actually create the thing. A great way to solve this is by getting in reps. I personally think that creating your own reps, while still helpful, will factor inherent biases to the kinds of tables you practice with. You have to be ready for anything- copying several people gives you more variety. Someone should be able to give you a goal, and the exact Clay table design pops into your head without much thought. This is when you know the mental map is complete. Some people I recommend watching - 🦾Eric Nowoslawski, Brandon Charleson, Matthew Lucero, and Clay's YouTube (semi-self plug). 

3. Learn Clay's more complicated functions like the back of your hand. 

These include, but are not limited to: Webhooks, HTTP API, Apify Integration, CRM integrations, Different Claygent models, Custom Waterfalls, Copywriting Integrations, and anything that takes more than a minute to set up. This is the stuff that, under pressure, will fold first. Practice to the point where mistakes are unlikely. I personally would always mess up some small part of the HTTP API integration, so if I was training, I would focus on that. Any expert knows their flaws, and I think it's worth working on yours for this. 

4. Bribe the judges. 

50k is SO much money. What's a few thousand to some judges for a hefty profit? (this is a joke Munnawar Hashim and Owen Ciha please have me back). 

Hope this is helpful! Best of luck to those who plan to compete 🏆

### 38. 7419410200484548608

- URL: https://www.linkedin.com/posts/patrickspychalski_jesse-aseoff-just-made-one-of-the-coolest-activity-7419410200484548608-4RYi
- Author: Patrick Spychalski (@patrickspychalski)
- Posted: 2026-01-20T16:07:17.771Z

#### Verbatim Content

Jesse Aseoff just made one of the coolest Clay tables I've seen in a while. 

I asked our team during all hands if they had anything interesting I could show off for content, but I genuinely wasn't expecting something this mind-blowing. 

Jesse believes heavily (as do I) in "showing your offer" to prospects when reaching out to them. It's far more powerful to say "check out this project I've built for you", than to say "can we get on a call so we can talk about our services". 

So, he built a table that:
1. Takes in a LinkedIn profile from a Lovable frontend
2. Extracts their company's logo and colors
3. Generates sample ad specs based on enriched company data
4. Sends the ad specs to an n8n workflow that generates the images associated with the specs, the sends the images back to Clay 
5. Creates draft social media information (bio, username, etc)
6. Sends all of the above data back to Lovable
7. Creates an entire interactive draft social media account demo for a prospect. 

Absolutely nuts. 

While this is one cool example, the general idea here is that many companies could make demos like this for their prospects, eliminating the "get on a call with me" friction usually associated with outbound and significantly increasing response rates. 

Jesse has offered to share step-by-step documentation on how this was built for free, so he'll put it in the comments ⬇️

### 41. 7419850541406683136

- URL: https://www.linkedin.com/posts/patrickspychalski_here-are-a-few-gtm-trends-that-ive-noticed-activity-7419850541406683136-VWsP
- Author: Patrick Spychalski (@patrickspychalski)
- Posted: 2026-01-21T21:17:03.228Z

#### Verbatim Content

Here are a few GTM trends that I've noticed across our clients over the past few months:

1. They're focusing a lot more on signals. 

I feel like there has been an interesting demand curve progressing with signals over the past few years. First, there was a hype train when tools like 6sense emerged and people opened their aperture to the world of intent. Then, people cooled off of them because they weren't the magic that people expected them to be. Now, custom signals have been all the rage. My personal opinion? Super direct signals (website intent) work quite well, while more abstractive signals (company just raised money) don't work as well. You might be thinking "Patrick, this is so obvious", and you'd be right, but I'm saying that they're not even worth the time setting up. Like, it's worth investing 100% of your time into niche signals, not 80%. Just ignore any fluff signals, they cause notification bloat if anything. 

 2. Many enterprise companies don't want fully automated outbound. 

I think many of us knew this from the start (including Clay), but AI tools aren't meant to steal jobs, they're meant to supercharge them. Enterprise companies would save millions of dollars a year if they fired BDRs and replaced them with Clay, but the intelligent GTM leader knows that BDRs are incredibly necessary in many cases. They have the brute force and flexibility required to actually get people on the phone. Almost all of the systems we've built recently are just salesperson supplements- automated research workflows, meeting prep, post-meeting CRM updates, the list goes on. We have worked with a ton of enterprise businesses and none of them want to fire BDRs. They just want them to sell more. However, its worth noting here that there are exceptions where automated outbound works for enterprise orgs, but generally the trend is against. 

3. They're focusing more on content-driven GTM. 

I think most companies have realized that relying solely on outbound campaigns to sustain your GTM motion is not the way- this isn't new. However, we've seen a huge increase in content-based prospecting, and gathering the insights for much more targeted prospecting. I'm talking social media monitoring, scraping engagement on posts, scraping company followers, exporting personal followers, and generally creating a machine that squeezes the juice out of every post, every connection made, and sorting them via an intent calculator. This was happening already in startup world, but I've been seeing a much more concerted effort from enterprise leadership to take advantage of it as well. I think it shows that companies are realizing you can build parasocial relationships with prospects before they even get a message from you, and that it really works if done correctly. I'm in full support of it, and frankly it's how a lot of our business at The Kiln has been generated. 

Interested to hear other trends people have seen, or any general thoughts!

### 42. 7416903660808568832

- URL: https://www.linkedin.com/posts/patrickspychalski_there-are-a-million-ask-your-business-a-activity-7416903660808568832-QNo6
- Author: Patrick Spychalski (@patrickspychalski)
- Posted: 2026-01-13T18:07:12.143Z

#### Verbatim Content

There are a million "ask your business a question" tools out there. Here is one of my favorites for GTM:

We've been toying around a bit with Dust recently and have been loving its functionality, particularly how easy it is to set up.

The most annoying aspects of other MCP tools I've tried are:
- They're difficult to get started with
- They don't integrate with Slack well
- The models aren't great (they don't answer the question completely, leave out context, etc).
- They haven't gotten the "team" functionality right (admin permissions, different user workspaces, etc.)

Dust seems to have addressed all of these, and makes it incredibly easy to create agents, connect MCPs, and deploy them where team members actually work.

In GTM, adoption is by far one of the biggest problems we face when launching systems, and Dust seems to be taking this into account by creating a super easy to use MCP and agent tool.

Btw, they aren't paying me to say this, I just really like it. Quick demo video below ⬇️

### 44. 7448001420097863680

- URL: https://www.linkedin.com/posts/patrickspychalski_clay-just-launched-an-ads-integration-and-activity-7448001420097863680-JSPY
- Author: Patrick Spychalski (@patrickspychalski)
- Posted: 2026-04-09T13:38:35.726Z

#### Verbatim Content

Clay just launched an Ads integration, and I think the real headline is being missed here.

Sure, it’s cool that you can sync your Clay lists directly to an ads platform (saves the 5 min it would take to do manually), but I think the real aspect of this launch to focus on is the coverage.

For those who aren’t running ads all day- uploading lists actually requires a process called identity-based hash matching.

In short, an encryption system creates what is called a “hash” to avoid personal information being uploaded to ads platforms.

The platform in question (LinkedIn or Meta) then checks the hash against their own user-base to try and find the profile associated with the hash. If it can’t find it, then you can’t run ads to them.

Coverage for LinkedIn is usually quite low, generally below 50%. Clay’s coverage averages over 60. This is a MASSIVE value add for targeting, and inevitably improves the quality of the ads you’re running.

I highly recommend using this function if you’re running ads- if not for the ease of use, at least to improve your coverage :)

I presented this Clay table in front of hundreds of people at their SCULPT conference last week. 

Here's a walkthrough for those that missed it:

...and yes, this is a Clay table that creates Clay tables. 

When presenting at SCULPT, I wanted to create something that had actual value for those attending, while also creating a little wow factor. 

I settled upon a CRM enrichment table that:
- Enriches companies for basic data like revenue, funding, sales team count, etc 
- Scores each company for ICP fit 
- Searches their tech stack to see which tech the company uses that Clay integrates with 
- Deep researches everything you could possibly want to know about their sales team
- Finds their revenue model 
- Creates a custom POC spec for our internal sales team to use when building a Clay table for them
- Uses Claygent navigator to login to my account, create a blank Clay table, and name it after the prospect
- Creates a full custom slide deck using Gamma's API, which is currently in beta for pro users

I was pretty pumped about the table, and hopefully everyone attending was too! 

If you want the template, feel free to shoot me a DM asking for it :)

also, huge shoutout to Petra Hajal for an amazing presentation to pair with my table!

Clay just launched the feature that might get it from $3B -> $10B.

It solves the biggest problem that users have faced to date- actually learning the product. 

Their new Sculptor feature lets you use natural language to build Clay tables. Think Lovable, but for Clay. 

I personally think this is a huge step for the business and arguably their most important feature to date (maybe besides Claygent). It's going to be massive for adoption, and going to make the tool so much more friendly for beginners than before.

It cuts out the agencies that could just "use Clay" and bolsters the need for those who can thoughtfully implement the tool into a broader GTM system, particularly at larger companies.

### 50. 7365744300741115905

- URL: https://www.linkedin.com/posts/patrickspychalski_clay-just-made-their-local-leads-data-10x-activity-7365744300741115905-UbW2

Clay just made their local leads data 10x better. 

For anyone who has tried to source complete local leads lists in Clay before, you know that their Google Maps source was super incomplete. 

It would rarely return the true number of results in a given radius, and at The Kiln we would always have to rely on an external data provider. 

Their integration with Openmart is a MASSIVE unlock here. It can find thousands of results at a time, and provides more data on each company than the Google Maps integration could. 

That, combined with Claygent Navigator, means you can extract local leads data easier and at a higher accuracy than any time in human history.

### 52. 7370821687971389442

- URL: https://www.linkedin.com/posts/patrickspychalski_new-to-clay-here-are-three-essential-use-activity-7370821687971389442-0VYC
- Author: Patrick Spychalski (@patrickspychalski)
- Posted: 2025-09-08T14:13:53.657Z

#### Verbatim Content

New to Clay? Here are three essential use cases that I would recommend trying:

1. Automated or Semi-Automated Outbound 
The three main outbound use cases that we come across at The Kiln are a) completely automated outreach, b) pre-drafted copy for sdrs/bdrs to review/edit prior to sending, and c) an automated research workflow that allow for sdrs/bdrs to focus on outbound without needing to do research themselves. Regardless of which you decide to choose, the flow looks pretty much the same.

2. Inbound Lead Enrichment/Scoring
Dealing with inbound leads can get annoying, because inevitably the data payload from each source varies in content and format. Clay solves the formatting problem, and you can turn a bunch of unstructured data into a unified lead source. Then, you can use their unreal enrichment capabilities to score inbound leads to figure out whether they're worth engaging or not. 

3. CRM Enrichment 
To this day, Clay's most valuable use case across the board imo. Every company with a CRM should have this, and it solves so many data integrity problems I couldn't even fit them in the post. It turns a messy, stale CRM into a constantly updated and enriched source of truth, which is what it was supposed to be in the first place. Can't recommend doing this enough- it's been a gamechanger for so many companies already. 

Interested to hear if people would add any others to this list of essentials! ⬇️

### 57. 7363231450101366784

- URL: https://www.linkedin.com/posts/patrickspychalski_less-than-10-of-gtm-workflows-actually-add-activity-7363231450101366784-Eoxu
- Author: Patrick Spychalski (@patrickspychalski)
- Posted: 2025-08-18T15:32:59.929Z

#### Verbatim Content

Less than 10% of GTM workflows actually add value AND are fully adopted by teams.

This isn't because tools like Clay, n8n, etc. are ineffective. It's often because of these two problems:

1. The workflow is too out-of-the-box.

A tool that touts ease-of-use is a poison apple. In my experience, workflows are like snowflakes- no two are the same even if they look super similar. There are usually formatting discrepancies, differences in responsibilities held by team members, different technologies used by the team that need to be integrated, the list goes on.

Most GTM tools fail because they're too basic on the backend. Easy is amazing when you're abstracting a complex thing down to an easy format. ChatGPT is a great example of this. But, when the workflow doesn't PERFECTLY fit a business' systems, it's over before it even started. Custom is king.

2. The workflow isn't easy for team members to use. 

A quote from a mentor of mine that often proves to be true- "if you want a team to adopt a new system, you'll have to tell them to do it at least 7 times before it actually happens".

Almost every tenured team member at a company is biased towards not changing their existing systems. Changing anything is a risk and an inconvenience. You should actively assume that they don't want to adopt whatever you're building. The only way you have a chance of this happening is by making it easy to use. They should be spoon-fed something that actively makes their lives easier.

3. It never added value to begin with.

Hot take, but I think 95% of AI GTM tools don't add any value at all. My entire agency is incentivized to find tools that actually do add value, so I can say this with more confidence than most. Because of this, every company should be allocating 90% of their GTM tech budget to 3-4 tools that actually do. Search for depth in tools, not for breadth.

How do you determine value? If an AI tool doesn't: 
- Save your team a ton of time in doing a task
- Improve the effectiveness of an existing system
- Make better decisions than you about a topic on a consistent basis
Then get rid of it. 

In short- build custom, optimized, and easy to use workflows that add real value as soon as they're implemented. That's the only way they'll actually work. 

I'm planning to show a few examples of these workflows in Midnight to Millions hosted by 🤙Neil Weitzman on November 6th- link in the comments to register!

### 58. 7349436398606725121

- URL: https://www.linkedin.com/posts/patrickspychalski_clay-has-started-to-democratize-6-figure-activity-7349436398606725121-KQDV
- Author: Patrick Spychalski (@patrickspychalski)
- Posted: 2025-07-11T13:56:23.353Z

#### Verbatim Content

Clay has started to democratize 6-figure intent signal providers. 

Gone are the days of massive annual contracts just to find out who's been watching a YouTube video about your product category recently. 

I have always been somewhat of a skeptic about "intent" providers that track data about whether people are in-market for a specific set of products. To me, many of the signals seem weak and the data hard to action upon. 

However, there are certainly some edge cases where I can imagine this data being ridiculously valuable. The problem was, companies had to pay massive upfront fees just to find out whether that was the case for them. 

Now, Clay's TrustRadius source lets you do this on a per-lead basis instead of having to fork over half of your tech budget. I created a quick video walking through how it works, how to use it, and why I think this is huge for companies wanting to use intent ⬇️

### 59. 7348069071134900224

- URL: https://www.linkedin.com/posts/patrickspychalski_if-your-gtm-tech-stack-feels-bloated-it-activity-7348069071134900224-HHMN
- Author: Patrick Spychalski (@patrickspychalski)
- Posted: 2025-07-07T19:23:07.080Z

#### Verbatim Content

If your GTM tech stack feels bloated, it probably is. 

I've been asked several times over the past few months on what the "full tech stack" we use over at The Kiln is, and honestly, the list is insanely short. 

Like 80% of GTM systems work can be done using a combination of n8n, Clay, a CRM like HubSpot/Salesforce and a sequencing tool like Salesloft or Smartlead. 

This doesn't mean the work is easy, as often these systems take a ton of time to build and integrate properly into an existing stack. However, I still think many leaders feel that procuring a dozen different of enterprise GTM tools will solve their lead gen problem. 

You shouldn't buy tools like you hire employees. Tools alone won't solve your problem, and often they'll just create more. Before you know it, you'll have three team members hacking away at connecting Useless Tool 1 to Useless Tool 2, and the business problem you were attempting to solve will be left on the side of the road wondering whether it'll ever get picked up. 

For people who have read this far- feel free to comment a use case and I can respond telling you what tools we use for it. 

Hopefully this saves you some of that hard-fought GTM budget so you can use it well!

### 61. 7358487625004949504

- URL: https://www.linkedin.com/posts/patrickspychalski_clay-just-raised-100m-at-a-31b-valuation-activity-7358487625004949504-2cPG
- Author: Patrick Spychalski (@patrickspychalski)
- Posted: 2025-08-05T13:22:43.890Z

#### Verbatim Content

Clay just raised $100m at a $3.1B valuation. Here are my thoughts on this announcement:

1. This is a clear sign that the GTM Engineer role is here to stay. 

In my mind, investors made this decision under two broad assumptions- that Clay is going to get easier to use, and that companies will continue to hire people that administer the tool. There are a ton of features Clay could launch to increase ease-of-use, but ultimately a tool with this much flexibility will always require a learning curve. Even if its 5x easier to use, Clay is so full of possibilities that unlocking the true value is a function of one's ability to navigate them. GTM Engineers are a necessity in this value-maximization case.

2. Clay is on its way to become more than a GTM tool.

Zoominfo, whose name is so synonymous with GTM that the acronym is literally their stock ticker, is valued at $3.6B. Investors are valuing Clay quite close to that, while presumably thinking that they're getting a deal. To me, this shows that they see Clay becoming bigger than GTM, but a tool for growth as a whole. Given the platform's current architecture, this is more than feasible. Clay has been built for progress. 

3. Every other GTM tool in the space should think deeply about their moat.

I don't mean this in the conventional "companies should be worried about their competition" way, as this is pretty obvious. I mean it in the sense that this AI race will ultimately end in an oligarchy of winners. I think the small group of powerhouse companies that rise up will do so as a result of brand recognition and differentiated value. Clay already has brand recognition (a previously unseen level of LinkedIn hype) and differentiated value (better data than every GTM tool, and still improving). The problem for other GTM AI startups is at this valuation, Clay's coming for the market, not a sector of it. Because of this, it's worth thinking to oneself, "could my product become a Clay feature overnight?" before raising money. 

As the owner of a Clay agency, I'm ridiculously hype for the team and of course, incredibly optimistic about Clay's future. MASSIVE congrats to Kareem Amin, Varun Anand and the rest of the team for this massive achievement :)

Also, check out the NYT announcement article in the comments- they even mention Claygencies! Shoutout to 🦾Eric Nowoslawski for representing us 🫡

Incredibly proud of our team for their great work, and hoping to continue striving for the best quality of output in the space. This is a cool step, but I think we have so much more to do in order to achieve services perfection.

Huge shoutout to the partnerships team at Clay for creating such an amazing ecosystem, and to our fellow partners for working alongside us towards the same goal of great service! 🏋‍♂️

### 64. 7340371140898381824

- URL: https://www.linkedin.com/posts/patrickspychalski_everybody-should-be-paying-attention-to-n8n-activity-7340371140898381824-yrlh
- Author: Patrick Spychalski (@patrickspychalski)
- Posted: 2025-06-16T13:34:17.491Z

#### Verbatim Content

Everybody should be paying attention to n8n. 

In my opinion, it's the best general workflow tool in the world right now. Here's why:

1. It's ridiculously flexible. 

I have a hard time thinking of a workflow that couldn't be built in N8N with enough skill and time. It provides all of the building blocks to make something that meets your exact specifications. Tools like Zapier rely on direct integrations to create workflows, while N8N uses nodes that can include javascript, custom api connections, and more. Plus, they have a ton of workflow functions like delays, branches, triggers, and more. 

2. It's SO inexpensive.

We self-host our N8N server and it literally costs us $7/month. The only other costs associated with our workflows are API credits for AI tools like Anthropic or OpenAI. That's tough pricing to beat, and it allows us to think of building without really any concern for cost. 

3. It's AI-forward. 

N8N has better AI features than any other workflow tool I've used. You can call upon any model super easily, you can use AI to parse through and transform data, and you can even train your own agents without much work at all. It's adapted to changes in the AI space incredibly well, and I commend them for that.

The below video is meant to serve as a quick high-level on the tool, as well as a demo of some of it's capabilities. I would love to check out any workflows others have built in the tool as well! Let me know thoughts ⬇️

### 65. 7355967561630457856

- Author: Patrick Spychalski (@patrickspychalski)
- Posted: 2025-07-29T14:28:53.961Z

#### Verbatim Content

Here's how to build a meeting summary chatbot for $7/month:

I tasked Akansh Gupta with creating a bot that I could ask meeting questions to without having to peruse the entire transcript for the answer. It took him four n8n nodes and a Lovable frontend to do it. 

This workflow can add some quick value to anybody who has a lot of calls in a week, and the only cost was our $7/mo self-hosted N8N server, so I wanted to demo it so you (or your small team) can save money instead of purchasing a more expensive tool that would do the same thing. 

If I had more time to work on this, I'd add a Slack and Notion integration to make this more of a company-wide chatbot instead of limiting the scope to my calls. Regardless, I think it's better than spending the extra 10 mins finding a call and reading through the whole transcript myself :)

Here's a video walking through it ⬇️

Claygent Navigator almost turned me into a pastry chef 🧑‍🍳 

Well, I'm sure I wouldn't ever get the job (I can't bake), but it did help me apply.

Claygent's new iteration is absolutely nuts. For those who have used OpenAI Operator before, you probably have a general idea of what this does. 

In short, it can take actions on a website, in an app, or in a document to extract even better data than it was able to before. It can also complete form fills if needed. 

While I have a feeling that companies will likely have bigger restrictions on their form fills because of things like this, it's perfect for the use case it was actually built for, which is paginating through complex web pages, logging in to software, filling out forms, etc to get more nuanced data for use in Clay. 

The main use cases that came to mind for me, besides trying to become a pastry chef, were logging in to hard-to-navigate public databases and paginating through directories to find data that you care about. 

Super interested to hear how others have considered using it!

### 67. 7333192457574117376

- URL: https://www.linkedin.com/posts/patrickspychalski_clay-just-launched-custom-signals-here-are-activity-7333192457574117376-n3Cq
- Author: Patrick Spychalski (@patrickspychalski)
- Posted: 2025-05-27T18:08:46.013Z

#### Verbatim Content

Clay just launched custom signals. Here are my thoughts:

1. They're super powerful if used correctly.

Signals have been a popular GTM buzzword for a while, so almost everyone knows that they can work well. However, the difference with Clay's signals are that you can create ones that competitors aren't. Every company with a mature GTM team has been tracking the same 5-10 signals for years now. This unlocks some real alpha in a business' GTM strategy, because their creativity isn't nearly as limited by technological constraint.

2. They subtly exposes the tech behind signals, and will likely lead to a lot of signals companies going out of business.

This is the ultimate example of "your product is a feature" being put into action. Companies (I wont name names) used to package signals as this magical box that outputs interesting information that you can action upon. Now, it's pretty clear that every "signal" is just an API call to a data provider with a dedupe mechanism built into it. Honestly, I'm glad Clay indirectly exposed how this was done, as it makes ideating relevant signals much more accessible.

3. For existing Clay users, this is essentially just a credit saving mechanism. 

You could pretty much do the custom signals thing in Clay before this feature released, using scheduled enrichment runs. The only difference is that now, you only pay for new results- before, it would cost you a credit per row per search, regardless of whether new results turned up.  Saves a ton of credits, and makes signals more financially feasible for larger data sets. 

I've included a quick tutorial of how to use them below! ⬇️

### 72. 7335667025056354306

- URL: https://www.linkedin.com/posts/patrickspychalski_there-are-thousands-of-ai-sales-tools-out-activity-7335667025056354306-o-hd
- Author: Patrick Spychalski (@patrickspychalski)
- Posted: 2025-06-03T14:01:48.876Z

#### Verbatim Content

There are thousands of AI Sales tools out there. Here are 5 you should be using:

1. n8n

N8N is far and away the best general workflow tool I've ever used. It feels like the possibilities are absolutely endless in what you could create with it, and the UI is incredibly intuitive for anybody who has used tools like Zapier and Make before. The AI integrations are UNREAL. You can create agents using any AI model, trained on your own data, in minutes. 

2. Lovable

Lovable is at the forefront of vibe-coding, specifically for assets like web apps, dashboards, graphic design assets, and more. They've skyrocketed to $60M in ARR for a reason. You can create unbelievably valuable software just by using plain english, and the benefits for sales teams here are huge. Check out my recent post using it to create software at scale, pinned in my profile. 

3. Exa

Exa's product, or something like it, is the future of lead sourcing. For years, our minds have been constrained by basic filters for creating lead lists. Exa allows you to source your exact ICP using plain english, instead of having to use revenue and industry filters to approximate who you're targeting. I highly recommend playing around with their websets tool. 

4. Anthropic MCPs

MCPs allow you to interact with any technology like it's an employee. Simply sent a command to your MCP server and perform an action, return data, or help create insights super easily. We use MCPs internally at The Kiln for campaign analysis, which help us quickly find new efficiencies in campaigns we otherwise would not have found via traditional dashboards. You may not know it, but you'll be using MCPs for nearly everything in a year or so. 

5. Clay

Anyone who follows this profile knows what Clay is already, but for those who don't, it's the best workflow and enrichment tool for sales teams on the market, bar none. It has better enrichment data than any tool ever, and this combined with powerful AI workflows allow you to create incredible things. 

Any that you think I'm missing? Let me know!

### 74. 7325887578379870210

- URL: https://www.linkedin.com/posts/patrickspychalski_this-is-the-most-important-clay-table-that-activity-7325887578379870210-YN9s
- Author: Patrick Spychalski (@patrickspychalski)
- Posted: 2025-05-07T14:21:47.095Z

#### Verbatim Content

This is the most important Clay table that I've ever made. 

I truly think this represents the future of GTM. Spoiler- this table literally ideates and builds software at scale. 

This table has the ability to:
- Enrich a company for dozens of data points including brand colors, company description, website data and more
- Ideate a software, game, or web app for the prospect that would add value to their business
- Builds the entire project that it ideated, automatically, in Lovable
- Returns a link to the completed software back to Clay
- Writes a personalized messaged based on what we built

I truly think the game has changed. You can now build an entire piece of software at the click of a button. Better yet, you don't even have to ideate it, and you can build thousands of them at a time. 

The value add here is incomprehensible, and it makes the power of Clay and Lovable even more unreal.

Massive shoutout to Alexander Wikström for the API plug, Elias Stråvik for showing me the power of Lovable, and Akansh Gupta for the stellar idea on this. 

Super interested to hear all of your thoughts on this 🚀

### 76. 7328835410812440576

- URL: https://www.linkedin.com/posts/patrickspychalski_its-crazy-that-companies-still-assume-they-activity-7328835410812440576-RuZG
- Author: Patrick Spychalski (@patrickspychalski)
- Posted: 2025-05-15T17:35:25.115Z

#### Verbatim Content

Its crazy that companies still assume they have to choose between writing highly personalized emails that take hours, or automating outreach that gets ignored.

There are tools out there now that make it truly easy to that blend relevance and scale without compromising either.

At The Kiln we’ve been building outbound campaigns for our clients that feel bespoke for each lead, but are actually powered by systems on the backend that let us move fast. 

One recent example involved an e-commerce client where we enriched their account with real-time site speed, monthly traffic estimates, and average product pricing directly from their storefront.

From there, we crafted messaging that tied performance to revenue — showing how a one-second improvement in load time could result in thousands of dollars in additional monthly sales. The email didn’t just look personalized — it was grounded in data they could verify themselves, and it connected to a real business outcome.

This works because it uses publicly available data that tells a clearer story — one that the prospect already knows, but hasn’t yet connected to what you’re offering.

The takeaway for anyone struggling with outbound is to stop treating personalization as a novelty and start treating it as a way to deliver value upfront. 

With the right tooling and workflows in place, this level of relevance is absolutely possible to achieve at scale.

Thanks to the Smartlead team for having me on to chat through this!

### 77. 7323351368902008834

- URL: https://www.linkedin.com/posts/patrickspychalski_most-companies-are-sitting-on-so-many-valuable-activity-7323351368902008834-s14I
- Author: Patrick Spychalski (@patrickspychalski)
- Posted: 2025-04-30T14:23:47.635Z

#### Verbatim Content

Most companies are sitting on so many valuable customer insights— but have no system to act on them.

Clay just launched a new integration to help change that.

TLDR: last week Clay launched an integration with Gong that pulls call intelligence directly into the tool.

For any startup or scale-up, hundreds of critical conversations happen every week — packed with buying signals, objections, and opportunities.

The old way of indexing these insights looked like:

- Manual call reviews.
- Slack channels drowning in call notes.

Now, you can turn every insight into action immediately.

Here’s a simple play you can run today:

1. Set up a signal to track when prospects mention integrations your product connects with — one of the biggest buying triggers in a deal.

2. Push those signals into Clay automatically.

3. Use Claygent to instantly draft a hyper-relevant follow-up email that links to demo videos, case studies, or your integration marketplace.

This not only saves your account executives a bunch of time, but ensures your prospects get impactful, timely information which speeds up sales cycles!

And this is just one of dozens of plays this integration unlocks (included a few more in the image below)

Kudos to both teams, I love this new functionality 👏

### 78. 7325539786159476736

- URL: https://www.linkedin.com/posts/patrickspychalski_the-future-of-gtm-engineering-wont-just-activity-7325539786159476736-kHrZ
- Author: Patrick Spychalski (@patrickspychalski)
- Posted: 2025-05-06T15:19:46.968Z

#### Verbatim Content

The future of GTM engineering won’t just involve being good at Clay.

Don’t get me wrong — Clay is one of the most powerful tools out there.

But if you want to stay ahead, you’ll need more than just smart workflows and enrichment. You’ll need to build completely new experiences.

Here’s why:

Every GTM tactic eventually saturates.

Mass email → worked, then dropped off

Personalization with {first_name} → worked, then got ignored

AI-written messages → short spike, now table stakes

What’s next in my opinion?

Custom builds, full-stack creativity, and GTM engineers who think like product people.

Here’s an example:

Our team (shoutout Akansh Gupta and Elias Stråvik) built branded SEO dashboards for outbound — auto-generated with each company’s logo, color scheme, and performance data. 

It wasn’t just built in Clay. It was spun up in Replit using custom code.

The result of this campaign?

Every prospect got something relevant, and impossible to ignore.

This is the direction we’re heading in:

GTM engineers who combine no-code, code, and creativity to build assets provide value to prospects!

That is the future imo!

### 79. 7329184952812511233

- URL: https://www.linkedin.com/posts/patrickspychalski_sendosos-acquisition-of-postal-is-representative-activity-7329184952812511233-i21Q
- Author: Patrick Spychalski (@patrickspychalski)
- Posted: 2025-05-16T16:44:22.422Z

#### Verbatim Content

Sendoso’s acquisition of Postal is representative of two things:

1. They’re crushing it, enough to buy a massive company in their space. Hopefully some of our work with them at The Kiln helped contribute :)

2. People still prefer forming relationships instead of being lectured to about a product or service.

The second point seems to be forgotten in the world of automation. I’ve found that in sales, people often form a disconnect away the golden rule of selling to others how you would want to be sold to. 

You should not use automation to replace human connection. You should use it to accelerate the processes that allow for relationships to be formed. 

I really think Sendoso helps solve this problem. Every time someone sends a gift to me, I’m immediately more of a fan and more interested to chat with them. Call me a sucker, but wow does it work, and Sendoso does it in the most sales-friendly and scalable way. 

Big shoutout to 🎁 Katie Penner and the rest of the team for the piñata! And for being such a fun client 🫶🏼

### 81. 7324085456256520194

- URL: https://www.linkedin.com/posts/patrickspychalski_lookalike-audiences-are-one-of-the-most-reliable-activity-7324085456256520194-GJ-E
- Author: Patrick Spychalski (@patrickspychalski)
- Posted: 2025-05-02T15:00:47.699Z

#### Verbatim Content

Lookalike audiences are one of the most reliable ways to scale outbound without starting from scratch.

If you’ve already closed a customer and they’re getting results, your next move should be:

Who else looks just like them?

At The Kiln, we’ve used Ocean.io for years to build high-signal outbound lists using this exact model.

They just rolled out a major update—and it’s made a strong tool even stronger. 

1. New lookalike algorithm

List quality is up. Fewer false positives. Better alignment on industry, size, and intent signals.

2. Prospect-level lookalikes (here’s how we’re using it)

You can now find people who resemble your best buyers—by title, function, and company type.

Here’s how we’re using it:

1. Start with a high-performing customer
2. Pull the key contact (ex: “Head of Revenue Ops at a 200-person fintech”)
3. Use Ocean to find 50+ people with similar titles at similar companies
4. Run targeted outbound that speaks to their shared KPIs and pain points

This turns one great persona into a repeatable outbound segment in under 10 minutes.

It’s a fast way to scale without sacrificing relevance.

We’ve already worked this into our outbound systems. 

If you build outbound lists , this is worth testing, kudos to the Ocean.io team!

### 82. 7322981679642386435

- URL: https://www.linkedin.com/posts/patrickspychalski_this-conference-follow-up-hack-using-clay-activity-7322981679642386435-6Kz9
- Author: Patrick Spychalski (@patrickspychalski)
- Posted: 2025-04-29T13:54:46.847Z

#### Verbatim Content

This conference follow-up hack using Clay is one every GTM team should steal.
Enterprise companies love events — but most of them completely drop the ball when it comes to pre- and post-event outreach.

They either:

Blast every attendee with the same generic email

Or wait until after the event to scramble something together

Here’s a better play — and you can build it entirely in Clay:

1. Pull the full attendee list (usually from event software or sponsor access). 

2. Enrich each contact in Clay with past engagement:

Are they a current lead in your CRM. Are they a connection to anybody on the team?

Have they liked or commented on LinkedIn posts from me or my team?

Have they posted anything recently related to what we do?

4. Use those signals to identify who actually knows your brand — and who’s most likely to take a meeting

Now you’re not just emailing a list. You’re reaching out to people who already have context, familiarity, and higher intent. It’s more relevant, more efficient, and way more likely to convert.

Try this the next time you are running an event and want to book high quality meetings.

### 84. 7318640646985056279

- URL: https://www.linkedin.com/posts/patrickspychalski_heres-how-to-turn-website-traffic-into-leads-activity-7318640646985056279-kXRd
- Author: Patrick Spychalski (@patrickspychalski)
- Posted: 2025-04-17T14:25:04.009Z

#### Verbatim Content

Here’s how to turn website traffic into leads — regardless of which deanonymization platform you use.

Everyone loves the idea of uncovering who’s visiting their site…

But most teams struggle with what to actually do with that data.

You get insights like:

- Who’s visiting
- What pages they’re viewing
- How long they’re sticking around
- But raw intent data is only valuable if you can act on it.

So I broke down how we take this type of data and turn it into high-converting outbound — in a repeatable, scalable way.

Here’s the play:

1. Push the data into your workspace
 Start with the essentials — company, contact name, title, LinkedIn URL.

2. Filter for ICP + decision-makers
Run logic to confirm:
Is this a B2B company?
Is the contact a decision-maker?

3. Enrich missing fields
If the contact email is missing, use waterfall enrichment to fill the gaps with verified data.

4. Personalize the outreach
Scrape recent LinkedIn posts
Check for relevant keywords (e.g. AI, growth, etc.)
Generate a custom first line referencing their content

5. Add buying signals
Layer in signals like:
New role
Actively hiring
Recent funding

6. Run a tailored AI prompt
Use AI to read their company description and generate 2–3 use cases specific to their business.

7. Add a personal touch at scale
Generate a custom image with their profile picture and a personalized message — all automated.

Once it’s set up, this becomes a self-running, personalized outbound engine.

Check out the full video workflow below ⬇️

### 85. 7317589856321171456

- URL: https://www.linkedin.com/posts/patrickspychalski_this-clay-table-can-judge-your-prospects-activity-7317589856321171456-2pzs
- Author: Patrick Spychalski (@patrickspychalski)
- Posted: 2025-04-14T16:49:35.992Z

#### Verbatim Content

This Clay table can judge your prospects' design choices, so you don't have to.

Here's how it works:
- You import a list of prospects
- Clay enriches the company to find their LinkedIn URL and logo
- A Wordware (YC S24) API call allows us to determine (with AI) whether they have a good logo/banner
- Based on which one Wordware says is bad, we write a custom first line to target the prospect (nicely) suggesting to improve it 

The Kiln's very own Elias Stråvik built this for a series that we're running, where we make super creative Clay tables for cool companies- in this edition, he built one for Canva 🎨

I thought this table was so cool- hopefully you all do as well!

### 86. 7320116735179689985

- URL: https://www.linkedin.com/posts/patrickspychalski_clay-just-launched-a-ton-of-signals-you-activity-7320116735179689985-Y2e7
- Author: Patrick Spychalski (@patrickspychalski)
