### 163. 7245424205238870016

- URL: https://www.linkedin.com/posts/patrickspychalski_5-best-intent-data-tools-on-the-market-activity-7245424205238870016-fasC
- Author: Patrick Spychalski (@patrickspychalski)
- Posted: 2024-09-27T13:29:04.001Z

#### Verbatim Content

The 5 Best Intent Data Tools on the Market:

1. Common Room 🏠
Common Room allows you to aggregate essentially every intent signal you could possibly want, all in one place. It's great for teams that are using several at once as a hub for high-intent leads 

2. RB2B 🔬
You can see your website visitors now. No further explanation needed

3. Trigify.io
Trigify allows you to constantly search (and export) for 30+ different intent signals to act upon in an outbound campaign. Great for connecting to tools like Clay to further enrich the leads and run an automated outbound campaign

4. Clay
Clay finds weaker intent signals than the other tools on this list, but it allows you to waterfall these triggers on what would otherwise be a cold email list. 

5. PhantomBuster
Phantombuster is great scraping tool for social data that can then be used as a list build. Their phantoms are really good- browse the whole library to get some creative ideas. 

Let me know which of these is your favorite and/or if I missed any ⬇️

### 164. 7260322668325875712

- URL: https://www.linkedin.com/posts/patrickspychalski_we-built-a-system-that-enriched-a-clients-activity-7260322668325875712-UyvT
- Author: Patrick Spychalski (@patrickspychalski)
- Posted: 2024-11-07T16:10:14.401Z

#### Verbatim Content

We built a system that enriched a client's Salesforce instance in 1 hour. Here's how we did it:

Our client (a well-known company with strict name usage restrictions 😔) had an insanely unorganized Salesforce. 

Some of their problems included:
- Data from years ago that hadn't been updated
- Blank space where super important data (job titles, company names, etc) were completely missing
- Unorganized data that hadn't been put in the right field
- Data that hadn't been normalized (for example, the company's location was just one field instead of city, state, etc.)

Luckily, you can build a Clay table to do this entire thing. Here was the process:

1. Connect the client's Salesforce account to Clay (if you're an mid market or enterprise company, we recommend you create a separate OAuth to add the correct restrictions
2. Put every SFDC record you want updated into a list view
3. Create a table in Clay that imports the list view (they have an SDFC integration for this)
4. Build out the enrichment, normalization, and blank-space filling flow in Clay
^ this will always be different depending on what you're building, but in every case it will be a combination of enrichments, formulas, and conditional runs- quite a web!
5. Sync the data back to Salesforce using Clay's native integration

The best part is, the table updates every day with new records. Now your Salesforce can look good ~forever~

P.S - the video below is not the client's build, just a little demo haha

### 165. 7257780407046127617

- URL: https://www.linkedin.com/posts/patrickspychalski_you-should-use-clay-for-inbound-heres-why-activity-7257780407046127617-LZsA
- Author: Patrick Spychalski (@patrickspychalski)
- Posted: 2024-10-31T15:48:12.079Z

#### Verbatim Content

You should use Clay for inbound. Here's why:

While outbound is a great use case within Clay, their inbound use cases are arguably stronger. 

Clay can act as an aggregator for all of your inbound marketing data, which is all automatically routed, enriched, and then sent to:
- Your sales team to act upon the new leads
- Your CRM with newly enriched Clay data
- An automated sequencing tool like Smartlead, HeyReach, or La Growth Machine. 

A major problem within essentially any mid-market/enterprise business running a mature marketing/sales system is the ability to verify, standardize, and route data easily. Clay can do all of that. 

I made a quick video outlining this idea- we've been doing this for clients at The Kiln and the value add has been pretty massive 🚀

### 166. 7259959560021393408

- URL: https://www.linkedin.com/posts/patrickspychalski_i-still-get-asked-every-day-what-clay-can-activity-7259959560021393408-k697
- Author: Patrick Spychalski (@patrickspychalski)
- Posted: 2024-11-06T16:07:22.635Z

#### Verbatim Content

I still get asked every day what Clay can be used for. Here's a breakdown:

The tough part about defining Clay's possibilities lies in the fact that it's ridiculously versatile. However, there are 4 main categories:

1. Outbound/ABM
- Source leads from essentially anywhere using their native sources, webhook connections, or chrome extension that can scrape websites
- Enrich outbound leads for any data point possible. Like, if it can be found in a database or anywhere online, it can be found with Clay. 
- Personalize outbound better than any other tool, partly due to the fact that you have better data from the previous point

2. Inbound
- Connect all of your inbound data into Clay to use as a source of truth
- Enrich inbound prospects for more data than what you get from your marketing initiatives
- Automatically qualify inbound and send the qualified prospects to the appropriate database or sequencing tool 

3. RevOps Work
- Detect and purge all outdated CRM data 
- Automatically keep all of your Hubspot and Salesforce data up to date
- Enrich existing CRM contacts for better scoring and overall data accessibility
- Score CRM contacts for better lead routing 

4. General Automation and Enablement
This use case is broad, but Clay has a ton of other usage outside of GTM. Clay's team uses it for programattic SEO blogs, social tracking, and more. 

I hope this provides a better idea of how Clay can be used, and I'm definitely going to have to update this soon as they roll more features out!

### 167. 7251610599141343233

- URL: https://www.linkedin.com/posts/patrickspychalski_claygent-just-got-cheaper-and-better-all-activity-7251610599141343233-CSap
- Author: Patrick Spychalski (@patrickspychalski)
- Posted: 2024-10-14T15:11:35.245Z

#### Verbatim Content

Claygent just got cheaper and better, all at the same time 

Adam Eldefrawy (its genius developer) has been burning the midnight oil and has developed TWO new models: Helium and Argon. 

It joins Claygent Neon as a gaseous trio of AI web scrapers that can find you just about anything you want online, at scale. Here are what each of them do:

Helium: Clay's most cost effective model- can be used for most tasks, especially those being run at high volume. A good balance of speed and accuracy. Costs one credit

Neon: Semi-complex tasks at a reasonable price point. If Helium doesn't work for your given prompt, try Neon and see if results are better. Costs two credits

Argon: Meant for deep research and complex analysis. Good for analyzing large texts, multi-modal info gathering, and anything that takes heavy thought. Costs three credits

Let me know what your experience is with these! I played around with Argon and had great results

### 168. 7249068841433985024

- URL: https://www.linkedin.com/posts/patrickspychalski_what-is-nearbound-and-how-can-you-use-it-activity-7249068841433985024-oZve
- Author: Patrick Spychalski (@patrickspychalski)
- Posted: 2024-10-07T14:51:32.984Z

#### Verbatim Content

What is Nearbound and how can you use it to close more deals? 

From a high level, it's a way of securing leads, prospects, and ideally closed deals from partner companies. It works by sharing CRM data with partner companies in exchange for their CRM data. A nice barter system. 

Clay recently launched a source connection with Crossbeam, the leading nearbound tech on the market, who actually just merged with Reveal, which is how I found out about nearbound in the first place. 

This now means that you can import partner companies' CRM data into Clay, and then run an automated outbound campaign to them at scale. Exciting stuff. 

Full transparency, I haven't tried it yet. However, I plan on it, and wanted to share what I know about the system so far. Hoping I can get back on here after running nearbound campaigns for a few clients with some positive updates and results 🚀

### 171. 7263230918398545920

- URL: https://www.linkedin.com/posts/patrickspychalski_smartlead-just-released-a-game-changing-deliverability-activity-7263230918398545920-YNX0
- Author: Patrick Spychalski (@patrickspychalski)
- Posted: 2024-11-15T16:46:35.251Z

#### Verbatim Content

Smartlead just released a game-changing deliverability feature. Here's how it works:

It's called "copy warmup" and it's used to address the increasingly high policing of email copy and it's effect on what emails get sent to primary inboxes.

The process:
- You write copy and put it into Smartlead
- Smartlead sends the copy (with variables) to their accounts
- The accounts positively reply, as well as take any emails that get put into spam and put them into primary inbox
- Your copy has an increased chance of getting sent to primary inbox now!

Huge shoutout to Vaibhav Namburi and the team for constantly innovating; stuff like this is what keeps cold email alive 🚀

### 172. 7267182873982517248

- URL: https://www.linkedin.com/posts/patrickspychalski_this-automation-creates-lookalikes-for-every-activity-7267182873982517248-j1PJ
- Author: Patrick Spychalski (@patrickspychalski)
- Posted: 2024-11-26T14:30:14.886Z

#### Verbatim Content

This automation creates lookalikes for every closed-won lead, so you can close their competitors too. Here's how it works:

This table isn't anything super advanced, but I wanted to share it because it's something every company can (and should) easily set up in Clay. 

Here's the breakdown:
1. Whenever a lead in your CRM gets marked as closed-won, a Zapier automation is triggered and the lead gets added to Clay. 
2. The lead being added to the table triggers an Ocean.io integration that finds ten lookalikes of the closed prospect. 
3. Then, those 10 lookalikes get added to another table. 
4. From there, the lookalikes are put through a competitor check with Claygent to make sure they're worth actually reaching out to. 
5. Now, you can do whatever you want with the data 😎

Huge shoutout to The Kiln's very own Akansh Gupta for building this flow, it's a banger!

If you want the templates for this, just comment something and I can send it over ⬇️

### 173. 7241828191470764034

- URL: https://www.linkedin.com/posts/patrickspychalski_its-officially-over-for-every-other-salesforce-activity-7241828191470764034-k0P2
- Author: Patrick Spychalski (@patrickspychalski)
- Posted: 2024-09-17T15:19:47.508Z

#### Verbatim Content

It's officially over for every other Salesforce enrichment tool.

You can now use your Clay workflows in Salesforce without ever leaving the comfort of your CRM ☁️

What does this actually mean though?

1. You now have the aggregate power of every enrichment provider out there, combined into one platform, that's completely accessible within Salesforce.

2. You can build out complex workflows that your SDRs never have to touch- they just click a button in SFDC and the workflow runs on its own. No more teaching salespeople complex Clay tables. Just make the Clay tables once (or have The Kiln do it) and adopt them company-wide. 

3. You can send CRM contacts to a complex, multichannel campaign, complete with personalization, with just a few clicks. 

This is such an insanely good move on Clay's end. One of the most difficult aspects of the product was getting teams to adopt workflows once they were built- now the learning curve has significantly lessened.

### 174. 7239260205245476864

- URL: https://www.linkedin.com/posts/patrickspychalski_if-you-dont-think-rb2b-or-clay-are-worth-activity-7239260205245476864-SpKC
- Author: Patrick Spychalski (@patrickspychalski)
- Posted: 2024-09-10T13:15:31.881Z

#### Verbatim Content

If you don't think RB2B or Clay are worth it, watch this video and reconsider 🧐

### 178. 7244339800336396290

- URL: https://www.linkedin.com/posts/patrickspychalski_do-you-refuse-to-hire-agencies-run-by-gingers-activity-7244339800336396290-C9MW
- Author: Patrick Spychalski (@patrickspychalski)
- Posted: 2024-09-24T13:40:01.725Z

#### Verbatim Content

Do you refuse to hire agencies run by gingers? 

Here's how to run a successful top-of-funnel initiative without hiring The Kiln to do it:

Running outbound with new tools like Clay isn't actually that difficult when broken down, so here's how we do it from a high level. 

You need these three things to run a campaign:

1. A Lead Source- For cold B2B, use Clay, Sales Nav, Apollo.io, or Ocean.io. For local businesses, use Clay's Google Maps Integration. For warm leads, use PhantomBuster, Common Room, Trigify.io and RB2B. There's exceptions but for 95% of people these recommendations hold. 

2. An enrichment provider- Clay can likely do all of the enrichment you'd ever need. 

3. A sending tool. I recommend Smartlead for email, HeyReach for LinkedIn, anbd La Growth Machine for multi-channel. 

If you're running email campaigns, you need sender accounts. Use G-suite or Microsoft accounts for this. Don't use private servers to cut corners. Follow deliverability guides online to make sure you do it right. 

Ok, you have all the tech now. Next, you need to write the copy. 

No matter what channel you use, keep it short, to the point, and have a killer offer as the CTA. For LinkedIn, keep it even shorter. Personalize your first line using Clay's enrichments to find relevant data on the company that ties to your value prop. 

Your offer is the most important thing. If you don't have a good offer to provide, personalize your copy more to add relevant value. If your offer is incredible, get to the point, and don't waste your time and Clay credits personalizing. 

Make your sequences no more than 3-4 messages long, reframe the value prop in the second message, improve the offer in the third message, and breakup in the fourth. 

Test the copy on 500-1000 leads. If it doesn't get you a single positive response, iterate until it does. Set a threshold for success- I'd recommend between 100-350 emails per positive response as a range, dependent on TAM size. 

Once you iterate to success, scale your campaigns, then focus on using similar messaging for warmer leads. Run lookalike campaigns based on closed-won customers. Do CRM reengagement, social follower campaigns, scrape slack channels where your customers reside, etc etc. Find creative ways to deliver this messaging to customers at the right time. Your campaign type is the vessel for your offer, and the offer is the vessel for your product. 

That's pretty much it! There's more nuance to everything I assume, but from a high level this is how to win. 

You can now succeed despite your bigoted view towards the redheads of the world 🧑🏼‍🦰 🚫

### 179. 7238907686317142016

How to use RB2B in 5 levels of complexity, from easiest to hardest (part 1)

Adam Robinson and I agreed on our last webinar that the most commonly asked question about RB2B is, what do I actually do after I get my website visitors? 

Well, the short answer is, just do whatever effective outreach method you're using with your cold leads (with some changes based on landing page etc), but if you're looking for some inspiration, here are 5 ways to use RB2B to get more leads.

This video goes through levels 1-3, which from a high level, are:
1. Enriching website visitors and sending them to your CRM
2. Sending website visitors to a basic email campaign
3. Scoring website visitors for ICP fit and sending them to one of three campaigns based on their score, with some enrichments included

I highly recommend watching this if you're pretty new to Clay and looking to see how it interacts with RB2B. If you want to see the crazy stuff though, wait til tomorrow :)

### 180. 7234186835764137985

- URL: https://www.linkedin.com/posts/patrickspychalski_this-new-clay-integration-will-fundamentally-activity-7234186835764137985-rFen
- Author: Patrick Spychalski (@patrickspychalski)
- Posted: 2024-08-27T13:15:46.338Z

#### Verbatim Content

This new Clay integration will fundamentally change the way we score leads. 

The new MadKudu collab with Clay taps an advanced leads scoring tech that allows you to score leads at scale, but in a more effective fashion that increases conversion rates more than how it was traditionally done. 

The old way to score leads (as I'm sure most of you know), was defined by determining the data points you want to score by, then assigning scores to each interval of that data point (for example, company size being 10-20, 20-30, etc and assigning a score for each one). 

The hard part here was weighting the scores- you never knew if your lead scoring mechanisms were actually working because the scores you assigned them were arbitrary. 

The new way is using AI to determine whether a prospect fits ICP by taking a more all-encompassing approach. Madkudu does this by looking at residual data points associated with each lead and creating what is likely a better scoring system than you would have done manually.

Clay's new integration allows this to be applied at scale- however, it's worth noting that using Clay's API key will probably not do the trick. If you're going to use this integration, I highly recommend making a Madkudu account and creating your own ICP instead of the default one Clay uses.

Hope this is helpful! Definitely interested in hearing anybody's thoughts on this if they've tried it.

### 181. 7233828042329014272

- URL: https://www.linkedin.com/posts/patrickspychalski_heres-an-outbound-tech-stack-i-would-recommend-activity-7233828042329014272-crVi
- Author: Patrick Spychalski (@patrickspychalski)
- Posted: 2024-08-26T13:30:03.317Z

#### Verbatim Content

Here's an outbound tech stack I would recommend you consider for 4 different budget sizes:

DISCLAIMER: This is a generalized list. Many of the technologies listed in the higher budget ranges may be necessary even if you don't fall within that range, depending on what systems you're using.

Low Budget (<$300/mo):
- Clay
- Smartlead
- RB2B 

This gets you the basics, which is the ability to run a personalized outbound campaign at scale. 

Clay's lowest plan will give you enough credits to do the majority of enrichments and personalizations required for a basic campaign, and you can download the Clay table and upload it as a CSV to Smartlead. 

Clay also has more than enough with regards to lead sourcing where you likely don't need other databases for a basic use case.

Medium Budget ($300-$1000)
- Clay
- Smartlead
- RB2B
- HeyReach

With a higher budget, you can add two integral things to a matured GTM system, which are intent signals and additional channels. The reason I didn't add a dedicated multichannel tool here is because I still think it's important to prioritize volume when starting out, and keeping your sequencing tools separate allows for this. RB2B is one of strongest intent signals you can ask for, which is people literally visiting your website, and HeyReach allows you to expand your outbound efforts to LinkedIn as well. 

Somewhat large budget: ($1000-$2000)
- Clay
- Smartlead
- La Growth Machine
- RB2B
- Trigify.io
- Apollo.io
- Sendspark

This section just rounds out the benefits of the previous budget size- even more intent signals, even more channels, and an added lead source in Apollo which can help supplement some of the gaps in your TAM mapping. The addition of Sendspark helps send messages that stand out via a different communication medium. 

Unlimited Budget:
- Clay
- Smartlead
- Heyreach 
- La Growth Machine
- RB2B
- Sendspark
- Common Room
- Ocean.io
- Trigify.io
- PhantomBuster
- LeadMagic
- Apify
- Zapier
- Twain
(I'm probably missing a few here)

With this tech stack, you've pretty much unlocked outbound. Like, you can run any campaign that you would reasonably need to achieve success in the long term, given enough knowledge of your product, a good offer, etc. You could automate a presidential campaign with this lol

Hope this is helpful! Let me know which ones I missed 👇🏼

### 184. 7243970999992426496

- URL: https://www.linkedin.com/posts/patrickspychalski_5-enrichment-providers-to-try-in-clay-activity-7243970999992426496-lAsv
- Author: Patrick Spychalski (@patrickspychalski)
- Posted: 2024-09-23T13:14:32.871Z

#### Verbatim Content

Here are 5 Clay Enrichments every beginner should try:

1. Claygent
Developed by the illustrious Adam Eldefrawy, Claygent allows you to scrape the internet for essentially any piece of publicly available information. It's insanely powerful if prompted correctly, and it's getting better with each passing OpenAI model. 

2. Store Leads
If your target audience is in ecommerce, Store Leads is an incredibly robust and in-depth data provider on B2C stores. It provides you with SO much- social links, average product price, web visitors- the list goes on. 

3. LinkedIn
This one's obvious, but if you've never tried Clay before, it will still blow your mind to see all of that data in one place, especially in such an actionable format. 

4. PredictLeads
Shmuel Jacobsen recommended this for the list and I completely agree. They have data like company news, connected companies, tech stack, and other super hard to find data on companies that you probably couldn't even Google search if you wanted to. It's great, give it a try. 

5. Google
I think this one is great to try because it's a tool everyone uses, but Clay takes it to a new level by allowing you to search for things at scale and then action upon the data as well. If you know how to use Google search operators, this can be even more powerful.

Hope this is helpful!

P.S- I didn't include Apify because I use it more as a data source than enrichment provider. But that's for the next slideshow ;)

### 185. 7237485990796308480

- URL: https://www.linkedin.com/posts/patrickspychalski_this-clay-table-turns-a-list-of-leads-into-activity-7237485990796308480-A3lu
- Author: Patrick Spychalski (@patrickspychalski)
- Posted: 2024-09-05T15:45:26.193Z

#### Verbatim Content

This Clay table turns a list of leads into a credit-efficient GTM powerhouse 🚀

Clay's new MadKudu integration now opens up the capability of routing leads to specific channels based on an AI-assisted lead score. 

This means that you can save a ton of credits by only enriching, personalizing, and sequencing your highest priority leads, while still sending the lower-scored ones to a sequence as well. 

Here's the high level of how to use the table:
1. Create your custom lead scoring mechanism in Madkudu by training it on past customers and any other data you deem necessary for it to make an informed ICP decision on a list of companies
2. Import a large list of leads into a Clay table
3. Run the Madkudu integration to automatically score all of your leads using your company-specific AI scoring mechanism
4. Choose the channels you'd like to route your leads to (in my case, I chose SDR enablement in HubSpot, then a Multi-Channel Campaign in La Growth Machine, then a pure email campaign in Smartlead. If I was smarter, I would add HeyReach as well between LGM and SL)
5. Run any enrichments and create any personalizations you'd like to, and use conditional runs to make sure they're only run on prospects whose score fall within a certain range
6. Use conditional runs in Clay to route leads to the proper channel based on their score. In this table, I had all leads with a score of 93 or above go to Hubspot, any leads between 85 and 93 go to LGM, and then any below 85 to go to Smartlead. 

Voilà! Just like that, you've saved thousands of Clay credits and prioritized your leads to create a more efficient GTM team.

If you want the share table link for this buildout, just comment something and I can send it to you 📫

### 186. 7241464566398787587

- URL: https://www.linkedin.com/posts/patrickspychalski_were-running-a-nurture-campaign-for-one-activity-7241464566398787587-OGL8
- Author: Patrick Spychalski (@patrickspychalski)
- Posted: 2024-09-16T15:14:52.535Z

#### Verbatim Content

We're running a nurture campaign for one of our clients that's gotten positive responses from Amazon, Dell, Asus, Disney, and more. Here's how to do it for your company:

1. ACTUALLY make it a nurture campaign

The point of nurture campaigns are to legitimately form a relationship with the prospect on a good basis, not to sell your services to them the second you get on the call. Where most people go wrong is that they forget the objective is more long term, and try to make it a hybrid campaign that ends up being interpreted as a weak sales pitch. 

2. Use personal personalizations

I never recommend using personalizations that don't directly tie into your company's value prop.... unless you're running a nurture campaign. In this case, it's all about showing you did research on the person, not the company. With this campaign, we would look for one of three attributes on a person's LinkedIn profile (recent post, location, title) and make a comment on it. 

3. Keep it short, casual, and make your offer enticing. 

Just because you're not directly selling your product or services doesn't eliminate the need for the offer. In this case though, it has to be a personal offer. Dinner, coffee, virtual value exchange- something that the person will respond well to in an emotional sense, not just a value sense. We're reaching out to executives at Fortune 500 companies - they can afford dinner. They're showing up for the connection. 

Hopefully this is helpful, and if you'd like more content on nurture campaigns just let me know 🤝

### 187. 7231705160853397505

- URL: https://www.linkedin.com/posts/patrickspychalski_there-are-likely-dozens-if-not-hundreds-activity-7231705160853397505-vDSj
- Author: Patrick Spychalski (@patrickspychalski)
- Posted: 2024-08-20T16:54:28.932Z

#### Verbatim Content

There are likely dozens (if not hundreds) of warm leads wasting away in your CRM right now. Here's how to re-engage them:

Most businesses will run marketing initiatives of some kind, collect form submissions, newsletter subscribers, etc, and then give up after the prospect doesn't respond the first time. 

We've been seeing incredible results running re-engage campaigns for our clients' stale CRM leads, and honestly, I think everyone should do it. The steps are pretty simple too-

1. Go to Clay -> create new table -> import Salesforce/Hubspot Contacts

2. Find the list where all of your stale leads are housed, then import them to a Clay table

3. Write re-engage copy to send to your stale leads. We've seen basic, unpersonalized campaigns work (so you Clay beginners don't have an excuse to not run this). Even a 2-step sequence asking if they're still interested in whatever it was they engaged in can work. 

4. Create necessary personalizations in Clay, then use their Smartlead integration to sequence variables to your copy template

5. Watch as responses stream in 🦦

If these campaigns keep bringing these kind of results in, it may be a standard that we run one of these for every client over at The Kiln. Go and give it a try!

### 189. 7227667448601157632

- URL: https://www.linkedin.com/posts/patrickspychalski_we-generated-1m-in-pipeline-first-month-activity-7227667448601157632-bN8q
- Author: Patrick Spychalski (@patrickspychalski)
- Posted: 2024-08-09T13:30:03.314Z

#### Verbatim Content

We generated ~$1M in pipeline first month for a client using Store Leads, Clay and some fun math. Here's how we did it:

As a quick background, our client operated in the e-commerce space and provided site speed optimization services. We targeted Shopify Plus stores because their offer was relatively high ticket. 

We realized before running our first campaign that we had all of the data needed for a classic "do the math" play- we were able to find their web traffic, their average product value, and their site speed all using Clay. 

Our first email (the one that converted really well), went something along the lines of (obviously written way better than how I'm about to say it):

"Hey John -- I noticed that your site speed is failing in essentially all metrics. 

Did you know that your conversion rates will increase by 17% if you improve site speed by 1 second?

Given ExampleCo's ~1,500,000 monthly site visitors, your average product price being $30, and assuming a conversion rate of 2%, this would mean ~$153k of extra revenue per month.

I'll install something for free that improves your speed by 20% in exchange for a call. Any interest?"

Obviously the offer really carried here, but I also think allowing the prospect to get a tailored idea of what ROI would be helped considerably as well. 

Hope this inspires some cool math-based Clay plays!

### 190. 7219697782809374720

### 194. 7219332520452993024

- URL: https://www.linkedin.com/posts/patrickspychalski_your-personalization-only-needs-to-be-as-activity-7219332520452993024-oWR-
- Author: Patrick Spychalski (@patrickspychalski)
- Posted: 2024-07-17T13:30:01.588Z

#### Verbatim Content

Your personalization only needs to be as strong as your offer is weak. 

Bottom line, using tools like Clay when creating relevant personalization costs money, and when running outbound I'm sure you'd prefer to save it.

But frankly, if your offer is good enough, you don't need it. If you're giving away a million dollars, do you really need to reference the technology they're using first?

Obviously not every company can offer $1M in exchange for a meeting, or even anything variably different from their competitors, and this is where personalization comes in. The less enticing and differentiated your offer is, the more valuable your use of personalization can be.

### 195. 7222231655577464832

- URL: https://www.linkedin.com/posts/patrickspychalski_dont-know-where-to-start-for-your-next-outbound-activity-7222231655577464832-REjF
- Author: Patrick Spychalski (@patrickspychalski)
- Posted: 2024-07-25T13:30:09.265Z

#### Verbatim Content

Don't know where to start for your next outbound campaign? 

Here are 7 ideas for high-conversion campaigns you can set up and run in a day:

1. Website visitors campaign 

Connect RB2B to Clay, use Clay's enrichments to find data on the prospects and then use Claygent to qualify them. From there, write copy, use Clay to create personalizations, then sequence it to your favorite sending tool.

2. LinkedIn engagement campaign

Use a tool like PhantomBuster or Apify to scrape LinkedIn followers or post engagers. From there, you can create custom messaging based on the type of engagement they've had with you (ex: Saw you liked my post about ___, figured this might interest you). Ideally you use a tool like HeyReach or La Growth Machine to send these out via LinkedIn for even higher conversion. 

3. Current client/customer lookalikes campaign

Get your favorite customers or clients and import them into a Clay table. Use Ocean.io to find their lookalikes that for your basic ICP attributes. Run a campaign with a personalization that references your current customer's success story for maximum conversion. 

4. Intent signal waterfall campaign

Define 5 intent signals that heavily indicate that a prospect would benefit from your solution or at least be interested in hearing you out. Import a list of leads, and then use Clay to check if the prospects fit at least one of the intent signals. Write a custom first line based on that intent signal, and of course provide a backup in case prospects don't match any. 

5. Personalized video campaign

Create a small list of targeted leads that all fit a specific message well. Use Sendspark to create a dynamic video template that addresses this message, but ensure that it's specific enough to where people will actually think you did your research (the leads all sharing an intent signal is a great common denominator for this, like "Hey! Saw you liked my LinkedIn post"). Connect it to Clay, then send as part of a sequence through a multichannel tool like LGM. 

6. Job openings campaign

Figure out what problem your product or service solves. Then, figure out what position at a company your product or service would either compliment well or just replace entirely. Then, scrape those job listings using Apify, write copy that tailors to the solution you're providing fitting the job listing, then run the campaign.

7. Positive response lookalikes campaign

Get all of the positive responses or interest from past campaigns, find their lookalikes using Ocean.io, and run a campaign on them. This works even better if positive responses generally follow a trend for you, like same industry etc. 

Hope this helps spark some ideas! 

p.s: we can help create these flows for you over at The Kiln as well :)

### 196. 7209917841389559808

- URL: https://www.linkedin.com/posts/patrickspychalski_local-leads-campaigns-have-been-absolutely-activity-7209917841389559808-2Lwv
- Author: Patrick Spychalski (@patrickspychalski)
- Posted: 2024-06-21T13:59:27.183Z

#### Verbatim Content

Local leads campaigns have been absolutely kilning it for us (horrible pun) over at The Kiln. Here's why:

Small business owners  don't get nearly the volume of cold outbound that decision makers at larger companies do. 

The obvious reason for this is that they have less spending power, but of course there are still tons of businesses that target them, so that can't be the full answer. 

The other reason is, it's much harder to scale local leads campaigns if you don't know how to use tools like Clay properly. 

A local HVAC company's owner probably won't have their email on Apollo. They probably don't have a LinkedIn page. You might not even be able to find the owner's name publicly. If you want to find it, you'll likely have to scrape their website or something else that houses updated info.

Luckily (due to Adam Eldefrawy's genius), Claygent, Clay's AI scraper, can do this. The way we find emails is a two part process- first, scrape the internet for any emails that we can find in the company, then use ChatGPT to determine what the best one to reach out to would be (in the case you only want to email them once). 

Here are the prompts for both:

Scraping the web for emails:

"Visit {{website}} and, first, search for a link to the company's Facebook page. If you find the company's facebook page, look on their "About" page and output any email addresses in their "Contact Us" section. If you cannot find the company's facebook page, then find me any emails that are on the website. You can search other pages in the site, not just the link that I gave you. For example, if I gave you Amazon.com/contact, you can search Amazon.com as well. Print out just the emails you find, nothing else. You can print out multiple emails in a comma separated list if you find more than 1. If you can't find any emails on the website, print 'no'. exclude any help or support emails. "

Finding the best one (this one can be changed depending on need):

"this is the company: {{companyName}}

this is the company's website: {{website}} 

I am trying to determine which email address would be best for reaching out to them with a cold sales email. If the input is a list of email addresses, select the one that would be the best for reaching out to this company for a cold sales email. Note that we would prefer to not reach out to customer service or support email addresses. Output the selected email, nothing else. If there is only one email in the list, just output that email. DO NOT output anything except for one email address. This is the input: {{scrapedEmails}}"

Hope this is helpful :)

### 197. 7223701614270816258

- URL: https://www.linkedin.com/posts/patrickspychalski_if-your-icp-contains-small-local-businesses-activity-7223701614270816258-s_Ed
- Author: Patrick Spychalski (@patrickspychalski)
- Posted: 2024-07-29T14:51:14.728Z

#### Verbatim Content

If your ICP contains small local businesses, I cannot recommend Clay enough for your outbound campaigns. 

I was recently on a webinar with Jesse Ouellette and we were talking about the importance of finding niche data when running campaigns, either for impressive personalization or for running outbound other people couldn't.

One of the best examples of this, to me, is finding data about small businesses- HVAC companies, dentist offices, restaurants, you name it. Even information as basic as contact data is so much harder to find than the tech companies that you see on Apollo or LinkedIn. However, if you're able to find it, then you've got a massive advantage over competition. 

Clay is the perfect way to find this data, because it has AI scrapers (courtesy of Adam Eldefrawy) that can find any niche data point about a business as long as it's somewhere online. 

I recently ran a campaign to every vintage store in NYC so I could offload some of my clothing, and it resulted in a fair amount of inventory getting sold. Small businesses just don't get as many emails, and if your offer compliments their business model, I've found owners of said businesses can be far more open-minded.

Give it a try, they're a ton of fun to run!

I used this to create a multi-channel campaign that combines Clay, Sendspark, and La Growth Machine that:
- Automatically qualifies the connection to ensure you actually want to reach out to them
- Normalizes the company name so it doesn't sound templated
- Creates a bulleted list of three ways the prospect can benefit from your product
- References a recent LinkedIn post in a first line
- Creates the personalized video and sends it to the LGM campaign

Warm outbound is the way forward, and this table turns your LinkedIn connections to warm leads.

### 199. 7221869918890196992

- URL: https://www.linkedin.com/posts/patrickspychalski_outbound-is-easily-the-most-popular-clay-activity-7221869918890196992-K9W3
- Author: Patrick Spychalski (@patrickspychalski)
- Posted: 2024-07-24T13:32:44.518Z

#### Verbatim Content

Outbound is easily the most popular Clay use case at the moment, but one of its most effective is CRM enrichment. 

You can import your entire CRM database into Clay and enrich the accounts/contacts for any publicly available data point you can imagine. From there, you can send the leads straight back to your CRM via the "Update Lead" integrations natively available in Clay. This can be completely automated. 

The best part is, you can do this with any CRM, even if it's not a native integration. Just use Clay's HTTP API integration to connect it using the CRM's API docs.

Additionally, if you REALLY need updated data, you can create a table that updates automatically (Clay doesn't have this natively but an hour of a Clay expert's time can get this done for you) for whatever interval of time you'd like (a day, a month, a year). 

To me, even if you're hesitant to use Clay for your outbound sales motion, this is a no-brainer. I've heard countless stories of businesses making an intern manually research and update CRM leads when Clay could do the same thing for $800 and an hour of your time. I highly recommend giving it a try!

For anyone that has done this, would love to hear your experience and how much time/money you think it's saved you. For anybody who doubt's Clay's value, try this use case out and get back to me.

Haven't tried Clay yet? Here is a link to sign up for free: clay.com

### 200. 7217162761967931392

- Author: Patrick Spychalski (@patrickspychalski)
- Posted: 2024-07-11T13:48:10.853Z

#### Verbatim Content

I've been working on a beginner's course for outbound with Maja Voje and I was super interested to see the responses for the survey we put out.

I always assumed messaging was the biggest bottleneck for most people (as I feel it's objectively the hardest part of the process to perfect) but a good half of the survey responders said otherwise. 

I think that the "tools and process" portion of outbound will become an increasing challenge for many as there are so many new pieces of software coming out every year that completely shift the landscape of outbound (Clay, La Growth Machine, Twain, RB2B and Sendspark to name a few). With so many new tools, it just gets ridiculously confusing. Gone are the days where Zoominfo and Outreach are the only two techs required. 

We'd love to hear your thoughts on this topic, so if you'd be so kind as to submit your thoughts we'd appreciate it :) 

Survey link in the comments

### 201. 7216801957502480386

- URL: https://www.linkedin.com/posts/patrickspychalski_heres-how-to-create-lookalikes-of-your-website-activity-7216801957502480386-Nt9o
- Author: Patrick Spychalski (@patrickspychalski)
- Posted: 2024-07-10T13:54:28.365Z

#### Verbatim Content

Here's how to create lookalikes of your website visitors, then automatically send them a personalized video:

Somebody visiting your website is obviously an amazing intent signal, but what if you don't have enough marketing-led traffic to run a scaled campaign?

This fixes that problem by effectively 10x-ing the volume of leads for every qualified website visitor you have. 

In short, the flow does the following:
- Imports website visitors automatically via RB2B
- Ensures the website visitors match our ICP before continuing the flow
- Finds up to 10 lookalike companies that match my ICP criteria
- Finds ICP contacts at those 10 lookalike companies, then finds their emails
- Creates a personalized video via Sendspark with the prospect's website scrolling in the background
- Sends the video to Smartlead for an email campaign.

Voila, you now have warm (via RB2B) and cold (via Ocean.io) outbound campaigns that run on their own! 

I demo'ed this Clay flow on a collab webinar with Adam Robinson of RB2B and Bethany Stachenfeld of Sendspark, who both created tools that allowed this flow to exist. 

If you want the templates for this flow, just comment something and I can send them over.

### 203. 7212463114548584448

- URL: https://www.linkedin.com/posts/patrickspychalski_there-are-two-people-every-sales-org-should-activity-7212463114548584448-lpmj
- Author: Patrick Spychalski (@patrickspychalski)
- Posted: 2024-06-28T14:33:27.593Z

#### Verbatim Content

There are two people every sales org should have:

1. A sales tech wizard
2. A creative outbound artist 

This first person knows every piece of sales tech like the back of their hand- they probably obsess over ColdIQ's AI tools list, they can build super advanced Clay tables, and likely have a dev background. They can write python scripts to scrape sites, can connect APIs to Clay in their sleep. 

This person is absolutely imperative to running good top-of-funnel motions. If you're not using the right tech, you've already lost. Having someone who can put your best ideas into action effectively is just as important as the person who comes up with them. 

The second person knows how to write copy that sells. They have a background in sales, are super familiar with effective go-to-market motions, and love experimenting with new ideas. They sit down for an hour a day and think of the most enticing offer for their campaign, they arduously pore over every word of their copy to make sure it "flows" well, and want nothing more than to pioneer a new way of selling. 

Without someone like this, your copy, offer, and overall strategy will be so bad that tech won't save you. Tools like Clay, Smartlead, La Growth Machine, RB2B, Sendspark, etc are incredible, but no tool can save you if your strategy and copy fall flat. 

So, how do you find these people?

For the technical person:
- Find people with technical backgrounds via hackathons, technical job boards, or just ask around for anyone with some coding experience. Make them take Nathan Lippi's Clay bootcamp, and then have them try every piece of sales tech they can get their hands on. 

For the creative person:
- Find the best social media writer you can, then give them 🦾Eric Nowoslawski's doc on copywriting (or the article I recently published on the same topic), and let them learn to use their persuasion and attention grabbing abilities for outbound instead of social posts. 

(or, talk to The Kiln- our whole team is made of these types, and they're already masters at their craft ;))

Clay just released a Dealroom.co integration. Here's how I used it to customize the first line of a cold email:

The main reason I'm pumped about this one in particular is because of how fresh the data is. You can literally see when they last checked for funding info in the Clay table itself, as is shown in the video. 

In the table, I:
- Used Dealroom to get funding data
- Qualified leads based on the round the business raised as well as when they raised it
- Found decision makers at the company + their emails
- Reformatted funding data to look normal in an email 
- Wrote a custom first line for every lead

This table took around 30 minutes to put together, and could be super useful for qualifying leads or even for mentioning their round (or lack thereof) in messaging! Hope this is helpful and if you'd like the template just let me know.

### 206. 7213896810791137280

- URL: https://www.linkedin.com/posts/patrickspychalski_heres-how-to-find-your-favorite-clients-activity-7213896810791137280-hoQm
- Author: Patrick Spychalski (@patrickspychalski)
- Posted: 2024-07-02T13:30:27.414Z

#### Verbatim Content

Here's how to find your favorite client's twin and sign them:

Clay just came out with an integration with Ocean.io, easily the best company lookalike finder on the planet. 

They've scraped ~250,000,000 websites and keyword indexed all of them (Ocean team, let me know if I'm remembering that correctly), to semantically match them with each other and find the most similar company to whoever you input. 

This means you can feed your favorite clients, people who've positively responded to your campaigns, or lookalikes of your best case studies and pop out a lead list of the companies most similar to them. Even better, you can filter the lookalikes for country, size, technology, etc. 

This is absurdly powerful for obvious reasons, but to demo it I decided to make a fake campaign on behalf of Clay where I reach out to companies that are lookalikes to their current integrations. 

Each lookalike also comes fully enriched, which saves you the extra credits usually required to do so. 

There's a source and integration for the tool, both of which I seriously recommend giving it a try. Check out how I used it below ⬇️

Haven't tried Clay yet? Here is a link to sign up for free: clay.com

### 210. 7209557064090578947

- URL: https://www.linkedin.com/posts/patrickspychalski_intent-signals-are-only-as-good-as-the-data-activity-7209557064090578947-r1nZ
- Author: Patrick Spychalski (@patrickspychalski)
- Posted: 2024-06-20T14:05:51.172Z

#### Verbatim Content

Intent signals are only as good as the data that backs them. 

Ben Wright asked me about what I think the future of intent-based outbound is, and I truly think the only way it can keep pushing forward is if new data can start being uncovered by new tools. 

A great example of this is RB2B - we were previously not able to de-anonymize the personal identities of our website visitors, and now that we can, the utility of intent signals increased as a whole. 

By definition, intent signals are pieces of data that indicate a company might in the market for a particular solution. Without new signals, the envelope can't be pushed much.

However, I see Clay tables using intent signals in new and cool ways every day. With the category increasing in popularity, it will soon be necessary to find creative ways of using them. 

I'm interested to hear some intent signals that have been working for your particular outbound. As generic as it is, someone being hired for a new role has been a solid one across a few of my campaigns. Drop yours below ⬇️

p.s - I'm now aware that dependent and contingent mean the same thing. This is what happens when you try to use two big words in a row 😔

### 217. 7193965367851929600

- URL: https://www.linkedin.com/posts/patrickspychalski_every-b2b-company-should-have-an-rb2b-x-clay-activity-7193965367851929600-NirD
- Author: Patrick Spychalski (@patrickspychalski)
- Posted: 2024-05-08T13:30:01.109Z

#### Verbatim Content

Every B2B company should have an RB2B x Clay campaign. Here's why:

1. The positive response rate will be significantly higher than that of pure cold email. 

A qualified prospect visiting your website is unbelievably high intent. You're finding the right person, at the right time, looking at YOUR product or service. If you have a GTM motion at all, it just makes sense to reach out to them. Intent products like 6Sense are cool, but nothing comes close to a website visitor. People using RB2B have seen 5% positive response rates (sometimes more) for their inbound-led outbound campaigns. Pretty nuts. 

2. You can ACTUALLY personalize your messaging. 

I constantly get the question on webinars, "do you think that personalized emails will eventually become saturated?" and my answer is always "no, only bad personalization will". Referencing a prospect's college in an opening line will get overplayed, if it isn't already. Clay can find so many different data points on a company that the personalizations in your copy actually provide value and context. Add relevant personalization to already qualified leads, and you've got a winning campaign. 

3. It's super inexpensive for the value it delivers.

An RB2B plan is free if you just want the Slack channel, which in itself is all you need to run a campaign. Adam Robinson (the founder) himself has released extensive write-ups on how to use the product for free. If you want an easy connection, their paid plan is $500/mo. A Clay account that enables webhooks and email integrations is $350/mo, making your total investment for a high-intent email campaign to be $850/mo. No matter what your product costs, that's likely worth it with any amount of meaningful web traffic.

These two products used in tangent with each other define the future of outbound to me- using incredible data to help prospects before they even respond to your first message. 

P.S - We do RB2B x Clay setups over at The Kiln. If you ever want one set up for your business, just message me! Even if you decide to just set them up yourself, I can't recommend both tools enough 🫡

### 221. 7199038802529533952

- URL: https://www.linkedin.com/posts/patrickspychalski_ive-made-thousands-of-clay-tables-and-this-activity-7199038802529533952-6gs_
- Author: Patrick Spychalski (@patrickspychalski)
- Posted: 2024-05-22T13:30:02.196Z

#### Verbatim Content

I've made thousands of Clay tables and this might be my favorite one. 

I just walked through it live on a Clay x RB2B webinar with Adam Robinson, and the hundreds of attendees were as hype as I was. 

Here's what it does:
- Imports website visitors automatically into the table
- Enriches site visitors for person and company info
- Qualifies prospect automatically
- Has an intent signal waterfall that checks for three different signals and writes a first line based on the one it finds
- Suggests ways the prospect can use Clay in a custom snippet
- Creates a custom image with the prospect's LinkedIn picture in it 
- Writes a custom line based on recent news on the company
- Writes a custom line based on the prospect's tenure at the company
- Sends a sequence of LinkedIn voice messages, LinkedIn DMs, and Emails to the prospect in a multi-step flow

This is all done COMPLETELY automatically. You never have to touch the table again. Plus, it combines SO many high-response methods in one- inbound warm leads, hyper-personalization, and several message channels.

This is part one of the walkthrough - I'll post part 2 tomorrow. 

If you want the template for this table, just comment something and I'll send it over. It's a game changer, seriously.

### 224. 7195777317736980480

- URL: https://www.linkedin.com/posts/patrickspychalski_heres-our-method-at-the-kiln-for-finding-activity-7195777317736980480-vZCH
- Author: Patrick Spychalski (@patrickspychalski)
- Posted: 2024-05-13T13:30:03.626Z

#### Verbatim Content

Here's our method at The Kiln for finding our clients' entire TAMs:

When prospects/clients ask me how I'm going to find their entire target audience, it's a more complicated answer than just telling them what database I use. 

Most databases are incomplete and may not provide all of the information required to import the prospects into Smartlead and start cooking. 

Instead, we do the following:

1. Identify the different sources that will best yield a certain ICP. Here are a few favorites:
- Store Leads when prospecting Ecommerce companies
- Apollo.io, Linkedin Sales Navigator Premium, Ocean.io (thanks for the rec 🦾Eric Nowoslawski), and Crunchbase for Tech/Finance/Media 
- Clay's Google Maps scraper for local leads
- Clay Chrome extension for niche business lists or any database that doesn't have an export option
Sometimes clients already know where their TAM is housed- in that case, our job is just reliably scraping all of it. 

2. Scrape all of the databases that house a client's ICP, then import the lists into Clay
- It's important to understand how to map columns from two different CSVs in Clay. Otherwise, combining them will be an absolute mess. I highly recommend practicing with two data sets you don't care about first. 

3. Use Clay's enrichments to fill the data gaps between different databases
- For example, Apollo finds emails for prospects when you export them. Clay's LinkedIn import doesn't. This means we'll need to run an email integration and combine the two columns to make a dedicated email column 

4. Use Clay enrichments to qualify prospects by metrics not found in ANY of the databases
- Some data points cannot be found in conventional databases. Example- if a client only wants companies that have posted on LinkedIn about AI in the past week, I'm not going to be able to filter by that in Apollo. Instead, I do this in Clay and filter out the list to ensure that only the fitting prospects are found

5. Clean up the list of companies and find people using the company table
- Depending on the database we use, we may need to find people working for the client's target companies. In that case, we import the companies into Apollo to find people that way, then use Clay's "Find People" integration to find people that way, then combine the two lists. If people can't be found with that method (this happens with local leads a lot), then we use Claygent or a creative Google Search integration. 

Hope this was helpful! Am I missing any data providers here that I should check out?

### 227. 7196133574280892417

Formulas are Clay’s secret (and free) superpower.

Before using credits on any of the AI integrations, try to see if a formula can do it first. You may be surprised at their versatility.

Some things formulas can do:
- Editing text
- Conditional formatting
- Exclusion based on certain criteria
- Combining and cleaning data
and more 🚀

While they definitely can’t perform high level tasks like GPT-4, they’re still pretty powerful. Plus, they don’t cost a cent.

Give them a try! Also, if you have any favorite uses of formulas in Clay, drop them in the comments.

### 228. 7193240597711704064

- URL: https://www.linkedin.com/posts/patrickspychalski_the-reality-is-one-person-with-clay-smartlead-activity-7193240597711704064-WK_K
- Author: Patrick Spychalski (@patrickspychalski)
- Posted: 2024-05-06T13:30:02.442Z

#### Verbatim Content

The reality is, one person with Clay, Smartlead and Apollo.io is more efficient than 10 SDRs without.

With those three tools, you can import 10,000+ leads into a table and have them enriched, qualified, and personalized for a campaign in 10 minutes.

Think about how long it would take for an SDR to do that manually. Weeks? Months?

On top of that, hiring a Clay agency is less expensive than hiring 10 SDRs, without the commitment of a full time employee, and with the collective knowledge of all our clients’ campaigns being applied to yours.

Here are some common rebuttals that I hear from people when I make this claim:

1. AI can’t write emails as well as my SDR can.

You’re right, it can’t. However, a person using AI is definitely able to. Any emails that your SDR send have a pattern in the structure, wording, and cadence, even if they don’t notice them. By identifying the openers, value props, problem statements, and offers that work in your SDR’s manual emails, you can easily templatize and replicate their email to the point where the difference is hardly noticeable.

2. My SDR is able to do better research on prospects, which leads to better copy.

This is a common misconception that people who don’t use Clay have, but I would argue that Clay can actually do it better. On top of its hundreds of native integrations, you can use AI web scrapers like Claygent to literally find any publicly available data point on a person or company, at scale. If prompted correctly, you’re training an aggregation of all human knowledge to do research for you. On top of that, it connects to non-native data sources using the HTTP API integration, allowing you even more access to data. Research isn’t a problem.

3. A full-time SDR knows the product better, which leads to better copy and higher conversion.

This is absolutely correct- I’m not at all advocating for the end of the SDR. In fact, I think the day to day of the SDR is evolving to something far more exciting, now that their entire top of funnel can be automated. At The Kiln, our engagements involve a lot of hands-on work with in-house sales teams to figure out the best way to automate their workflows. Ultimately, they know their product best. We’re essentially scaling their best ideas while testing some of our own.

I’m bullish to the point where I think most companies’ entire top of funnel can be automated at this point, especially in the early stages. Clay accelerates testing, iteration, research, email writing, and ultimately, chances of success.

Super interested to hear any thoughts on my long and wordy take on the future of sales ⚡️

### 229. 7227321098420326402

- URL: https://www.linkedin.com/posts/patrickspychalski_heres-a-general-rule-to-follow-when-building-activity-7227321098420326402-Q05I
- Author: Patrick Spychalski (@patrickspychalski)
- Posted: 2024-08-08T14:33:46.996Z

#### Verbatim Content

Here's a general rule to follow when building campaigns around intent signals:

The weaker the intent signal, the more contacts you're going to be able to find, and the more emails you'll be able to send.

A great example: RB2B is an incredible intent signal as it shows that someone is directly interested in your products or services. However, the amount of website visitors that fill a form will obviously be lower, but thats a REALLY good intent signal. 

Conversely, the amount of people that were recently hired in a role that you're targeting (for the past 6 months, let's say) will be way higher than the amount of ICP website visitors you have. 

Why is this important? Well, you have to run a cost-benefit analysis on whether tools like Clay are worth it for specific use cases. If you have 2 ICP website visitors a week for a $10/mo product, it's not going to be worth paying $1k/mo for the tech stack required to automate an RB2B flow. You might be better off moving further up the funnel and trying a campaign there. 

The benefit of marketing-led outbound is that the volume of high-level intent signals increases significantly when funneling leads into a sales motion. Hubspot form fillers, website visitors, social media engagers- the list of possible campaign lead sources goes on and on.

What are some of your favorite intent-based campaigns to run?

### 230. 7206664558847930368

- URL: https://www.linkedin.com/posts/patrickspychalski_clay-just-integrated-with-a-tool-that-completely-activity-7206664558847930368-yL1L
- Author: Patrick Spychalski (@patrickspychalski)
- Posted: 2024-06-12T14:32:04.182Z

#### Verbatim Content

Clay just integrated with a tool that completely writes sales emails for you. Here's why I think 90% of people should actually use it:

The new Twain integration promises to write sales emails completely for you using AI. If you're like me, you're probably skeptical on whether it actually works. Here's my analysis:

The good: The output is actually pretty solid. It follows best practices, does a good job of using your input to write emails, and I don't feel like I'm reading an AI-generated output. Frankly, it's better than most of the old email copy I see from clients when they first start working with us. 

The "bad": It's still not as good as copy from the best writers I know. People like Katie Thies, 🦾Eric Nowoslawski, and the copy we write at The Kiln (ofc I'm biased on the last one) write emails that I would be significantly more comfortable sending at scale. 

"Ok Patrick, but if it's not as good at writing as your favorite writers, why should 90% of people still use it?"

1. Most people don't have access to a world-class cold email copywriter, and most of the good ones are either expensive or completely booked. I would venture to say Twain is better than your average copywriter, which leaves it to be the next best option. 

2. It's still pretty dang good, and it's only going to get better. If you actually give it a lot of good inputs, it's generating pretty great stuff. Think of it as you would an actual copywriter- if you don't give them enough info, it's really hard to write good copy. 

3. Your turnaround time for trying new campaigns just cut in half. Have a new angle you want to try? Write some copy using Twain, sequence the emails in Clay, and test faster. 

Super interested to hear others' thoughts on this! I first heard about Twain from Michel Lieben 🧠 and was skeptical, but after trying it, I'm pretty bullish.

### 232. 7189632972067651584

- URL: https://www.linkedin.com/posts/patrickspychalski_the-webinar-i-hosted-with-michel-lieben-yesterday-activity-7189632972067651584-b48w
- Author: Patrick Spychalski (@patrickspychalski)
- Posted: 2024-04-26T14:34:37.462Z

#### Verbatim Content

The webinar I hosted with Michel Lieben yesterday was one of the most value-packed ones I've ever done.

I think a lot of people get introduced to tools like Clay and are overwhelmed by the amount of technologies available to connect to it.

I've always said that Clay NOT a competitor to sales tools, but a compliment. Here are a few tools that we think compliment it well:

1. La Growth Machine - an emerging tech, but incredible for campaigns with multiple touchpoints across channels. Think email/LinkedIn/Twitter campaigns that all flow cohesively. 

2. Common Room - The GTM data you can find with Common Room is pretty unreal. They aggregate the engagements that you're had with people across several different platforms, as well as give you incredible insight on LinkedIn in general. Using this in conjunction with outbound prospecting tools is a gamechanger. 

3. RB2B - This tool has been taking LinkedIn by storm, and for good reason. It captures your website visitor's info such as name, company, title, and LinkedIn profile. When combined with Clay for custom outreach, you can run an inbound-led outbound motion that converts well. 

4. Twain - This tool helps you write better sales copy using AI. It's really cool to see AI being used in such a niche way- I can only assume that it's going to get better and better as the model is trained. 

5. HeyReach - Top of the market for LinkedIn prospecting. There are so many tools out there that claim to be able to do it, and I always warn against using any that haven't been extensively tested by the pros. Michel is a pro, so this is all I needed to hear. 

If you haven't tried any of these tools, give them a whirl! Let me know of any sleepers that I didn't mention.

$0-$10k Plant Store Outbound Challenge - Episode 1

I've decided to take a bit of a risk over the next few weeks. 

The plan:
Document every moment of my process in getting Puneet Sabharwal's amazing plant store Horti some new clients for it's corporate gifting offering. 

The background:
Horti is the best store in the country for buying plants online. The New York Times just posted an article around a week ago saying so. They have an offer for companies to buy plants for their shareholders as part of a corporate gifting program. 

In exchange for letting me sell some clothing in Horti's Brooklyn retail location, Puneet asked The Kiln to run email campaigns to get Horti more corporate gifting clients. 

In this episode, I go over the first set of copy I wrote for Horti's offering, as well as the Clay table to go along with it. Now, it's time to run the campaign and see how it performs. If it does well, we scale, and if it doesn't, we change the targeting and copy. 

I haven't seen many people live-posting their campaigns, likely because if they fail, it looks bad. I get it, but also that's not very fun or educational. Even if this doesn't work, I think there's still value in it.

Also, I mention this in the video, but I'd love to have y'all take part in this too. Feel free to drop any fun campaign ideas in the comments- maybe we can have some collab episodes 🤝

### 235. 7188553244346863616

- URL: https://www.linkedin.com/posts/patrickspychalski_heres-how-to-save-10-hours-a-week-in-unqualified-activity-7188553244346863616-kZV2
- Author: Patrick Spychalski (@patrickspychalski)
- Posted: 2024-04-23T15:04:10.313Z

#### Verbatim Content

Here's how to save 10+ hours a week in unqualified sales calls:

1. Edit your calendar form to make the scheduler include their LinkedIn profile link. 
2. Create a calendar webhook that imports all booked call data into Clay
3. Use the person's LinkedIn profile link to enrich them via Clay's "Enrich Person" integration. This will find their company name and job title.
4. Define your qualification criteria. Some examples are company revenue, the person's position in the company, or even why they booked the call. 
5. Use Clay's other integrations to find the qualification data. Examples are "Find Revenue", "Find Tech Stack", "Find Mentions in News"- the list goes on. 
6. Then, use a formula to check a box based on whether the data matches your parameters. For example, "check the box if revenue is over $10m".
7. If the person does not match your criteria, use Clay's HTTP API integration and a Zapier automation to automatically cancel the meeting and send a custom message. 
8. Enjoy the extra time :)

Will drop the template for this later this week!

### 238. 7186000435500003328

- URL: https://www.linkedin.com/posts/patrickspychalski_we-used-clay-to-reach-out-to-15000-icp-companies-activity-7186000435500003328-BpdP
- Author: Patrick Spychalski (@patrickspychalski)
- Posted: 2024-04-16T14:00:13.255Z

#### Verbatim Content

We used Clay to reach out to 15,000 ICP companies without spending a single credit. 

The background:
A client of ours wanted to reach out to local leads from a list of 100 different locations. 

The problem- their product was super low-ticket. This meant spending as few Clay credits as possible per lead.

Our solution:
Use Claygent (with their OpenAI API key) to either find the company's Facebook account or scrape their website. Then, Claygent would find their email. 

Then, we used a Reoon API key to validate the emails. This isn't native in Clay yet, so we used their HTTP API integration to connect it. 

The client knew their offer was good and opted out of personalization, so from there, we categorized the leads into 5 different Smartlead campaigns based on industry.

In the video below, The Kiln's Head of Ops Mathias Powell walks through the campaign specifics and how he created the tables.

Haven't tried Clay yet? Here is a link to sign up for free: clay.com

### 240. 7181706566344024064

- URL: https://www.linkedin.com/posts/patrickspychalski_heres-the-secret-to-running-great-trigger-activity-7181706566344024064-ipt1
- Author: Patrick Spychalski (@patrickspychalski)
- Posted: 2024-04-04T17:37:55.072Z

#### Verbatim Content

Here's the secret to running great trigger campaigns without narrowing down your audience too much: 

We call them "trigger waterfalls" at The Kiln, and they're hyper-effective in getting better coverage in your ICP while still sending custom first lines. 

The problem with conventional trigger campaigns: 

Let's say that in your new campaign, you want to congratulate the company on their latest financial reports. You import 10k people into Clay, run the integrations to scrape their recent financials, and realize only 20% of the companies have financials that are worth congratulating. 

Now, you've spent 10k credits and only 2k companies you can actually reach out to. 

The solution:

Trigger waterfalls incorporate several different triggers into a campaign. Instead of just congratulating on financials, you also congratulate on a recent round, headcount growth, or a recent product announcement. Now, you can contact 8k of those 10k companies, 4x-ing your responses just from a few added integrations. 

In Clay, we prioritize our triggers, then use integrations to locate the data to qualify them. If a company qualifies for a trigger, we don't run any more integrations, saving tons of credits. 

This is a bit of a meta Clay concept so if you'd like a longer video on this just let me know. 

However, hopefully this was helpful enough to get started- trigger waterfalls are a great way to hyper-target leads without narrowing down your ICP 🌊

### 245. 7184182430319161344

- URL: https://www.linkedin.com/posts/patrickspychalski_easily-one-of-the-most-frequently-asked-questions-activity-7184182430319161344-h8Tj
- Author: Patrick Spychalski (@patrickspychalski)
- Posted: 2024-04-11T13:36:07.043Z

#### Verbatim Content

Easily one of the most frequently asked questions I get:

"How do I learn Clay well enough to implement it within my company?"

I decided to answer that question here. The following is a list (as of April 2024) of every resource I would recommend to get learning Clay yourself:

1. Clay University
The Clay team understands that the tool has a learning curve, and are constantly improving it to make it easier to use. While they're at it, they also have a great learning resource that allows you to learn the basic building blocks. It's also completely free, and I highly recommend using it. Shoutout Yash Tekriwal 🤔, Matthew Quan and the rest of the team on creating such a great guide. It's easily accessible via Clay's website

2. Clay's YouTube Channel 
I've made 100+ videos going through how to use Clay on their YouTube channel. Some of them are less serious (ex: having Clay train me to run an Ultramarathon) but I also have videos outlining how to create an entire email campaign from scratch, full copy breakdowns, and more. We've been getting great feedback there. 

3. 🦾Eric Nowoslawski's LinkedIn
The man is an absolute wizard with cold outbound, so you're not just going to be getting great Clay content, but also content that will make your company's GTM better as a whole. Easily one of the best follows on LinkedIn- he gives away more free value than some charities. I reference his stuff constantly.

4. Clay Webinars
I've been hosting webinars with Clay every week, partnering with other Clay experts as well as other GTM SaaS companies to give live demos of the coolest new things in prospecting. We've had experts demo things that blow me away, and I've been using Clay every day for more than a year. You can find the events under the Live Events tab on Clay's website.

5. Office Hours
Clay hosts office hours twice a week, with their customer team helping with onboarding and general Q&A's. Having such easy and free access to the Clay team is absolutely elite and I don't see many other brands doing it that well. 

I figured I would also include some non-free ways that you can learn Clay for your specific use case, or have someone helping you learn as you go:

1. Clay Bootcamp
Nathan Lippi has made a great course that allows you to learn Clay A to Z, walking through everything with experts helping you along the way. He's great with Clay and his course reflects his skill. 

2. Clay Consulting
My agency doesn't offer consulting at the moment, but there are plenty of experts that do. This can be great for getting a particular use case set up if you want someone to help you build it yourself. 

Lastly, if you just want someone to do Clay for you, I'll shamelessly plug The Kiln. We're a done for your Clay and outbound service that uses our expertise on Clay to automate sales flows and improve your outbound effectiveness. Feel free to book a call if you want to supercharge your current outbound.

Hope this is helpful!

### 249. 7182075902518775808

- URL: https://www.linkedin.com/posts/patrickspychalski_if-youre-not-using-api-keys-in-clay-youre-activity-7182075902518775808-Xsmc
- Author: Patrick Spychalski (@patrickspychalski)
- Posted: 2024-04-05T18:05:31.678Z

#### Verbatim Content

If you're not using API keys in Clay, you're doing it wrong. 

Adding API keys to your integrations instead of using credits can literally cut your costs in half, if not more.

Here are some API keys I always recommend adding:

1. Neverbounce
NB's API credits cost a fraction of a cent each, which is far, far less than what a Clay credit would cost. If you're running outbound campaigns, it's a no-brainer. 

2. OpenAI
AI is one of the most powerful use cases in Clay, and OpenAI integrations are the go-to. Get an API key to save $. Also worth considering Claude or Cohere depending on what you're trying to achieve. 

3. Apollo
Their rate limits suck, but the data is really solid. You can run some great enrichments using Apollo, and you're actually required to have any API key to run them. 

4. DeepL
If you're translating outbound campaigns to another language, DeepL is an amazing translator compared to others on the market. Their API key is also waaaay cheaper than Clay credits.

5. Storeleads
This is a must-have for any ecom-related Clay table. Storeleads is by far the best data source for online b2c shops, and getting an API key is recommended if you work heavily in ecom. 

Hope that this was valuable, did I miss any?
