### 252. 7173695134994182144

- URL: https://www.linkedin.com/posts/patrickspychalski_a-necessity-in-any-email-campaign-you-run-activity-7173695134994182144-xqgg
- Author: Patrick Spychalski (@patrickspychalski)
- Posted: 2024-03-13T15:03:20.993Z

#### Verbatim Content

A necessity in any email campaign you run is ensuring that company names and job titles don't sound regurgitated from the internet 🤖

Saying something like "Hey, saw you're Director of Revenue Operations - Boulder Northeast Division at Microsoft, LLC" is an easy way to immediately turn off a prospect. 

Clay has a "Normalize Job Title" integration, but it can miss less obvious things and doesn't work for non-english company names, either. 

Here are the AI prompts we use for normalizing job titles and company names:

Company Names (via 🦾Eric Nowoslawski):
Prompt:
The input is the name of a company. Output a normalized version. Your output should reflect how somebody would reference the company in conversation or in an email. Here is the input: {{company name}}
Example 1:
Input:
The input is the raw name of a company. Output a normalized version of the input. Your output should reflect how somebody would reference the company in conversation or in an email. Here is the input: The Kiln, Inc.
Output:
The Kiln
Example 2:
Input:
The input is the raw name of a company. Output a normalized version of the input. Your output should reflect how somebody would reference the company in conversation or in an email. Here is the input: GROWTH ENGINE X, UK
Output: Random Growth Engine X


Job Titles (via The Kiln):
Prompt:
Using the input, a raw job title, output a cleaned and normalized version that would sound smooth and natural in conversation between two people. If possible and appropriate, shorten the input and use acronyms. Make the job title more generic, less specific if possible. If the raw job title already sounds normal and natural, don't change it. Here is the input: {{raw job title}}
Example 1:
Input:
Using the input, a raw job title, output a cleaned and normalized version that would sound smooth and natural in conversation between two people. If possible and appropriate, shorten the input and use acronyms. Make the job title more generic, less specific if possible. If the raw job title already sounds normal and natural, don't change it. Here is the input: Chief Executive Officer
Output:
CEO
Example 2:
Input:
Using the input, a raw job title, output a cleaned and normalized version that would sound smooth and natural in conversation between two people. If possible and appropriate, shorten the input and use acronyms. Make the job title more generic, less specific if possible. If the raw job title already sounds normal and natural, don't change it. Here is the input: Data Analyst II (remote)
Output:
Data Analyst

Hope this is helpful!

### 253. 7171512429535813633

- URL: https://www.linkedin.com/posts/patrickspychalski_this-campaign-got-positive-responses-from-activity-7171512429535813633-Uye1
- Author: Patrick Spychalski (@patrickspychalski)
- Posted: 2024-03-07T14:30:03.459Z

#### Verbatim Content

This campaign got positive responses from Ralph Lauren and The North Face, and we only reached out to 173 leads 🚀

This is what we call a "micro campaign" at The Kiln, and it's a way we use Clay to differentiate our copy from the sea of outbound using two things:
- Hyper relevant and personalized messaging
- Trigger based targeting

In this case, we were reaching out to fashion brands with a new drop coming up. I'll admit, my background in fashion made this easier to set up as I knew what the results were supposed to look like. 

Running these takes a little more work, but can yield results if broader targeting isn't working as well. 

Hope this spurs some ideas for your own campaigns!

### 254. 7188167208512933890

- URL: https://www.linkedin.com/posts/patrickspychalski_if-you-want-to-learn-clay-poking-around-activity-7188167208512933890-UsHG
- Author: Patrick Spychalski (@patrickspychalski)
- Posted: 2024-04-22T13:30:12.198Z

#### Verbatim Content

If you want to learn Clay, poking around the tool isn't the fastest way to do it. 

You'll probably just get overwhelmed. Even if you do make some progress, there are features that would 10x your workflow's efficiency if you knew about them. 

The best way to become a master is to learn the fundamentals of each feature group- integrations, formulas, waterfalls, the list goes on. From there, you can easily solve automation problems by putting those different attributes together like a puzzle. 

I decided to share the original course I made to onboard the first few The Kiln employees. It's not entirely comprehensive, but it's a great way to get started in understanding the functions of the tool and putting them together to create a table. 

A couple things to note:
1. This is more specifically for using Clay in outbound. There are a million other use cases, but since most of our clients were in outbound at the time, that is what I wanted to train the employees on. 
2. All of the tutorials super informal, and I make notes to the hypothetical employee that won't always be best practice. At the time they were preferences, but not set rules on how to use Clay. 
3. This is not comprehensive whatsoever- it was meant to get an employee to at least use the tool relatively well. If you want a more in-depth course, hit up Nathan Lippi for his Clay Bootcamp.

Here's the first video. If you want the rest, just comment something and I'll send the whole doc over.

### 256. 7176960448078999553

- URL: https://www.linkedin.com/posts/patrickspychalski_clay-has-a-feature-that-can-scrape-just-about-activity-7176960448078999553-cDWq
- Author: Patrick Spychalski (@patrickspychalski)
- Posted: 2024-03-22T15:18:32.299Z

#### Verbatim Content

Clay has a feature that can scrape just about any website on the internet.

I feel like Clay's Chrome Extension is not talked about nearly enough, but it essentially unlocks the entire internet to being scraped and imported into Clay for enrichment. 

I've been using this a lot for micro-campaigns at The Kiln, where we'll scrape a list of 100-1000 companies and send hyper-specific copy based on what the list is about.

For example, in the video I show a website we scraped called "Fortune's Best Workplaces in Technology". We actually referenced this list in our email copy to the prospects at these companies, congratulating them on the achievement and then segueing them into a recruiting offer. 

I highly recommend giving it a whirl on a list that you've wanted to scrape but didn't feel like doing manually 🔧

### 257. 7178382706618060800

- URL: https://www.linkedin.com/posts/patrickspychalski_this-kind-of-campaign-is-severely-underrated-activity-7178382706618060800-wzn-
- Author: Patrick Spychalski (@patrickspychalski)
- Posted: 2024-03-26T13:30:05.159Z

#### Verbatim Content

This kind of campaign is severely underrated and can absolutely rip if done correctly

I by no means invented it, but have used it for my agency quite a few times with results good enough for me to want to share it. 

It's called a "Do the Math" campaign, and the name is pretty self-explanatory. It uses a math equation somewhere in the copy to convey the value that a client's service could bring the company, using data specific to the prospect. 

In this video, I show an example of this in a client's campaign in the ecom space. It's such a great example of using numbers to contextualize the effect of a great service on a large company. 

Hope this is helpful!

### 258. 7178745101832060928

- URL: https://www.linkedin.com/posts/patrickspychalski_i-created-an-email-flow-that-references-a-activity-7178745101832060928-YQoV
- Author: Patrick Spychalski (@patrickspychalski)
- Posted: 2024-03-27T13:30:06.911Z

#### Verbatim Content

I created an email flow that references a company's latest Instagram post in the first line. 

This actually wasn't possible to truly automate until Clay released their new source integration with Apify, a site that allows you to comb pretty much anything for data.

I used Apify to find IG account data, then imported it into Clay. From there, I used AI to reference a post based on it's caption, metadata, and more. 

I hope this video gives you some ideas on how you can leverage Clay with third party data providers like Apify!

### 260. 7166438994015162368

- URL: https://www.linkedin.com/posts/patrickspychalski_heres-the-story-of-how-i-got-a-43-response-activity-7166438994015162368-zH3P
- Author: Patrick Spychalski (@patrickspychalski)
- Posted: 2024-02-22T14:30:02.171Z

#### Verbatim Content

Here's the story of how I got a 43% response rate (and 2 deals) from a tiny local leads campaign 🏪

My side business, a vintage clothing store, was facing a problem. I had a ton of inventory, but nowhere to put it. 

I didn't want to deal with the problems of dealing online (no scalability, time consuming), and I didn't want to commit to a storefront either due to high upfront cost and long lease terms. 

I came up with an idea:

There are dozens of bookstores, record stores, plant stores, and other great spaces that are always looking for more revenue. 

My plan was to reach out to all of these stores and ask if I could house my clothing there, in return for a clothing sales commission

I used Clay to help find the businesses via the Google Maps integration, saving me a ton of time in research. 

This was a while ago- with new updates and knowledge, I could automate SO much more of this in Clay, including finding social media profiles, emails for the owners, and personalized email snippets based on the company's defining traits.

Regardless, I think this is worth sharing to prove that Clay can be great for local leads too 🤝

### 263. 7168613318650195968

- URL: https://www.linkedin.com/posts/patrickspychalski_claygent-is-easily-one-of-the-most-powerful-activity-7168613318650195968-4nBd
- Author: Patrick Spychalski (@patrickspychalski)
- Posted: 2024-02-28T14:30:01.561Z

#### Verbatim Content

Claygent is easily one of the most powerful tools in sales prospecting right now, and not nearly enough people know about it ⭐️

Claygent is essentially a combination of a web scraper and and AI agent that can find super niche data on companies that can't be found using a conventional scraper. 

Think upcoming webinars, company competitors, company tutorial videos, API docs, the list goes on forever. This can all be found automatically for thousands of companies using Claygent now. 

This video is a quick overview of the tool that I created when the feature was released, including an overview by Clay's insanely talented Adam Eldefrawy, who created this incredible tool. 

If you haven't tried it, I highly recommend giving it a whirl for your use case 💼

### 264. 7168251376878223360

- URL: https://www.linkedin.com/posts/patrickspychalski_heres-a-case-i-made-in-clay-a-while-back-activity-7168251376878223360-DtL0
- Author: Patrick Spychalski (@patrickspychalski)
- Posted: 2024-02-27T14:31:47.918Z

#### Verbatim Content

Here's a case I made in Clay a while back that finds emails out of thin air.

In this situation, I had a client that helped auto-generate API documentation for companies with public APIs. The founder realized that companies with "readme" API docs would be great prospects to reach out to. 

However, there was a problem. Readme wasn't easily scrapeable, as the docs essentially acted as their own website. 

To get around this, I used Clay to run a Google Search at scale, extract the company from the documentation, and then find decision makers and emails using the data I found from different integrations. 

If I did this now, it would actually be WAY easier to do this in Clay as I could just use Claygent to find the links to a company's API documentation. Regardless, it's a good exercise in using Clay's integrations creatively. 

Hope this is helpful!

#### Verbatim Content

/goal mode has quickly become my favorite AI tool. Here's what I used it on this weekend to accomplish 10 in depth tasks from one terminal. 

This weekend I went for a walk with my dog and made a 30 minute voice note building the context for each of these tasks, what good looked like, and how it would know it was done. 

All of this was done from one chat that I was checking in on just my phone while it ran. Used about 25% of my weekly usage. 

1. Automatically built a new Clay function as we got early access to the new CLI and it's awesome. So we built a new Clay function for email finding. The Clay table was built with my autoclay skill on github. 

2. Built a bounce rate report evaluating the source of bounces in our campaigns this week.

3. Ran a test to see which validation provider would have caught the most "this user wasn't found" bounces. At of our stack, LeadMagic won. (We also tested who would have falsely named our positive responses invalid). 

4. Finished a target market list for a customer finding all contacts at companies. It waterfalled Prospeo, BlitzAPI, and the new Parallel Web Systems Entity search. (I'll probably make a post just about that)

5. It completed a ChatGPT search ranking audit for 40k companies for a customer. 

6. Scraped a government directory of government agencies that still have budget left and what types of projects. 

7. Fine tuned the new Gemma model to test doing some custom variable writing/ICP scoring. (Another post necessary on this too).

8. I had 3 company lists I manually made and it audited that our internal company list building skill would find all the companies I manually found but also all companies that were added were real.

9. Launched campaigns for 4 customers to be ready for today.

10. We are speccing out scraping all of Google maps again so we can have all the data saved instead of scraping per category. 

Bonus: It also drafted this LinkedIn post update!

I'm finding the most important part for goal mode is to be even more clear about the end state and verifying that end state than you think but Codex is a great thought partner to help you do that!

### 3. 7467595535072276483

- URL: https://www.linkedin.com/posts/outboundphd_we-launched-a-fully-ai-managed-campaign-for-activity-7467595535072276483-2NmD
- Author: 🦾Eric Nowoslawski (@outboundphd)
- Posted: 2026-06-02T15:18:36.777Z

#### Verbatim Content

We launched a fully AI managed campaign for a customer using "Autoresearch" as the foundation and it doubled their positive lead rate. Here's what we did. 

 If you haven't seen me talk about this before, "autoresearch" is a GitHub repo that Andre Kapathi put out that basically helps people run local machine learning easily on their computers. 

I had the idea that instead of running a loop every 5 minutes to try to improve an outcome, we can run it every week on the data from cold email campaigns in order to increase positive response rates.

The first thing we needed to do was get the context together for all of the different messaging and audience targets that it could use and lock in a CTA for the campaigns that we wanted to optimize for. I didn't want it to just invent that. 

It could give away this software for free and then call that experiment a win. We needed the CTA to be locked in.

After we gathered all the context from the sales team and their calls and the audience targets that we wanted to go after, we made the underlying dataset using Clay Prospeo and stored it all in Supabase. 

The main learning that we got here was that you never want to have what I call a "game-time decision." For it to say, "Hey I want to run an experiment with this data point," you need to give it all the data points it might use so that it's not inventing ways to pull that data that you don't approve. 

We fed it the campaigns that we are already running and just let it rip, trying to optimize for more replies.

We're running over 150 campaigns in their Smartlead account right now and had two weeks in a row where we've increased the reply rate and the positive response rate. 

Keep in mind the CTA is locked in so it's always going towards what we want, which is a product sign up. 

I made a YouTube video about this that, if you'd like to see, just leave a comment and I'll send you the video link.

### 4. 7469818041262981122

- URL: https://www.linkedin.com/posts/outboundphd_heres-the-full-presentation-on-how-we-are-activity-7469818041262981122-p66o
- Author: 🦾Eric Nowoslawski (@outboundphd)
- Posted: 2026-06-08T18:30:03.545Z

#### Verbatim Content

Here's the full presentation on how we are turning AI into full blown employees at Growth Engine X. 3 steps I talk about in the video are below. 

1. You need to be able give it context. Dump your written communications and meeting recordings into Obsidian with the help of Codex/Claude Code.

2. Sit down and spend 20 minutes giving it all the API keys it needs. 

3. Create a loop where it can see that something needs to get done. 

Have it propose a plan proactively to do the work for you. Accept the plan or edit and it'll save a skill when it successfully runs. 

Step 1 and 2 you should get done today. 

For those that are new, I highly recommend just using the Codex desktop app because it's the easiest to work with and get connected. 

I think using Hermes or OpenAI is absolutely worth it for those of you that want to dabble. I'm having more fun with Hermes right now. 

Step three is the key. Most people don't know what AI can actually help them with and what it can do. 

Having it review your action items and proactively propose how to help you, and then working with it on that, is the key to getting it closer to being an employee. 

Video here for those that want to watch the full presentation.

### 5. 7468651616347459585

- URL: https://www.linkedin.com/posts/outboundphd_just-gave-a-talk-about-how-to-turn-codex-activity-7468651616347459585-_pRb
- Author: 🦾Eric Nowoslawski (@outboundphd)
- Posted: 2026-06-05T13:15:06.174Z

#### Verbatim Content

Just gave a talk about how to turn Codex/OpenClaw/Hermes (whatever you'd like) into a real employee for your business in 3 steps. Here it is for those that couldn't be at the event. 

I fully believe that these models are incredibly intelligent now and their tool calling has gotten so good. If you aren't using AI as an employee in your business, you just haven't put the energy in to get it done. 

Everyone can get started in 3 steps. The first 2 you should drop what you're doing this Friday afternoon and get them done right away. The last step takes more time. 

The agents need context, connections, and practice creating in order to be useful. 

1. Tips to gather context

For most of us, our world is recorded digitally and written down in Slack and CRM systems. 

Give these tools access to whatever you're comfortable with and have it start building a company brain for yourself from the transcripts of your calls, the conversations in your Slack, and your email. 

Whatever you can give it go for it. 

Someone asked me yesterday how I keep the context organized. The truth is beyond just asking Claude to organize itself I don't think about this at all. 

With a million-token context window, that's as much text as the Bible. My business for sure isn't that complicated. 

Have it create a local brain in markdown files. Use Obsidian (which is free) if you want to get fancy.

2. Give the agents connection to tools

This is going to sound like the dumbest suggestion but it was really a blocker for my team to get the API keys that were necessary in order to make the most use of these agents. 

It felt like "we have too many tools, I wish I just had a list of everythign so I could get it done in 10 minutes."

Your search history on your browser has everything.

Grab the last 30 days of the sites you visit and dedupe them in a google sheet. 

Pick out the ones you want to give access to your agents and you're done. 

3. Build a loop so it can actually work for you. 

After we built this, it feels so obvious but I think this is the number one tip to get people to start using AI. 

Tell your agent to run a scheduled task any time new work is created 

A client call
An internal call
check slack every 30 minutes

Wherever a record of work is created. 

 Use the schedule task for it to read the task. Based on the context that you gave it in your company brain and what it has done for you in the past, try to suggest the plan for how the agent would execute this for you. 

Edit and accept as necessary so the agent can start doing real work. 

 This proactive loop, where a suggestion for work that the agent can take on so that you can get a message every day in Slack after a meeting or something, is the thing that really increased the rate of learning for our agent. 

Instead of us coming up with ideas of how the agent could help us, the agent was doing the work for us. It has been absolutely pivotal in making this work.

### 6. 7465756271334371328

- URL: https://www.linkedin.com/posts/outboundphd_i-dont-write-prompts-for-list-qualification-activity-7465756271334371328-I875
- Author: 🦾Eric Nowoslawski (@outboundphd)
- Posted: 2026-05-28T13:30:02.130Z

#### Verbatim Content

I don't write prompts for list qualification anymore. Instead, I use a skill I call it the ICP prompt loop.

I tell Codex (or Claude Code): "This is the input I'll give you. This is the output I want. Write the prompt that makes that happen." 

Then I feed it what we actually mean by giving it human verified data. 

"This is what we count as a B2B SaaS company, this is what we count as manufacturing, this is what financial services means to this client, and so on"

Then it runs on 10 real companies and shows me its work: "This one is B2B SaaS. This one we thought was, but isn't, and here's why. This one is manufacturing and I agree."

I correct it. It revises the prompt.

The rule that makes it work: it does not get to run across the full dataset until it shows me 10 companies with zero edits from me, three times in a row. 

One edit anywhere and the counter resets. It keeps feeding me 10 at a time until we get three clean rounds.

This week we used it on a 6-vertical TAM. Sonnet 4.6 on the batch API did the actual qualification for about $50 because there were too many companies for task sub-agents.

This way, you stop guessing whether your prompt is good and start proving it on the only thing that matters, real rows from your actual list.

### 7. 7465114556546273280

- URL: https://www.linkedin.com/posts/outboundphd_how-we-are-building-clay-tables-with-claude-activity-7465114556546273280-exe9
- Author: 🦾Eric Nowoslawski (@outboundphd)
- Posted: 2026-05-26T19:00:05.402Z

#### Verbatim Content

How we are building Clay tables with Claude Code and Codex all relies on Browser Use and how it does browser automation differently than other tools

Most browser automation works off screenshots, look at the pixels, guess where to click, hope the layout didn't move.

The reason browser-use has been more reliable for me is that it doesn't only read the screen. It also reads the front-end API the page is using, so it can find the most dependable way to actually perform the action instead of pixel-hunting.

That sounds like a small distinction. In practice it's the difference between automation that survives a layout change and automation that silently breaks the moment a button moves four pixels.

So when we give it Clay tables to copy, we don't have to keep files on how all of Clay works, it just learns the same way a human would on the user interface and makes notes when it learns something new.

### 8. 7462487337751646208

- URL: https://www.linkedin.com/posts/outboundphd_a-client-needed-a-highly-targeted-list-of-activity-7462487337751646208-ojFi
- Author: 🦾Eric Nowoslawski (@outboundphd)
- Posted: 2026-05-19T13:00:27.630Z

#### Verbatim Content

A client needed a highly targeted list of regulated financial-advice firms in the UK. Not "financial services companies". Regulated advice firms, split by whether they were independent or sat inside a larger network.

The thing that cracked it was a sentence in a website footer.

Here's the build. 

Three sources to find every firm that could possibly fit: every financial services and investment management company on LinkedIn, the Companies House database (scraped with Browser Use), and the FCA registry (they have a downloadable API). 

Then the hard part was finding which of these are in-network vs independent, how are they regulated, how many advisors do they have. 

Advisor count came from LinkedIn. Regulation and network status had to come from the websites themselves.

In-network firms are required to put compliance language in their footer. So we used Exa to find what that language actually looks like. 

Once we had the pattern, it was over. Compliance text gets copy-pasted verbatim across an entire network, so footer-matching told us instantly who was in-network and who wasn't.

Claude Code built the orchestration and ran it in goal mode overnight — clear outcome, "check every page of every site for these keywords, don't stop until it's done." About three hours.

Out the other side: 1,000 ideal-fit firms, 2,000 ideal-fit contacts. And that was just the UK list — US is next.

The lesson that travels: when a segment is hard to define, don't guess the signal. Go find the exact language the right companies are already using, then match it.

### 9. 7460737440861978624

- URL: https://www.linkedin.com/posts/outboundphd_the-new-prospeo-data-filters-are-absolutely-activity-7460737440861978624-_wbT

The new Prospeo data filters are absolutely insane. I'll literally just drop the filters below so you can see them. 

They asked me 90 days ago, "if we could have any filters in the platform, what would you want to see?" and I can't think of a filter that they missed. 

This is what they added

AI Lookalikes - find companies by AI generated lookalikes with 3 tiers of targeting

Type & Business Model - b2b/b2c, free trail or sales led? usage based pricing, are they a saas or ecom. lots of great stuff here. 

Keywords - keywords derived from their internal keyword database, their website pages, their social media, etc. Website pages being my favorite upgrade.

AI Attributes - do they offer a demo, uptime guarentees, SSO podcast, blog?

Company Awards & Certs - filter for SOC2 and GDPR

Website Traffic - filter for monthly visits, changes, and audience by country

Key Executives Events - executives joining or leaving the company

Company News - keyword search for news. 

Website Search - search comapnies by what keywords they have on their page and can even refine to pricing pages only and such. 

ICP & Targeting - filter companies by who the companies sell to. never seen a platform that has this as a filter. 

Products & Services - filter by what they sell

Integrations - who do they integrate with

Key Customers - what are the case studies on their site? 

Google Discovery - target companies by what google search terms they rank for! never seen this either. 

The reason we went all in on using them as a primary source of data is that I knew betting on the team would pay off and this update certainly has. 

If you want to see how we are leveraging these new data points for our campaigns, I'm actually running an event with Will Aitken and Matthew Putnam next Thursday.

### 10. 7462124958282899456

- URL: https://www.linkedin.com/posts/outboundphd_browser-use-harness-is-actually-insane-let-activity-7462124958282899456-IOH7
- Author: 🦾Eric Nowoslawski (@outboundphd)

#### Verbatim Content

Browser Use Harness is actually insane. Let me show you some custom workflows we built last week with it. 

Browser Use Harness is a free, open-source browser tool. 

Here's three completely different jobs this week, ("browser-use" search GitHub for "browser-use" / Browser Harness). 

Job one: score 13,000 domains for blacklisting. I didn't want to pay for a tool, so I pointed it at MX Toolbox's free blacklist checker (their API is free but capped at 200 calls/day). 

I showed Claude Code a screenshot and said "use Browser Harness to check these." It's been pushing through them, 9,000 done so far, 7,500 clean, 1,600 flagged. Building the list while I do other things.

Job two: find and screenshot SaaS case-study pages. I asked it to find the case-study page for a set of SaaS companies, screenshot it, and scroll a bit so we didn't capture a broken-looking frame. 

It came back with Notion, Slack, and HubSpot clean screenshots, and it even flagged when something was obscured.

Job three: check sales tax on a Shopify store. I had it take a product all the way to checkout on a real store and record whether tax was applied. 

New Jersey doesn't tax most clothing; California does. It confirmed CA charged tax, and logged the addresses and shipping method it used to get there.

Same tool, three jobs, zero custom code Each one was "here's a screenshot, here's what I want, go."

It's free and it's fast. I can't imagine I won't keep finding go-to-market uses for it.

### 11. 7463208232631783426

- URL: https://www.linkedin.com/posts/outboundphd_joe-biden-for-america-has-a-shopify-store-activity-7463208232631783426-QyEC
- Author: 🦾Eric Nowoslawski (@outboundphd)
- Posted: 2026-05-21T12:45:02.363Z

#### Verbatim Content

Joe Biden For America has a Shopify store. That one fact is why almost every Shopify list you can buy is wrong.

If you want a list of real e-commerce brands, you have options: Apollo's technology filter, Prospeo's technology filter, BuiltWith, Store Leads. They all have the same two problems.

One: is the store even still active?

Two: does the company just have Shopify bolted onto a site somewhere, while not actually being a consumer e-commerce brand? A campaign, a content site, a B2B company with a swag shop. All are "Shopify stores," none of them what you meant.

On the actual home page: is there an add to cart? Are there product SKUs? Are the Shopify storefront widgets right there? A real DTC brand has this on the home page. 

A "technically has Shopify" site hides it on some subdomain, that's exactly where these lists get fooled.

So we combined and deduped everything, Apollo, BuiltWith, Store Leads, Prospeo, and scored on signals that mean commerce: non-empty product feeds, cart and checkout paths, payments, product and collection URLs. 

Default Shopify placeholders and dead stores got dropped.

2.76 million rows in. About 2 million genuinely active stores out.

I love giving things away for free but out of respect for these companies I can't just drop this list. 

Keep these things in mind or even tell your own agents to think about this the next time you target ecommerce and need to clean up your list.

### 12. 7466126252010000384

- URL: https://www.linkedin.com/posts/outboundphd_a-client-wanted-six-verticals-of-us-companies-activity-7466126252010000384-Q80-
- Author: 🦾Eric Nowoslawski (@outboundphd)
- Posted: 2026-05-29T14:00:12.397Z

#### Verbatim Content

A client wanted six verticals of US companies, 50–5,000 employees, properly qualified: B2B SaaS, retail/e-comm/CPG, manufacturing/logistics, insurance, healthcare, financial services.

The whole thing cost about $200 dollars of credits.

Here's the stack.

We keep every company we've ever gathered for any client in a Supabase of ~15M Clay-enriched companies, wired to a TypeSense search engine (~$200/mo) so we can query it in milliseconds instead of waiting on Supabase. 

We pulled everything matching from there first, free, because we already enriched it.

Whatever we couldn't cover, we uploaded as an exclude list and searched the gaps in Prospeo v2, one custom company search per vertical (their type + business-model filters are genuinely good now). Merge, dedupe.

Qualification ran on Sonnet 4.6 through the Anthropic batch API. This was about $50, because there were too many companies for task sub-agents.

Then more enrichment from our Clay database, 87 columns shipped. Client said it was an awesome list.

If you don't have an internal database, skip straight BlitzAPI (unlimited company/contact API), SIGNALIZ just dropped unlimited people and companies and so did QuickEnrich. 

Keep in mind, you don't need our setup to run this. You just need to stop paying agency rates for something a coding agent and a batch API can do for the price an amazing dinner.

Setting up daily scrapers with Codex and Trigger.dev to scrape custom data like government filings feels like a cheat code. Here's how we set it up for a client. 

We had a client want to scrape SEC filing information daily. They said someone on their team would manually find the information and put it into a spreadsheet.

It took just one screenshot and a prompt to Codex to get everything in the last 30 days scraped and another prompt to host it on trigger.dev to save and push straight to a Smartlead campaign. 

I feel like when I would make posts like this in the past I had to share all this detail but literally it was just 

1. give the link and a screenshot of data I want scraped. 
2. Let codex explore the structure of the site. 
3. It returned a file I validated manually that the data was correct.
4. I asked it to deploy on trigger.dev 

Literally that simple.

Then we use OpenWeb Ninja and Parallel Web Systems to find the contacts based on their name and company and we're all set.

### 14. 7465390129390538752

- URL: https://www.linkedin.com/posts/outboundphd_we-crawled-thousands-of-websites-for-this-activity-7465390129390538752-AxE5
- Author: 🦾Eric Nowoslawski (@outboundphd)
- Posted: 2026-05-27T13:15:07.088Z

#### Verbatim Content

We crawled thousands of websites for this list and paid nothing to actually read the pages and score them for ICP fit. 

For a lot of jobs, you do not need a paid scraping API to get website text. An open-source HTML-to-text converter gets you the homepage and footer content fine. 

Paid tools like ZenRows and Firecrawl earn their money on genuinely hard targets — heavy anti-bot, JS-gated content, sites that fight you. "I just want the text on this page" usually isn't that.

The second cost people add without thinking is AI scoring. We didn't score anything with a model here. We were matching specific compliance keywords in footers, that's deterministic. 

A keyword match doesn't need an LLM, and bolting one on would have made it slower and more expensive for a worse answer.

So the run was: open-source text extraction, keyword matching, Claude Code orchestrating it in goal mode overnight. Ridiculously cheap for thousands of sites.

The general rule: every paid API and every AI call in a pipeline should have to justify why a free, deterministic step couldn't do it first. Most of them can't justify it. 

They just got added because that's the default everyone copies.

Link here to the repo I used. https://lnkd.in/ex2un2Kb

### 16. 7461041479667531776

- URL: https://www.linkedin.com/posts/outboundphd_ive-thought-on-many-walks-with-my-dog-about-activity-7461041479667531776-mn0s
- Author: 🦾Eric Nowoslawski (@outboundphd)
- Posted: 2026-05-15T13:15:08.200Z

#### Verbatim Content

I've thought on many walks with my dog about how to build a great company list from multiple sources. This video goes over the best way I can think of today. 

I think there's a spectrum of how to create lists. 

Standard filters -> Lists fully made with AI

The standard filters you can get from LinkedIn data via BlitzAPI and Google Maps data

Lists fully made with AI filters need to be found with tools like Exa/Parallel Web Systems or DiscoLike/Ocean.io

Each method has it's tradeoffs. 

So I put them into 4 buckets

1. List where standard filters are more than enough. Like when you want to email any CEO/Founder with 10-20 employees for some reason. Blitz API can handle this alone. Say you want everyone with google my business profiles. Go to google maps first and then find contacts via Blitz/prospeo or your other favorite tools. 

2. When standard filters are good but won't get everything. Companies that provide marketing agency services won't ALWAYS say that they are in marketing services/advertising services. 

So you want to use standard linkedin filters via a tool like Blitz, then move to a tool like Discolike/Ocean for AI targeting. Then you have a database to work off of. Use that database as exclusion and run Exa to find more until it runs 10 times in a row and only adds one company because of non fit or dedupe against your current list

3. Standard list filtering just won't cut it. Targeting SaaS companies. There's too many industries to account for. You need Disco/Ocean/ or prospeo with their new company filtering release. Exa to follow to clean up anything you didn't find. 

4. No fitlers will work. must start with Ocean/Disco and clean up with Exa

All the way, using an ICP prompt loop strategy that creates task sub agents to ensure the companies fit your ICP for every company that it pulls. 

I explain the full process in detail in the video below and I'll be producing a YouTube video talking about this specifically in the coming week

### 17. 7457832794098978816

- URL: https://www.linkedin.com/posts/outboundphd_we-automatically-pulled-35-sales-signals-activity-7457832794098978816-4KG_

We automatically pulled 35+ sales signals for a customer's TAM with Parallel Web Systems and your favorite batch API. Here's what we got and how we did it.

We had a customer that wanted to check for multiple signals of growth in investor filings.

Regulation Changes
Investments in AI
Investments in new plants
Capital expenditure reductions
Market outlook for the company
Etc.

Tons of juicy data to help craft an awesome campaign. 

All of which we could find in the company's investor filings.

In the past we would use Claygent to do this. My main complaint with claygent is that it's very goal oriented. So when it finds one thing, it'll stop and return the results. Which is great for keeping costs low and when you want one signal.

But we wanted everything.

Enter Parallel Extract, Codex, and the gpt 5 nano flex api. (Life hack, the flex api pricing from open AI on gpt 5 nano is the same as the batch pricing. 10 minute turn around promise vs. 24 hours)

We found and extracted all of the information with parallel extract orchestrated by codex sub agents. 

Ran a loop to ensure that the prompt we were using for the data summary and extraction with gpt 5 nano would get the job done. 

We manually verified the data at this point. 

When it looked good to us, we ran the whole extraction. 

Over 35+ signals found per company prompted with just Wispr Flow and I just came in to help make sure it was pulling all the data we actually wanted. 

Screenshot below of a summary of data findings from the run!

### 18. 7463574704591728640

- URL: https://www.linkedin.com/posts/outboundphd_the-biggest-cost-hack-in-list-building-right-activity-7463574704591728640-CU1e
- Author: 🦾Eric Nowoslawski (@outboundphd)
- Posted: 2026-05-22T13:01:16.087Z

#### Verbatim Content

The biggest cost hack in list building right now is asking codex/claude code to use sub agents to clean your lists. Don't pay for the API tokens. 

Cleaning a big list, the reflex is "send it to the batch API." Sometimes you should. But I had 80,000 rows that needed an ICP pass, and instead of paying for it, I told Codex: "set up a task sub-agent to figure out if these are good fits."

It ran a GPT-5.4-mini sub-agent across all 80,000 and sorted good fits from bad. Used only my usage on the Codex $200 plan. No API costs. 

I use Codex every single day this week and I still have something like 40% of my weekly usage left each week. The amount they give you is genuinely hard to spend.

So the order of operations isn't "AI." It's:

1. Free, deterministic checks first: a Python script reading obvious signals off the page.
2. Coding-agent task sub-agents for the judgment calls: included in usage you're already paying for.
3. The paid batch API only for what's left, when the volume truly justifies it.

To get it to work in your session without it pusing you to use the API, just ask "Spawn 10 task sub agents to handle this for me" and it'll engage the function I'm talking about here. 

There is a limit where the time trade off is worth it and it'll take more time to do this than to just pay for the API.

For those cases, keep in mind that the gpt 5 nano and mini API on the batch tier (24 hour turn around) and flex tier (10 minute turn around) are the same price.

### 19. 7462928895928029184

- URL: https://www.linkedin.com/posts/outboundphd_someone-in-the-clay-community-wanted-the-activity-7462928895928029184-YKSF
- Author: 🦾Eric Nowoslawski (@outboundphd)
- Posted: 2026-05-20T18:15:03.303Z

#### Verbatim Content

Someone in the Clay community wanted the CES Exhibitors list and couldn't figure it out. I screenshotted their message and Codex did it in one shot. (full video). 

Rule one of scraping: if you can see it, you can scrape it.

I went to the CES exhibitor directory. They have a category page with a "View All" button. That's it — that's the keys to the kingdom. 

No pagination tricks, no searching A, then AB, then AC to brute force the list.

I copied the page, opened Codex, and described the job in plain English (dictated with Wispr Flow, because typing it would have taken longer):

"Click into each category. Grab every company. For each company pull the address, the website, and the LinkedIn URL. Move to the next category until they're all done."

Sent a screenshot so it knew what the page looked like. Then I kicked back.

It came back with 3,565 unique exhibitors and 14,000 category-membership rows (companies show up in multiple categories so I asked it to confirm that's why the numbers differ).

No scraping tool. No proxies. No paid API. A coding agent, a screenshot, and a clear description of what I wanted.

I feel bottlenecked only by what work from AI I can verify, not it's actually capacity to do things.

### 21. 7468374845337026560

- URL: https://www.linkedin.com/posts/outboundphd_1-positive-response-for-every-90-contacts-activity-7468374845337026560-bRQP
- Author: 🦾Eric Nowoslawski (@outboundphd)
- Posted: 2026-06-04T18:55:18.823Z

#### Verbatim Content

1 positive response for every 90 contacts on completely cold contacts is a welcome sight these days. Here's what's going on with this campaign. 

First, this campaign is working so well not only because the customer has a good offer, they are targeting an industry that rarely gets any kind of good cold emails. 

None of these emails were found using any linkedin data based contact based. 

We built the whole database ourselves by scraping google maps and mining the website for emails. 

So already, their inboxes are far less busy. 

This campaign in particular also has 3 levels of personalization

1. Analyzing their Google reviews for complaints about a particular service. If any complaints mention our service keywords, they are qualified for the campaign where we make the offer that we can guarantee the complaint with that service won't happen again. 

2. The messaging changes based on the role we found on the website for the email and if it's a generic email.

3. our PS line is asking for a referral to another contact we found on the site in case they aren't the right one. 

This all packaged together means that we are only going after people that we can prove have had the pain in the past (and customers have complained about it online), we segmented based on the role so each role gets the best message for them, and if they aren't in charge of this at their company, we name drop someone else. 

Offer by far is the most important. 

When we tested just a regular campaign with just spintax it still got 1 lead for every 250 emails. 

So the AI variables doubled the lead rate but again, offer is always king.

### 22. 7465024294767521792

- URL: https://www.linkedin.com/posts/outboundphd_the-list-is-the-message-i-dont-know-who-activity-7465024294767521792-VDUX
- Author: 🦾Eric Nowoslawski (@outboundphd)
- Posted: 2026-05-26T13:01:25.317Z

#### Verbatim Content

The list is the message. I don't know who first said it, but it's one of the truest things in outbound.

When the list is segmented precisely enough, the email almost writes itself. 

When it isn't, you end up writing clever copy to paper over the fact that you don't actually know who you're talking to.

Most platforms only let you segment by what a company is. Industry, size, tech stack. 

Useful, but it rarely changes your message on its own. CEO vs founder doesn't change what you say. Industry often doesn't either.

What changes the message is who they sell to. A company selling into legal teams has a completely different world than one selling to restaurant owners — different buyers, different pain, different proof. That difference is the message.

Prospeo's v2 now lets you filter on exactly that. Who a company sells to, the size of their customers, the department they sell into. That's not a nice-to-have filter. 

That's a segmentation axis that actually splits a list into groups that need different emails.

If a data point would change what you say, it's a reason to split into a separate campaign. 

If it wouldn't, don't split. "Who do they sell to" almost always changes what you say.

### 23. 7464665333078020096

- URL: https://www.linkedin.com/posts/outboundphd_0-difference-between-may-21-and-may-22-sending-activity-7464665333078020096-6-a2
- Author: 🦾Eric Nowoslawski (@outboundphd)
- Posted: 2026-05-25T13:15:02.181Z

#### Verbatim Content

0 difference between May 21 and May 22 sending in this screenshot except for changing subject lines which is a new spam filter hypothesis we are working on. 

Recently, Nikita MAILDOSO told me about their theory that copywriting is going to spam before a domain actually is. 

Yes, spintax and swapping your copywriting is good but the biggest leverage can come from your subject line, signature, and unsub language having more variation in it. 

This was a perfect proof point that just switching the subject line helped us a lot. 

Day 1 - just under 1% reply on of course a small sample set
Day 2 - 0 replies and 10% bounces..... terrible
Day 3 - subject line got 10 new variations. over 1% reply, bounces back to normal.

We didn't change the inboxes or anything. In this case, we just added more variation to the subject lines. 

To test this automatically, we've also built a inbox placement checking skill that deletes words to zero in on what's putting us in spam. I'll post about that another time but that's also been a huge help.

### 24. 7453442747140763648

- URL: https://www.linkedin.com/posts/outboundphd_if-your-campaign-is-getting-decent-out-of-activity-7453442747140763648-cl-V
- Author: 🦾Eric Nowoslawski (@outboundphd)
- Posted: 2026-04-24T14:00:29.188Z

#### Verbatim Content

If your campaign is getting decent out of office / negative reply rates, people are clearly reading, and nobody's responding positively, there's a good chance you're raising an objection you're not addressing. 

And you'll never know it because the prospect won't tell you.

They just delete. 

Every cold email creates an implicit pushback in the reader's mind.

"We help companies scale outbound" — they think "I already have an agency."

"We reduce your cloud costs by 40%" — they think "Switching sounds like a nightmare."

They read. They think the objection. They're gone.

Read your own email like a skeptical buyer. What's the first pushback? Now address it in one sentence. Not defensively. Casually.

"We help companies scale outbound (most of our clients already had an agency, we work alongside them, not replace them)"

"We reduce cloud costs by 40% (no migration required, we optimize your existing setup)"

One sentence. Inserted naturally. Kills the objection before it fully forms.

But don't start here.

If your targeting is wrong, objection preemption won't save you. If your offer is weak, addressing objections just highlights the weakness. And critically — don't bring up objections the prospect wasn't thinking about. If you're selling accounting services and you write "even though we're a small firm," you just planted doubt that didn't exist.

This is an advanced move. Use it when you've validated the campaign is reaching the right people, they're reading, and something in the copy is creating friction.

### 26. 7454566727675383808

- URL: https://www.linkedin.com/posts/outboundphd_people-have-been-asking-to-see-some-real-activity-7454566727675383808-EGZ2
- Author: 🦾Eric Nowoslawski (@outboundphd)
- Posted: 2026-04-27T16:26:47.030Z

#### Verbatim Content

People have been asking to see some real live GTM use cases in Claude code so I posted a video building your own email waterfall. Here's simply how to do it. 

Email finding all starts with up to date contact @ company finding data. 

For most business emails, it's some combination of the person's Name and company website. 

You can save a lot of money using MillionVerifier or MailTester.Ninja to just guess emails with common permutations. 

Then there's many reasons that this fails though so you want to bring out the big guns. 

Prospeo
LeadMagic
BlitzAPI
Icypeas
GetProspect 🇺🇦
TryKitt.ai
Smartlead's email finder 

Anyone that you can hook up to the API that also only charges you for valid emails. 

They can help you with edge cases like 

- The person's name is James on LinkedIn but their email is Jim@ something. 
- Catch all domains
- People that work at companies where the marketing site is different than their email (slack emails is actually slackhq and notion is actually meetnotion, etc.)

Have Claude take the input of the name, domain, and linkedin and ask it to make a waterfall for you.

First guessing common permutations
Then deferring to the big guns

Store with the date you verified the email in Supabase for use later. 

Add in something like Parallel Web Systems to find emails on websites if any of these providers miss it if you really really need the email. 

I explain this all in detail and show you how to build it on my YouTube as well if you want to see that video! Sorry it was too long to post here

### 28. 7462581598727708673

- URL: https://www.linkedin.com/posts/outboundphd_there-is-no-such-thing-as-a-b2b-saas-filter-activity-7462581598727708673-r1xf
- Author: 🦾Eric Nowoslawski (@outboundphd)
- Posted: 2026-05-19T19:15:01.198Z

#### Verbatim Content

There is no such thing as a "B2B SaaS" filter. There's no clean "e-commerce brand" one either. And if you pull "financial services," you will miss every bank.

This is why client vertical lists come back full of junk.

A few real examples from a TAM we built this week, US companies, 50–5,000 employees, six verticals:

Bacardi is an e-commerce/retail brand. It lists itself under beverage manufacturing. Hit just "retail" and you'll never see it.

Manufacturing and logistics companies notoriously aren't all on LinkedIn, and the ones that are file themselves under wild industries.

Healthcare is the worst. Pull "healthcare" and you've blended medical device makers, healthcare SaaS, hospitals, and health insurers into one pile that means nothing to the client.

Don't filter your way to a vertical, define it, then qualify into it. 

We pull broad from our internal database and Prospeo's v2 type/business-model filters, merge and dedupe, then run every company through a written definition of what that vertical actually means to this specific client. 

"Banks count as financial services here. Fintech does not."

This way we get more coverage on great companies and can toss out companies that say they are in Real Estate but are really services companies for real estate professionals and such.

### 29. 7445470149790285824

- URL: https://www.linkedin.com/posts/outboundphd_the-most-common-thing-i-hear-from-founders-activity-7445470149790285824-MiM3
- Author: 🦾Eric Nowoslawski (@outboundphd)
- Posted: 2026-04-02T14:00:13.856Z

#### Verbatim Content

The most common thing I hear from founders: "Cold email doesn't work for my business."

5 years of doing this, I've never found a business where some version of cold email doesn't work. The problem is what you're trying to accomplish with cold email, not the channel.

When people say "cold email doesn't work for my business," what I hear is: "cold email isn't helping me grow revenue." I think they're picking the wrong kind of outbound.

There are 3 types:

Type 1: Meeting-ready leads

Direct ask. "Want to save 40 hours/month on bookkeeping?" → "Yes, let's talk."

Works when the prospect already knows they have the problem. Accountants, agencies, SaaS with obvious pain points. You just need to show up at the right time.

Type 2: Hand raisers

Give value first. "I built a list of 200 companies engaging with your competitors on LinkedIn. Want it?"

Works when the prospect doesn't know they have a problem yet. Complex sales, high-trust products. You're not asking for a meeting. You're starting a conversation by providing something useful upfront.

Type 3: Partners, recruiting, and affiliates

Not selling. Connecting. "We send 8 million emails a month and need deliverability partners" or "We're hiring SDRs who want to learn at scale."

This is the most underused cold email funnel. Nobody thinks of recruiting or partnerships as cold email use cases, but the mechanics are identical.

I think every business should be using cold email. It just matters: can it work to get you meeting-ready leads? Can it work to get you hand raisers? Or does it work to help you build partnerships, affiliates, and recruit new employees?

And how much energy can you spend converting a meeting-ready lead to a deal closed versus a hand raiser to a deal closed versus a partner or affiliate?

### 30. 7452072352647077888

- URL: https://www.linkedin.com/posts/outboundphd_heres-the-subject-line-framework-weve-been-activity-7452072352647077888-6VoD
- Author: 🦾Eric Nowoslawski (@outboundphd)
- Posted: 2026-04-20T19:15:01.680Z

#### Verbatim Content

Here's the subject line framework we've been using for the past year or so. 

Our guiding principle: the job of the subject line is to get our prospects to open. That's the only goal.

There are two main frameworks to think through:

Framework 1: The subject line looks like something a colleague or customer would send.

"pizza order" — we used this for a restaurant platform client. Looks like an internal email. Open rates were insane.

"sales reports" — looks like something from your team.

"quick question" — classic for a reason. I know people love to hate it and I hate it too, but it still works. Did I mention that I hate it?

These work because they bypass the "this is a cold email" filter in the prospect's brain.

Framework 2: Put your offer in the subject line to entice them.

Subject: "[Company]'s competitors"
Preview text: "I have a list of people engaging with {{competitor}}..."

The subject creates a gap. The preview text hints at something valuable. Together they create enough curiosity to earn the click.

The worst subject lines try to sell. "50% Off Our Platform" — deleted without opening.

The best subject lines look like they came from someone you know. The best subject lines look like something they should already be keeping their eyes on. Or they can be punchy enough to earn the click from a very short offer.

### 32. 7444371635652059137

- URL: https://www.linkedin.com/posts/outboundphd_before-you-write-a-single-email-theres-activity-7444371635652059137-xXoE
- Author: 🦾Eric Nowoslawski (@outboundphd)
- Posted: 2026-03-30T13:15:07.676Z

#### Verbatim Content

Before you write a single email, there's one question that determines everything — your copy, your CTA, your follow-up sequence, your entire funnel.

Most people skip it and wonder why their campaigns underperform.

The question: Can I ask for the meeting directly, or do I need to earn the right to ask?

That's the difference between generating meeting-ready leads and generating hand raisers.

A meeting-ready lead is someone who responds to your cold email and says "yes, let's talk next week." They're ready to book.

A hand raiser is someone who says "yes" to a lead magnet or a Loom video. They're interested, but they are not ready to meet with you right now. They require much more follow-up and warming before you book the meeting.

I see too many people going wrong here. They start using lead magnets, get excited about the response rates, and then try to immediately book meetings,  forgetting that not everyone is ready after one touchpoint. 

Everyone should try to start with getting meeting-ready leads. But if you can't, maybe your offer is very cyclical and prospects are locked into contracts, or it's a commoditized offering and you need to differentiate, you're going to run into problems. That's when you pivot to hand raisers.

Here's what we do at GEX. We offer a free campaign launch before people become customers. That generates meeting-ready leads. But when we paired that with a follow-up email offering to share a list of every new hire on LinkedIn or all the people engaging with their competitors' content — notified monthly — we 5x'd our response rates.

Sometimes it's not even one or the other. Email one goes for the meeting-ready lead. Email two follows up with a hand-raiser offer. Layer them together.

Which approach are you running right now?

### 33. 7443284459396857856

- URL: https://www.linkedin.com/posts/outboundphd_ive-changed-my-mind-on-a-lot-of-things-when-activity-7443284459396857856-_siN
- Author: 🦾Eric Nowoslawski (@outboundphd)
- Posted: 2026-03-27T13:15:04.658Z

#### Verbatim Content

I've changed my mind on a lot of things when it comes to cold email best practices. But these 3 principles have never changed. Not once.

Everything else people sell courses about? Noise on top of these.

1. Deliverability

If your emails don't land in the primary tab, nothing else matters. Not your copy. Not your list. Not your offer.

We always keep the amount of inboxes we need to send for a customer plus anywhere from 50 to 100% extra capacity warming up. That way we can switch out anything that's not working immediately. We monitor this daily for customers having issues and weekly across all customers automatically with Claude skills.

Most people skip this because it's boring. That's why most people's campaigns don't work.

2. The list

Accurate data on the right people. What doesn't work is a mediocre list with 30% bad contacts in it. You should spend as much time on list building as copywriting.

3. Copy with a clear offer that is easy to say yes to

There's a huge difference between copy that sounds clever and copy that converts cold traffic.

I think Alex Hormozi's framework — "We help customers accomplish their goal in this time without this risk" — is the perfect offer framework for cold email. But you also need to think about what's easy to say yes to. If you pitch "We help B2B companies speed up their sales process with a new CRM in 30 days without losing sales momentum," that's still a very tough ask. Changing out somebody's entire CRM is a big commitment. You want to go in with something easier to say yes to.

Shout out David Ogilvy — "your first line gets them to read the second line, and the second line gets them to read the third line" — one of my guiding frameworks. And Josh Braun is one of the best in the game at cold email copywriting if you want to study someone.

People ask me about send times, follow-up cadences, A/B test frameworks, personalization tokens. Those are all optimizations on top of these 3 things.

If your deliverability is broken, your list is wrong, or your copy doesn't resonate. no optimization saves you.

Get these 3 right and you're about 80% of the way to what most agencies charge $10K+/month to do.

### 34. 7451981768162844672

- URL: https://www.linkedin.com/posts/outboundphd_claude-code-doubled-response-rates-in-one-activity-7451981768162844672-7jDy
- Author: 🦾Eric Nowoslawski (@outboundphd)
- Posted: 2026-04-20T13:15:04.656Z

#### Verbatim Content

Claude Code doubled response rates in one week for an enterprise client of ours. We're starting to run Autoresearch for our customers. This is what it means. 

Last month, Andrej Karpathy dropped a public repo he called Autoresearch which was designed to give Machine Learning capabilities to anyone with a Claude Code or Codex account. 

The intention was to run short 5 minute experiments to improve AI on a task for himself. 

I immediately thought, "what if we were to do this for cold email campaigns." 

The basis for the experiment is to pick a singular metric to improve. Positive Response Rate is the obvious choice here. 

So we built a little repo that is trained on the client's past campaigns, their current sales/marketing context, ICP, etc and we connected it to our DiscoLike / Prospeo Account for list creation. 

It now runs experiments that proposes 

1. A list segmentation that has won in the past or something new to try.
2. Copywriting to go with this list and the angle that the copy should take
3. Uploads everything from valid emails to copy straight into Smartlead via the API. 

We're beginning to roll it out to other customers as we learn what makes this work. 

We have already learned that you need to give it rules of what the CTA can be so it doesn't just give away your product for free and that ALL of your data needs to be enriched in Clay and then stored in Supabase so that it doesn't have to make game time enrichment decisions. 

I think this ushers in the next phase of outbound (I would call it outbound 4.0) and I'm so excited for it. 

We custom made this for this customer and we are working on standardizing it so it works for everyone. When that's ready, we will of course be giving the repo away. 

Drop a comment so I know others would want to see it! 

Thanks to Gabe Emerson and Marielle Camba ☕️ for carrying the banner on this one!

### 35. 7442197288204042240

- URL: https://www.linkedin.com/posts/outboundphd_heres-three-things-i-see-beginners-worry-activity-7442197288204042240-hlpR
- Author: 🦾Eric Nowoslawski (@outboundphd)
- Posted: 2026-03-24T13:15:02.847Z

#### Verbatim Content

Here's three things I see beginners worry about in cold email that I don't worry about anymore.

5 years and 8 million emails a month later. Here's what I've learned.

1. CTA changes.

"Would you be interested in speaking about this 15 minutes next week?" versus "Any interest in this?" 

I don't think changing your CTA so slightly from one of these to the other has any meaningful change on success anymore. 

People either want what you're offering or they don't. The call to action is not the variable that moves the needle. 

2. Gaming email deliverability.

Trying to come up with some kind of warmup schedule or email deliverability hack that other people aren't doing. 

Obsessing over rotation patterns and trying to find the magic configuration. That's really more of the thing that wastes people's time. 

3. Finding free hacks instead of paying for tools.

I used to spend a lot of time trying to scrape data for free. The amount of time it would take me to do that was never worth it. 

Just pay for the thing that works. The hours you spend building a workaround cost more than the tool subscription.

### 36. 7445092760828723200

- URL: https://www.linkedin.com/posts/outboundphd_ive-seen-clients-with-a-08-positive-reply-activity-7445092760828723200-L69g
- Author: 🦾Eric Nowoslawski (@outboundphd)
- Posted: 2026-04-01T13:00:37.316Z

#### Verbatim Content

I've seen clients with a 0.8% positive reply rate printing money.

I've seen clients with a 4% positive reply rate struggling to stay afloat.

The difference? One was selling $80K contracts. The other was selling a $500 product.

Reply rate is a vanity metric if you don't connect it to revenue.

Here's what to calculate instead:

1. Size your TAM. How many companies actually fit your ICP? If it's 3,000, you'll burn through them in 2 months at scale. Plan accordingly.

2. Find your positive reply rate. Not total reply rate — positive replies divided by emails sent. That's your real conversion.

3. Multiply by deal value and close rate. One positive reply on an $80K deal with a 25% close rate is worth $20K. One positive reply on a $500 deal with a 20% close rate is worth $100.

Those are fundamentally different businesses that require fundamentally different outbound strategies.

The $80K business needs precision targeting and can afford to send fewer, better emails. The $500 business needs massive volume and tight unit economics to make cold email work at all.

Before your next campaign: how much is one positive reply actually worth to your business?

That number changes everything about how you run outbound.

### 37. 7447278288743337984

- URL: https://www.linkedin.com/posts/outboundphd_your-campaigns-have-been-running-for-two-activity-7447278288743337984-L_-9
- Author: 🦾Eric Nowoslawski (@outboundphd)
- Posted: 2026-04-07T13:45:07.776Z

#### Verbatim Content

Your campaigns have been running for two weeks. Reply rate is 0.3%. Zero positive responses.

Here are the next steps we take.

1. Peel back everything and only send to Google accounts.

Strip your list to Google Workspace, not Gmail, just Google Workspace. 

Right now Outlook is harder to get into the inbox, and we don't want to send to any Proofpoint, Microsoft, or other email inboxes. 

We want to isolate the variable. Your Google deliverability might be fine while Outlook is putting you straight to spam, and you'd never know if you're sending to both.

Check blacklists and run inbox placement tests while you're at it. Verify your domain setup. DKIM, SPF, and DMARC.

2. Hyper-segment the list.

Take your lead list and whittle it down to a thousand prospects as focused as you possibly can. Let the list become the message and add whatever filters you need to get them as close to your current customers as possible.

3. Increase testing velocity.

A lot of times we try to set up campaigns that will work long-term. You can't be thinking about long-term here. 

Keep the lead list tight and speed up the testing velocity.

Launch multiple campaigns with different angles. 

Pain-based versus value-based. 
Different CTAs. 
Different target personas. T

ry to make your email easier to say yes to. 

Include more lead magnets or free offers delivered by AI, competitive intelligence reports, anything that provides value before asking for a meeting.

4. Test campaigns that generate meeting-ready leads versus hand raisers.

If your direct ask isn't working, test an offer that generates hand raisers instead. Sometimes people aren't ready to book a meeting but will say yes to something valuable.

Don't add complexity. Remove it. Find the one thing that's broken.

### 38. 7441834926003548160

- URL: https://www.linkedin.com/posts/outboundphd_for-3-years-people-scared-me-away-from-using-activity-7441834926003548160-XAyh
- Author: 🦾Eric Nowoslawski (@outboundphd)
- Posted: 2026-03-23T13:15:08.966Z

#### Verbatim Content

For 3 years people scared me away from using images in cold emails.

"Images hurt deliverability."
"Images trigger spam filters."
"Images increase email size."

All true.

But here's the math nobody does.

Let's say plain text gets you 80% inbox placement and 1% reply rate on 10,000 emails.

80% x 10,000 x 1% = 80 replies.

Now let's say adding an image drops inbox placement to 70%. But the image — a screenshot, a chart, a personalized mockup — increases reply rate to 1.8% among people who see it.

70% x 10,000 x 1.8% = 126 replies.

That's 57% more replies with WORSE deliverability.

The deliverability crowd will tell you the first scenario is better because your domain health stays cleaner long-term. And they're right. Probably. Maybe.

But the revenue crowd will tell you 126 replies beats 80 replies every day of the week. And they're also right.

Here's where I've seen images work:

1. Screenshots of the prospect's website with a specific callout
2. A before/after chart relevant to their industry
3. A Loom thumbnail (not embedded video, just the preview image with a play button)
4. A simple product screenshot that answers "what does this actually look like"

Here's where images don't work:

1. Stock photos
2. Marketing banners
3. Your company logo
4. Anything that looks like a newsletter

The real answer, like everything in cold email: test it.

Run 5,000 with the image. Run 5,000 without. Measure replies, not inbox placement.

Worse deliverability x higher conversion can absolutely beat perfect deliverability x mediocre conversion.

### 40. 7441914176207462400

- URL: https://www.linkedin.com/posts/outboundphd_most-people-use-linkedin-outbound-wrong-activity-7441914176207462400-_lXY
- Author: 🦾Eric Nowoslawski (@outboundphd)
- Posted: 2026-03-23T18:30:03.687Z

#### Verbatim Content

Most people use LinkedIn outbound wrong.

Where people are getting it wrong is they think: email somebody, then connect with them on LinkedIn the next day, then follow-up email, then LinkedIn message. 

That sounds great in theory. But the math just doesn't work out. 

The volume you can send on email versus connection requests you can send on LinkedIn is completely different.

Instead, we want to create an order of operations where we're reaching out to the most engaged, highest likelihood to reply leads first.

1. Engaged leads from your other channels. Automate connecting and reaching out to any person who is an engaged lead, inbound forms, cold email replies, ad responders, whatever. 

These people already showed interest somewhere. LinkedIn is your follow-up.

2. De-anonymized website visitors. Anybody visiting your website who was identified with your favorite de-anonymization platform like Snitcher or Visual Visitor. 

They were looking at you. Now go find them.

3. People in your ICP engaging with your current customers. As long as you have marketing rights, reach out and mention that you work with that company. 

When you name-drop the case study, they know exactly who you're talking about.

4. Your competitors' LinkedIn engagement. Connect with and message people commenting on and reacting to your competitors' posts. These people care about the category. They're already educated.

5. Your golden ICP. After all of the above, then go cold to your best-fit prospects.

By the time you go through these five tiers, it's going to be another month gone by, and you have more engagers to reach out to, more website visitors, more competitor activity to scrape. The cycle repeats.

This is a much better strategy than blasting your connection requests at a cold list.

### 41. 7442563490382274560

- URL: https://www.linkedin.com/posts/outboundphd_your-lead-lists-have-more-garbage-in-them-activity-7442563490382274560-UZrK
- Author: 🦾Eric Nowoslawski (@outboundphd)
- Posted: 2026-03-25T13:30:12.250Z

#### Verbatim Content

Your lead lists have more garbage in them than you think. And here's how you can work on fixing it.

The problem comes down to three things:

1. The data is scraped from self-reported data that people mislabel.

Companies are notoriously bad at putting the correct industries on LinkedIn. Someone marks themselves as "Information Technology" when they're really a logistics company with a website. You need to look at their actual websites and company descriptions and use AI to verify fit — not just trust the label.

2. The data is possibly outdated.

You need to figure out when the data was last updated from your provider. If it's stale, you need to either scrape Google for current information or get a more expensive data provider that will pull the data live. That "150-person startup" might have gone through layoffs and now has 12 people. Or got acquired.

3. Data providers can't predict every use case.

What counts as a "B2B SaaS company" versus an "e-commerce company"? Some people say e-commerce is any company with a Shopify store. But Biden for America was built on Shopify. Your definition of ICP doesn't match the category labels that data providers use.

You have to build a layer on top of their data to work with this.

Here's how we fix it:

For the self-reported data problem, use AI to look at websites and company descriptions to make a real fit determination. Show AI what 30 good companies look like, then keep editing your prompt until it gets all 30 correct. Then run it on everything.

For outdated data, check when it was last updated. If it falls outside your range, scrape or pay for live data.

For the use case problem - use Claude Code or Codex with the batch API to throw as much AI inference at it as possible. Rent GPUs if you have to. This is a problem that AI has made solvable for the first time.

The data will never be perfect. But you can get a lot closer than most people think.

### 42. 7444737803386650625

- URL: https://www.linkedin.com/posts/outboundphd_heres-a-strategy-to-turn-your-lead-list-activity-7444737803386650625-SKYb
- Author: 🦾Eric Nowoslawski (@outboundphd)
- Posted: 2026-03-31T13:30:08.867Z

#### Verbatim Content

Here's a strategy to turn your lead list into five or more experiments with Claude Code and Clay.

One thing we think about a lot with our customers is how the list is the message. I don't know who said that first, but it definitely wasn't me, so credit to whoever coined it.

What we mean is this: if you know you can sell to any founder with less than 20 employees, that's a pretty big list. 

But if you know the best founder for you is someone who has founded a company before, has been in business less than two years, has 10 to 20 employees, and has no accounting or finance job titles on their LinkedIn company page...

That person should be receiving a completely different message than someone who has one or two of those traits but not all of them.

We use Claude Code or Clay to enrich our lists for any criteria that might change how we'd message somebody.

For example, if somebody has been in business for two years, they're just getting systems ramped up. That's a way different message than somebody who's been in business for 10 years and has figured all this out already. 

If somebody is a CEO versus a founder, and there's no reason to change your message, then there's no reason to separate them in the list.

We separate the list by what data points change our messaging.

This way we can slice and dice our data and have different email campaigns for multiple layers of ICP fit. Sometimes your golden ICP (where everything is true about that person) might only have a thousand leads in the entire campaign. 

But I guarantee you'll get a higher response rate on those leads than on your broader ones, because you can niche your messaging down more and more.

The list is the message. Use that when creating your campaigns.

### 43. 7442922057081880578

- URL: https://www.linkedin.com/posts/outboundphd_ai-is-not-going-to-fix-your-cold-email-campaign-activity-7442922057081880578-PWaj
- Author: 🦾Eric Nowoslawski (@outboundphd)
- Posted: 2026-03-26T13:15:01.213Z

#### Verbatim Content

AI is not going to fix your cold email campaign.

I know that's weird coming from a guy who uses Clay and Claude Code for basically everything. We run AI personalization, AI list building, AI copy generation, AI analytics.

But here's the thing.

AI makes good campaigns better. It does not make bad campaigns good.

If your offer doesn't work, AI-personalized first lines just mean people read a more relevant email about something they still don't want.

If your targeting is wrong, AI enrichment just gives you more detailed data about the wrong people.

Cold email works when you have a large enough TAM to make it work, a clear problem with a clear solution, a buyer who can say yes without feeling like they have to rip and replace huge systems, and a deal size that justifies the cost per meeting.

Cold email struggles when your target market is too small, your deal size is too low, or you are in a commoditized industry with no specialization in your offer.

AI amplifies whatever you feed it. Feed it a strong offer and good targeting, and it works well. Feed it a weak offer and bad targeting, and it just burns through your domains faster.

Fix the fundamentals first.

### 46. 7447633136752066560

- URL: https://www.linkedin.com/posts/outboundphd_last-quarter-a-client-asked-me-which-intent-activity-7447633136752066560-kuK9
- Author: 🦾Eric Nowoslawski (@outboundphd)
- Posted: 2026-04-08T13:15:10.134Z

#### Verbatim Content

Last quarter a client asked me which intent signal they should use. Hiring data? Funding? Website visits?

I told them to use all of them, but not how they expected.

Most people pick one signal, build a campaign around it, and pray. That's backwards. You don't know which signal predicts a response until you let the data tell you.

Here's the framework we use across 50+ clients.

Run 3 campaigns at once:

1. Entire TAM. No signals. Just firmographics. This is your baseline — and sometimes it wins.

2. Focused segments. One signal per campaign. Hiring SDRs. Recently funded. Using a competitor tool.

3. Trigger-based. Real-time events. Job posting today. Funding this week. Website visit yesterday.

Now here's the move nobody talks about.

Every positive response across all 3, take that company and run it through every signal source you have. Pull hiring data, tech stack, funding history, news, social activity. Everything.

Look for patterns.

Maybe the companies that respond all hired a VP of Sales in the last 90 days. Maybe they all use HubSpot. Maybe they all posted about a specific pain point on LinkedIn.

You wouldn't have found that by guessing upfront.

We also run a catchall workflow in Clay with  Clayagent, every signal we can think of, just "what's notable about this company right now?" Sometimes the signal that predicts responses is something you'd never think to look for.

Sometimes you genuinely don't know WHY someone responded. That's fine. Not every response has a readable signal. But the ones that do give you your next 10 campaigns.

Stop picking signals from a menu. Let the data tell you what works.
