### 48. 7440747725953880064

- URL: https://www.linkedin.com/posts/outboundphd_heres-an-email-that-broke-all-the-rules-activity-7440747725953880064-P9fI
- Author: 🦾Eric Nowoslawski (@outboundphd)
- Posted: 2026-03-20T13:15:00.275Z

#### Verbatim Content

Here's an email that broke all the rules and outperformed two years of testing.

We had a campaign we'd been split testing for two years across multiple clients in the same industry. We thought we had nailed the playbook. Tens of iterations of very similar things, and we had found the thing that was performing the best.

A new customer in the same industry joined and said, "Yeah, I know you're running that for other people, but I have my email that I think would work better."

We said, "Hey, we should split test this because we've been running this for a really long time. Show us your email."

He sent us an email that was five paragraphs long. Not five short paragraphs with one sentence each. Literally three to four sentences per paragraph.

I like to joke with him that he is sending love letters to these companies.

When we sent it, it absolutely blew our test out of the water.

Here's why I think it worked. He's competing with much larger companies in his space. Those companies are sending stone-cold emails with no feeling in them. 

His email, every single sentence was different. It felt like a real person who genuinely cared about the problem.

I think David Ogilvy once said that your first line sells the second line and your second line sells the third line.

When we say in cold email that every email should be under 100 words, it's not so much because it must. It's because we have nothing better to say.

His email had something better to say in every sentence.

Even "gurus" like me that are trying to share best practices — anything that you come up with as a test is probably always worth testing if it's easily reversible.

What's the most surprising test result you've ever seen?

### 49. 7445821159532679168

- URL: https://www.linkedin.com/posts/outboundphd_if-there-are-first-time-founders-out-there-activity-7445821159532679168-J76Y
- Author: 🦾Eric Nowoslawski (@outboundphd)
- Posted: 2026-04-03T13:15:01.100Z

#### Verbatim Content

If there are first-time founders out there that need to start cold email for zero dollars, this is how I would do it.

There are four problems you need to solve: deliverability, list building, copywriting, and sending. Let's take each as a checklist.

**1. Deliverability.**
It's not best practice, and I wouldn't even say it comes across as professional, but if you want to do cold email for zero dollars, sending from your personal email address will do the trick until you close enough deals to invest. Your personal email likely has years of reputation on it, and it will work fine for the low-volume use case we're going to do here.

**2. List building.**
You need a way to build lists and store them. You could save files locally and use the free tiers of Codex or Claude Code to do research and find 50 contacts to reach out to. If you need their emails, most email platforms have great free trials you could work between. Start a Prospeo free trial, for instance.

3. Copywriting.
Any ChatGPT or Claude instance can help you get started for free. I would suggest creating cold emails in the style of Josh Braun if you really don't know what to do here. His frameworks are simple and they work.

4. Sending.
A new repo was just dropped that controls your browser, and you could use the "send later" function inside of Gmail to do the sending. It cuts a couple corners here and there, but if you have absolutely no money to get started, this is how you'd be able to do it.

Now here's the progression when you're ready to invest:

$50/month: Smartlead or Instantly for sending + scheduling. They have built-in lead databases.

$200/month: Add 3-5 domains and inboxes. Buy a list from Prospeo or Clay. Use Million Verifier to clean it.

$500/month: I'd just invest in more domains and inboxes at this level. A/B testing across segments.

$2,000+/month: Waterfall enrichment. Intent signals. 50+ domains. Multiple campaigns. This is where agencies like us operate.

Start sending. Upgrade later.

### 50. 7440385354169204736

- URL: https://www.linkedin.com/posts/outboundphd_heres-something-nobody-talks-about-in-cold-activity-7440385354169204736-4w5f
- Author: 🦾Eric Nowoslawski (@outboundphd)
- Posted: 2026-03-19T13:15:04.109Z

#### Verbatim Content

Here's something nobody talks about in cold email deliverability.

Your offer quality directly affects whether you land in the inbox.

Not indirectly. Directly.

Here's the cycle:

Bad offer → nobody replies → email providers see zero engagement → they assume you're spam → more emails go to junk → even fewer replies → inboxes burn faster → you buy more domains → those burn too.

Now flip it:

Good offer → people reply → email providers see real engagement → you stay in the inbox → more people see your emails → more replies → inboxes last longer → you need fewer domains → your costs drop.

This is why two agencies can run the exact same infrastructure 

Same warmup tool, same sending volume, same ESP, same domain setup 

But get completely different deliverability results.

One has offers people respond to. One doesn't.

We manage deliverability across 50+ clients at GrowthEngineX. Same Smartlead setup, same inboxes, same warmup schedules. The clients with the best deliverability are not the ones with the fanciest technical setup.

They're the ones with the best offers.

Every time someone asks me "how do I fix my deliverability" my first question is "what's your reply rate?"

If it's under 1%, your deliverability problem might be an offer problem.

Fix the offer and watch the technical metrics fix themselves.

It's a virtuous cycle when it's working. And a death spiral when it's not.

### 51. 7440022995848335361

- URL: https://www.linkedin.com/posts/outboundphd_if-youre-thinking-about-using-cold-email-activity-7440022995848335361-U2j1
- Author: 🦾Eric Nowoslawski (@outboundphd)
- Posted: 2026-03-18T13:15:11.153Z

#### Verbatim Content

If you're thinking about using cold email because you think it's another touch that will increase the ROI on other channels, you're looking at it the wrong way.

I've seen this play out dozens of times.

Early on in my career, companies would work with us and say, "Hey, even if we don't get replies, at least we're getting our name in front of them."

I used to say, "OK sure, we can do that." Send them the invoice. They'd pay and then cancel the next month because they weren't seeing any real attribution.

Here's the thing: cold email doesn't damage your brand (probably a whole post can be made about this) but it also doesn't build your brand.

I always ask people this: what was the most recent cold email that you received an hour ago?

Nobody can remember. At all.

Cold email doesn't have as many risks as people might believe to damage your brand as long as you follow the rules and follow best practices. 

Don't ignore unsubscribe requests. Don't follow up 12 times. Don't email people about products they literally built.

But on the flip side, I'm also very confident that it doesn't positively increase your brand either.

It's one of the only marketing styles that is really direct response and doesn't leave lasting impressions on people. For better or worse.

That's just my experience with it after 5 years and 8 million emails a month.

If you want brand awareness, invest in content and community. If you want pipeline, use cold email. They're different tools for different jobs.

### 52. 7439724743894728704

- URL: https://www.linkedin.com/posts/outboundphd_andrej-karpathy-dropped-this-github-repo-activity-7439724743894728704-jQk_
- Author: 🦾Eric Nowoslawski (@outboundphd)
- Posted: 2026-03-17T17:30:02.346Z

#### Verbatim Content

Andrej Karpathy dropped this github repo about a week ago and I think it has great use cases for GTM. He called it autoresearch.

The very idea of it is exciting enough for me to share and I just started getting some tests going.

The idea (like ralph wiggum loops) is actually quite simple. He built a repo that can keep track of baseline, come up with new experiments, evaluate a metric, and then iterate on any time frame you'd like. 

Kind of like giving machine learning capabilities to anyone with a laptop. 

The key to making it work for you is to give it access and the metric you want to optimize for. 

In cold outbound, I can see this being used for a couple use cases like

1. Directly editing copywriting with boundaries for your cold email campaigns optimizing for reply rate.

2. Improving lead scoring prompts where you manually review a dataset and then it keeps improving the prompt until it matches your results.

3. Optimizing long running workflows for speed (maybe a personal gripe with claude code is I have a hard time getting it to ramp up to my real API rate limits. Maybe I'm dumb"

I thought it was cool so I left the repo link here. 

https://lnkd.in/ePSEfCib

If you don't know how to use this for yourself, I think you have two options.

1. if you have already been building with claude code, ask it what you could use this for based on what you have already built.

2. if you haven't been building, ask it to review the calls you've had in the last 2 weeks and look at your workflow for things that can be optimized.

### 53. 7439657184575033347

- URL: https://www.linkedin.com/posts/outboundphd_most-people-use-job-posting-signals-wrong-activity-7439657184575033347-GqUo
- Author: 🦾Eric Nowoslawski (@outboundphd)
- Posted: 2026-03-17T13:01:34.949Z

#### Verbatim Content

Most people use job posting signals wrong.

They see a company hiring SDRs and email them: "Hey, instead of hiring SDRs, you should use our sales automation tool."

That's the worst way to use this signal.

Here's a better way.

If the job description says the mission is to grow enterprise logos, you don't pitch your product against the hire. You say: "I saw in the job description your mission is to grow enterprise logos. Here's how we'd specifically help with growing enterprise logos."

The signal isn't bad. How you use it is what matters.

You can use Claygent, Exa, Parallel Web Systems, Perplexity Computer, etc to get the job descriptions that are posted from the website.

Use Clay to get the open jobs from their database

OR use an Apify scraper to get Glassdoor/Indeed Data

Find the company domain if it isn't included and use that to target your decision makers. 

I like to find contacts in Clay or the Prospeo API. 

Then you can be off to the races.

### 54. 7437589072501657600

- URL: https://www.linkedin.com/posts/outboundphd_why-im-excited-not-scared-about-clays-activity-7437589072501657600-uStC
- Author: 🦾Eric Nowoslawski (@outboundphd)
- Posted: 2026-03-11T20:03:38.607Z

#### Verbatim Content

Why I'm excited (not scared) about Clay's new pricing update from today. And keep in mind, I'm their largest user.

I've had the privilege of meeting with Karan Parekh and Zona (Xiaotian) Zhang about this change once a month for the last 6 months and a lot of thought has gone into it. 

First, why the need for change?

There are many reasons but the biggest I was pushing for is that Clay's pricing on data is not competitive with the rest of the market if you want to use Clay credits. 

Finding emails could be up to .10 or more in a world where a competitive price is 1 cent and if you really know what you're doing, it can quite literally get 10-100x cheaper.

Clay had no way to be able to bring those savings to their customers. 

With today's pricing update, enriching data on the platform is more in line with where the cost of data is going.

Second, charging by actions is how Clay actually gives value to their customers and the action credits for each plan are set that 90% of users in each tier would never even think about their action credits. 

With the change, I wanted to see the data first hand that most customers would actually save money. 

Call me a shill. Believe me, don't believe me. I've seen the user data that most customers will not get close to their usage limit and because their data cost is going down 50-90%, they will probably save money.

Clay self admits in their announcement that they even forecast their own revenue to go down from this. 

Third, you can stay on your plan with no changes to pricing. This was also important in our discussions that the current customers can choose to not be affected. 

Varun Anand has told me directly that there are no current plans to change the legacy plans so current customers can just stay there. 

He has said it's on the clay team to build more features to convince legacy customers to move. 

Everyone here knows my love for the company so take my word with a grain of salt but after seeing all the data first hand, I think this will be a net benefit for most customers that aren't ramming API keys all day and put Clay in a better position to be more competitive in the long term.

### 55. 7426999649573515265

- URL: https://www.linkedin.com/posts/outboundphd_were-cutting-our-ai-token-spend-by-over-activity-7426999649573515265-9z9t
- Author: 🦾Eric Nowoslawski (@outboundphd)
- Posted: 2026-02-10T14:45:03.439Z

#### Verbatim Content

We're cutting our AI token spend by over 90% with one platform. Taking screenshots of technical websites is all you need to know how to do.

Vast(.)ai has made it really easy to upload any open source model (I went with OSS 120B and OSS 20B) and we are calling it with the API. 

On one GPU that I can rent for less than 75 cents per hour, I cranked it up to 16 requests per second with my test prompts that had about 10,000 input tokens in them. 

At these rates, 90% is actually an underestimate. It's probably approaching 95-99%. 

I can't even give you a tutorial because I literally just asked ChatGPT how to set it up and took screenshots along the way when I had issues so here's some use cases we are using it for. 

1. Finding the business owner of a local business. They don't often have linkedin profiles but will tell their story on their page. Basically free to go 2-3 levels deep on the website and get the content for OSS 120B to find the owner's name. 

2. ICP classification of companies. 

We can make the prompt as long as we want with as much input as we want in order to get the best results.

3. Org Chart Mapping

Lots of data can be passed in to the AI with the JSON of linkedin profiles and connections can be drawn by department, location, and self reported team affiliation on their profile.

4. Finding the right target to reach out to. 

Now you can get every contact in a department and pick the best one in order of your preferences. 

5. Keep going further than ICP Classification and get all of the content from websites and learn anything about your prospect's public facing content. 

With basically unlimited AI throughput, it feels that almost anything is possible now.

### 56. 7424817766785785857

- URL: https://www.linkedin.com/posts/outboundphd_this-claude-code-clay-1-2-punch-is-going-activity-7424817766785785857-wvB5
- Author: 🦾Eric Nowoslawski (@outboundphd)
- Posted: 2026-02-04T14:15:02.045Z

#### Verbatim Content

This Claude code / Clay 1-2 punch is going to make your message writing, research, ICP fit, and claygent prompts so much better. 

I'm like many other folks, jumping on the Claude code bandwagon, but one thing I can't see myself changing any time soon is the enrichment and final mile deployment of our campaigns to be done in Clay. 

One of my favorite combinations of these tools is writing AI prompts. 

If the data needs to be enriched in Clay, we will get the data there first and download it as a csv. If we are getting it via an API we might do it locally.

Either way, the ability to have Claude code bring up my Prompt writing skill that already knows what I'm looking for from past prompts, then we can write a prompt for what we need, see the data, use Wispr Flow to review and add

1) what would we want the output to be given what we see in front of us
2) based on what we want this output to be, Claude code can change the prompt for us
3) it adds more examples to our prompts

So instead of clicking in Clay and looking back and forth and click, click, click, clicking my life away. I just talk to it and it writes the prompts for me. 

Then we are done, I have a library of prompts that we've made that I update if we haven't used this one before so I can keep a backlog of prompts we've already made. We also use that session to update our skill. 

Then we can get an output of those prompts (optimized for cached inputs of course) and throw them in Clay to be ran as workflows and we didn't have to keep creating them and tweaking them manually. 

Enjoy this silly remotion animation of the prompts getting faster and faster.

### 58. 7425180180253478913

- URL: https://www.linkedin.com/posts/outboundphd_we-are-starting-to-think-more-about-our-cold-activity-7425180180253478913-OMsj
- Author: 🦾Eric Nowoslawski (@outboundphd)
- Posted: 2026-02-05T14:15:08.149Z

#### Verbatim Content

We are starting to think more about our cold email experimentation as Facebook ads internally. Experiments are far easier to run than even 4 months ago and we want to push more. 

Meta released data that the companies that ran the most Ad experiments were also the most successful. 

Cold Email and Meta ads are similar in the way that whole creative choices can be swapped out very easily. Subject lines can be split tested. CTAs challenged. First lines with 6 variations. 

All very easy to do. 

But orchestrating the audiences is a massive time suck vs. Meta ads. (Not to downplay the audience creation on Meta. Of course that still takes a lot of work). 

But to pull a list, validate it, get all of it segmented in Clay. "If they qualify for signal 1, they go to campaign 1. If they qualify for signal 2 campaign 2. If they are ICP but no signal, round robin between these 6 campaigns"

It turns into a nightmare keeping track of it all. All for tests that might not work. 

But now with agentic coding, all of this orchestration can be done with your voice and a couple plan sessions to make sure you thought of all the logic. 

It can then spit out a visual plan of the if/then statements that you want and the orchestration and you can let it rip. 

Then when something is working, you double down and build it in Clay.

### 59. 7424455384360206336

- URL: https://www.linkedin.com/posts/outboundphd_sometimes-we-have-some-agentic-research-that-activity-7424455384360206336-U6OZ
- Author: 🦾Eric Nowoslawski (@outboundphd)
- Posted: 2026-02-03T14:15:03.342Z

#### Verbatim Content

Sometimes we have some Agentic research that run up our Claygent bill. I swapped it out for an open source alternative and so can you. 

Note: This is way way harder to set up than Claygent and your time is not worth the investment here to replace it in most situations. We have a customer we have been working with for a year that needs to know this data about 2M companies so we decided to make the switch but only because it's been so long. 

Basically, this customer needs to know the pricing of the company they are going to reach out to. 

Do they have tiers of pricing?
What's their lowest and most expensive plan?
Do they not mention pricing?
Do they not have a pricing page?
Do they have enterprise pricing? 

All of these questions don't disqualify anyone from their outreach, it just changes the messaging. 

So we broke the workflow down into it's individual pieces. 

1) we should check if the website's site map has anything with "pricing" right after the root domain. 
2) Use OpenWeb Ninja to do a google search for their pricing
3) If page is found via keyword match or google search, mark it down and analyze with OSS 20B hosted on Vast.ai (maybe a whole other post to get into)

If this doesn't work, the Claygent still takes one more crack at it. 

If you ask Cursor or Claude code to build you something similar, mine is telling me it used these python libraries to get this done and you can probably take this post and ask Claude who to build it yourself too. 

Currently scaling this on trigger.dev but might be using too much of my concurrency, thinking of moving it to Cloudflare workers. Totally open to recommendations from anyone on this one but it totally works locally and on trigger!

Python Libraries it's using (also open to suggestions here as it picked these itself). Can't scrape javascript sites but most sites aren't blocking us at all. 

xml.etree for the site map
Trafilatura for the markdown etc.

### 61. 7426626267451588608

- URL: https://www.linkedin.com/posts/outboundphd_a-3-tier-tech-stack-to-build-lists-of-your-activity-7426626267451588608-yQ4k
- Author: 🦾Eric Nowoslawski (@outboundphd)
- Posted: 2026-02-09T14:01:22.204Z

#### Verbatim Content

A 3 tier tech stack to build lists of your TAM from beginner to advanced to build niche lists and lists over 100,000 contacts. 

To build a 100,000+ contact database without manually searching anything, you need three things:

1) A daily driver that's your go to
2) A deep TAM builder
3) An always-running background scraper to custom find your particular data. 

Here’s how I’d approach each one 👇

Tier 1: Prospeo.io (Daily Driver)

Customer asks for a list?

First thing we do is hit the Prospeo API.

Results are nearly identical to LinkedIn

The API is stupid easy to work with

This handles ~80% of requests without touching anything else.

Fast. Cheap. Reliable.

Tier 2: Internal Company Database (or just use Clay) + Contact Waterfall

This is for when you need the full TAM and you need deeper company filters. 

We maintain a Supabase database with every company in the US enriched through Clay.

Clay gives us AI-derived fields most people don’t even realize exist:

Do they make subscription revenue?
Are they B2B?
What sub-industry are they in?
Local company or national?

Once we have the expanded list of companies, we run them through Prospeo, Clay People Source, Blitz API and Smartlead's new contact finder to get all contacts. 

Tier 3: Sales Navigator/Google Maps Scraper (Autopilot)

This is the long-term edge.
We run a Sales Navigator scraper via RapidAPI with cron jobs firing every single morning.

75,000 companies/day
75,000 contacts/day

Dedupes on website or LinkedIn URL

Focused on:
Newly founded companies (2024–2026)
Newly created roles
Accounts posting in the last 90 days

Most people use these tools when they have a project.

We run them constantly in the background.

New companies.
New people.
Every single day.

By the time a customer asks for more contacts, we already have them.

At this point, you’re about 80% of the way to a TAM that many agencies would happily charge $10k+ to build. And you did it without manual searching.

### 62. 7406694642030911488

- URL: https://www.linkedin.com/posts/outboundphd_heres-an-example-of-an-email-that-i-was-activity-7406694642030911488-9ays
- Author: 🦾Eric Nowoslawski (@outboundphd)
- Posted: 2025-12-16T14:00:12.392Z

#### Verbatim Content

Here's an example of an email that I was particularly proud of, but I have to admit, didn't generate enough leads and we had to shut it down. 

This is one of those situations where we did a lot of split testing and the messaging was what I would send if. I were to send the message manually and it didn't hit. 

Maybe it was the segment (they were targeting large enterprise companies). I wish it did better. 

This company helps with software license management. 

Someone leaves - automatically get them off your plans so you can keep your data secure and save money. They also can analyze who is using what software so that you can save on licenses too but I thought bundling security with money saving would work better. 

Maybe I was wrong.

So for this play, I wanted to show that we knew that someone recently left the company. 

You can do this in Clay by finding people that recently started a new role and then look at their past experience to find if their past experience matches your ICP and then target your ICP decision makers from there. 

That way I could make line 1 of this email happen. 

Then, I also wanted to give them real examples of what they could save on so we used BuiltWith and open job descriptions that mention technology. 

We were looking for seat based technologies. 

Some people might use AI for this but we actually just used AI to make a huge list of seat based pricing tech and then used if/then statements to see if the keywords were mentioned so we didn't have to keep paying for the AI to review them and possibly be wrong. 

Also, anytime you are trying to show how smart you are by telling a prospect something you researched, always cite your source so your source is wrong, you aren't wrong.

It might have been a mistake to bundle security value prop with expense management but I promise we were testing them on their own as well. 

You'll also see that the p.s. line comes in from left field talking about having negotiating leverage which was a smaller value prop for them but I really wanted to make sure that anyone that saw this message really saw everything we had.

The message in my opinion was pretty sophisticated but not sophisticated enough or not juicy enough for that enterprise buyer segment.

### 64. 7407075858546438144

- URL: https://www.linkedin.com/posts/outboundphd_controversial-opinion-every-agency-should-activity-7407075858546438144-VpRU
- Author: 🦾Eric Nowoslawski (@outboundphd)
- Posted: 2025-12-17T15:15:01.492Z

#### Verbatim Content

Controversial opinion: Every agency should be using Supabase (or similar) to cache data.

What we're doing:
→ Every email we find gets saved
→ Before buying new data, we check our cache
→ Set refresh rules: 30 days, 90 days, 180 days—your choice

The result after a few months:
- 23M+ valid emails cached
- Constantly requerying instead of repurchasing
- Massive cost savings

"But I'm not technical."

You don't need to be.

Cursor will:
→ Set up your schema
→ Create indexes
→ Handle the queries

Seriously, start caching your data. Future you will thank present you.

### 65. 7425542541015478274

- URL: https://www.linkedin.com/posts/outboundphd_in-2025-we-launched-1517-campaigns-and-activity-7425542541015478274--VIO
- Author: 🦾Eric Nowoslawski (@outboundphd)
- Posted: 2026-02-06T14:15:01.687Z

#### Verbatim Content

In 2025, we launched 1,517 campaigns and generated over 60k positive responses across all of our customers. Here's 9 things we learned from the data. 

We had an AI analyze the offer, the leads in the campaign, and we made an AI summary. 

I threw 30 Claude sub agents at the database to answer a bunch of questions and here's what we learned. 

1. Our success metrics and average success of a campaign looks way different than it was 3 years ago. 

3 years ago, I said a campaign was successful and should be scaled if it was getting 1 positive response for every 300 people you reach out to. 

The numbers have moved for sure. I asked Claude to put our email response into quintiles and the results are in the screenshot. 

2. 70-90 words was optimal for email length

3. 2 Steps in an email sequence is ideal. 3 is ok. Anything over 3, HUGE reply drop off

4. For subject lines, including first name correlated with higher reply rates. I think this goes against 💜 🔮 Will Allred's data so I publish this at my peril. 

5. Monday - Thursday BEST reply days. Claude was saying we shouldn't even send on Fridays. Not sure we will make that leap but might be a good tip for those of you with a small market.

6. Heavy Spintax actually hurt some of our campaigns. It found lighter spintax did better. I think this has something to do with keeping control of the messaging with less variation

7. Industries that performed best for us - SaaS, Schools, Restaurants, E-commerce. This is for sure skewed by our data. We just have a lot of people targeting SaaS and then the people targeting Schools and Restaurants have crazy offers. 

8. Naming a case study actually hurt performance vs. just talking about outcomes. Definitely something I'm going to be keeping in mind. 

9. Generic AI personalization killed reply rates. 

I wanted to dig deeper into this one to see what it meant. We have naming conventions for our custom variables that match some templates we have in Clay so after manually reviewing, I could eye ball it and know what it meant. 

The generic AI that killed our reply rates was this trite, "Hey Eric, I saw you help people send cold email and I had a question." type stuff. 

Half way through the year, Taylor Haren even told me he thinks those are garbage and we reviewed mid year and I agreed

This was the AI that it said was garbage too. Any custom variable that was not in our template library and some custom template ones all correlated with higher response rates. 

I still have all the data. If you could ask a question about the success or failure of our campaigns, what would you ask it? 

DISCLAIMER: this is based on our internal data and might be skewed because of the customers we work with one way or another. If you have a question of your own campaigns, I always recommend not listening to people like me and just doing your own testing.

### 66. 7404890239984627712

- URL: https://www.linkedin.com/posts/outboundphd_another-fun-campaign-from-the-gex-archives-activity-7404890239984627712-J1YE
- Author: 🦾Eric Nowoslawski (@outboundphd)

Another fun campaign from the GEX archives. this one helped land a $300k deal. 

This customer was one of the first to make AI phone support possible for ecommerce brands and this was the winning campaign. 

We started off using Claygent to find:

If they had a customer support phone number
What the phone number was
Did they list their customer support hours or were they 24/7

So we could send an email like below. 

The other shout out to make here is leaning into the language that is relevant to the prospect.

Most people don't know this but WISMO stands for "Where is my order?"

As you can imagine, this is what most customer support teams deal with over the phone even though the tracking numbers are so easy to use and takes a ton of the team's bandwidth. 

Being able to handle these was a huge value prop for these brands. 

Ending with a P.S. about how we could customize it for them also turned into a nice touch. 

We get that information actually just from the home page and scraping with ZenRows!

### 67. 7425251899655901187

- URL: https://www.linkedin.com/posts/outboundphd_here-are-the-5-types-of-list-building-that-activity-7425251899655901187-zxXg
- Author: 🦾Eric Nowoslawski (@outboundphd)
- Posted: 2026-02-05T19:00:07.387Z

#### Verbatim Content

Here are the 5 types of list building that come up in our agency and the tools we use to build them so hopefully you can get some ideas for your own niche lists. 

We get a lot of different list requests and even though they all sound different and have mini requests, I believe they can all be built in these 5 buckets. 

1) Standard Industry and Contact Filtering

Great when your target market has a great presence on LinkedIn or Google Maps and they self report their industries well. Still requires some keyword clean up and AI Fit scoring but 80% of people's lists can be accomplished this way. 

Prospeo.io recently released their database and is our daily driver for this because of their API. I now have a prospeo skill that I use that just queries whatever I want and helps me improve my searches. We can get the whole search and store it in supabase for usage later. 

2) Lookalikes

When standard filtering isn't going to be quite good enough and your customers are all within a niche. For instance, finding a list of fintech companies is a great use case to use a lookalike tool. 

Ocean.io and DiscoLike are the go-tos here. 

Discolike's recent case study on how Anthropic uses their tool was very compelling. 

3) Google Maps Scraping 

Great for local businesses or other companies that don't have a huge LinkedIn presence. 

We built our own internally with a rapid api endpoint and what Nico Marino is doing with gtme.business looks very interesting. 

4) Custom Scraping

Great for when an event, niche directory, facebook group, government database holds all of the gold you need. 

Instant Data Scraper is the gateway drug of scraping chrome extensions for this. Browser automation and tools like Browser Use are making this even easier. 

5) Exa / Parallel Web Systems Custom Agent Searching

I made a post on this earlier this week of how you can start with your seed list of current good fit customers and use these tools to create agents that go find another 10 that look like your current customers, run another AI to make sure they actually fit, save them, find another 10 lookalikes, and so on. 

Great for really niche lists under 10k (probably even better when it's so niche your list is under 5k companies too). 

Any other list building types I might be missing?

### 68. 7424519555785400320

- URL: https://www.linkedin.com/posts/outboundphd_this-is-exactly-when-you-shouldnt-let-ai-activity-7424519555785400320-Oob_
- Author: 🦾Eric Nowoslawski (@outboundphd)
- Posted: 2026-02-03T18:30:03.002Z

#### Verbatim Content

This is exactly when you shouldn't let AI touch your campaign and using formulas in Clay wins instead. When you can segment your TAM into datapoints and make if/then messaging. 

In our onboarding form, we always collect information on desired signals and what they mean. 

This customer just said "running facebook ads" which of course brings up one important question.

If you were to manually reach out, what would you say to companies running a different amount of ads and how would you bucket them? 

This customer came back with this spread so every company running facebook ads got completely different messaging.

Offer was different, case study was different, first line was different, ps line was different. Everything. 

And you don't need AI to do this for you because it's free to have Clay create a formula

"If ad count is between 1-5, then say XYZ" etc. 

The most common use case for everyone would be in employee headcount targeting. 

I hear all the time "we target companies with 20-200 employees!" 

A 20 person company is a way different company than a 180 person company. What are the inflection points and buckets that you would change your messaging at? 

Works for a wide range of things. 

Recent LinkedIn posts
Google reviews
pages on a website
Technology installed on a website
total instagram posts
average instagram likes
etc
etc. 

Paraphrasing the great Freddie Mercury, "You can whatever you want with my [Emails], just never make [them] boring."

### 69. 7424093019735412736

- URL: https://www.linkedin.com/posts/outboundphd_i-think-most-people-are-wasting-their-linkedin-activity-7424093019735412736-4B8m
- Author: 🦾Eric Nowoslawski (@outboundphd)
- Posted: 2026-02-02T14:15:08.883Z

#### Verbatim Content

I think most people are wasting their LinkedIn outreach thinking of it like email instead of it's own channel. Here's the 3 strategies I'd do instead. 

I was speaking to James Winter about some suggestions I had for his portcos with Claude code and such and I brought up how one of their portcos wanted to sync LinkedIn outreach with email. 

"We think if we email them twice, we should move over to LinkedIn."

In my opinion, that's a waste of the channel. 

LinkedIn outreach (especially using voice notes) 99/100 times has higher reply rates than cold email but lacks the ability to scale without fake accounts. 

If you only had 25-50 connection requests to send per day, why would you use the same strategy of email where the channel scales linearly. 

When you're doing LinkedIn outreach, I'd focus on these things first. 

1) Use a tool like Trigify.io or some Rapid API endpoints to find your ICP engaging and connecting with customers you have permission to name drop. 

Find everyone engaging with your customer's employees posts and their company page.

Don't say, "I saw you engaging with my customer's post" but instead send your regular message you usually would but leave a custom variable in your social proof line of your message to reference that customer.

Instead of sending a message saying, "We help companies like {{company they have never heard of}}...." you can name drop that customer case study who they will of course know. 

2) Install every website deanonymization software on your site with a free trial. if they do contact lookup, clean them up and make sure they are ICP. If they do company lookup, use Clay or Prospeo.io to find contacts. 

Target people and companies with a high likelihood of being on your site. 

Again, don't mention anything about being on the site. Just reach out like you normally would and put a little delay on it. 

Track which tools give you the highest reply rates/success and pay for them. 

Probably a whole post can be dedicated on why I suggest you try them all for your niche but it's free, so trust me on this one. 

3) Reach out to people showing intent about your solution/problem/they are doing research/etc by targeting people in your ICP engaging with posts on the topic (or your competitor's post....) 

Same strategy here, just going a little bit more broad and using the last amount of capacity you'll have because of the limits of reaching out from 1 account. 

So instead of thinking about LinkedIn as an extension of email, remember you only get so many shots on goal and the reply rates are so much higher on LinkedIn. Use them to reach out to some of your hottest leads.

### 70. 7424893260273205249

- URL: https://www.linkedin.com/posts/outboundphd_3-times-in-the-last-month-we-had-a-niche-activity-7424893260273205249-Y-xa
- Author: 🦾Eric Nowoslawski (@outboundphd)
- Posted: 2026-02-04T19:15:01.095Z

#### Verbatim Content

3 times in the last month, we had a niche list we needed to build and a combination of Exa and Parallel Web Systems really came in handy. 

Lists that I was struggling to build with normal tools because the qualification they were looking for was high and they aren't on LinkedIn very much and don't have a google maps category. 

Here were some examples

- Funders of non-QM loans
- Luxury commercial security companies
- Newsletter companies (not just companies with a newsletter, they are media companies as a newsletter)

The funders might be on LinkedIn but you have to process a sea of Banking and Financial Services companies. 

There's no such thing as a luxury security company industry or Google maps listing and they don't have a LinkedIn presence anyway. 

Newsletter companies report themselves as being in whatever industry their newsletter is about and you can't just search for "newsletter" in their descriptions because so many regular companies have that as a CTA in their linkedin descriptions. 

So I fired up Claude code to run Exa and Parallel with some goals. 

I said, "here's a list of their customers that we know are good fits, find companies that look like them and create searches programmatically to find more. Dedupe along the way. Check in every 10 searches or so on budget. For this test, I'm willing to spend $20."

And then it got to work. 

Writing the prompt for what makes a company a good fit so I can approve.

Creating the programmatic searches like "non QM lender NYC" vs. "Los Angeles" 

Everything. 

Hit the budget so I could approve it. Made some tweaks, gave it a bigger budget and let it rock. 

I wouldn't suggest when you need to target a list you think is going to end being bigger than 10,000 but really good for helping build out really niche lists below that.

### 71. 7416845244027084800

- URL: https://www.linkedin.com/posts/outboundphd_i-built-a-way-to-monitor-your-customers-activity-7416845244027084800-La1h
- Author: 🦾Eric Nowoslawski (@outboundphd)
- Posted: 2026-01-13T14:15:04.497Z

#### Verbatim Content

I built a way to monitor your customer's LinkedIn content with a RapidAPI endpoint and Cursor so you can reach out to your ICP and name drop a customer they have heard of. Here's the step by step on how to do it.

I think one of the best ways that we can reach out to an ICP is if they already know of one of our successful case studies. 

You could ask your customer for a referral, but that puts work on them. 

You can monitor who they already know and reach out as if it's a regular message, and the prospect will actually have heard of the social proof you're mentioning. 

To set this up, all I did was give a voice note while walking my dog and Cursor took care of the rest. 

I hope you can even copy and paste this post and give some more context and set it up yourself. 

Step 1

Get the Rapid API endpoint. If you comment "monitor", I'll send it to you. Sorry to play this game but LinkedIn hates external links. 

Step 2

Use the MCP in RapidAPI and connect that to Cursor

Step 3

Explain what you want it to do. 

"Hey Cursor, I want to use this endpoint to build a micro app that I can enter a comma separated list of linkedin companies. I want you to 

1) find all of the posts of the linkedin companies
2) Find all of the people that work at the company and find their posts
3) Pull all of the people that have liked and commented on the posts and filter for XYZ titles and company size. 

Step 4

Add a little bit more to the prompt above about how you exactly want this to work.

Step 5

Enter Plan mode in Cursor. Drop your prompts and watch the plan

Step 6 

Edit/Approve the plan. I was lucky that Gemini Flash got this done in one shot, so I'm hoping I can pass this on and help others use it too. 

Again, comment "monitor" and I'll send it to you but if you don't want to wait for me, searching rapidapi(.)com(user)apibuilderz should help those that want to search on their own!

### 72. 7404161667007418368

- URL: https://www.linkedin.com/posts/outboundphd_this-campaign-was-split-tested-against-4-activity-7404161667007418368-Whbg
- Author: 🦾Eric Nowoslawski (@outboundphd)
- Posted: 2025-12-09T14:15:04.086Z

#### Verbatim Content

This campaign was split tested against 4 other tests and easily took the cake (while still sending images in Email 1). Here's how we made it happen. 

We were working with a company that can help impact-driven brands showcase the benefits of every purchase from their customers. 

We used Claygent to find a couple of things.

- Do they have a sustainability/mission page? Any company that we couldn't find one of these pages for were not allowed in the campaign.

- If yes, what's the mission and what's the link?

- We used screenshot(dot)net to then take a screenshot of the page to prove we were there. 

We then made the subject line all about the mission of their company, and that also translated into the first line/preview text. 

We also use Claygent to find actual products, so that we can name drop the actual product names into the middle of the email, highlighted below. 

And between these customizations and the image, we pretty much just had to give social proof and a light CTA in order to get the response. 

The other campaigns that we split-tested were:

AI-only campaigns without images
Look-alike campaigns
New in roll campaigns

But nothing really did as well as showing the image. 

 I know then I'll get a lot of questions about if you can have an image in an email, but what I like to tell people is even if the image hurts deliverability, but it raises responses, it's worth sending the image. 

Everything in marketing is about testing, and so if it's worth sending the image, send the image.

### 73. 7416516521214226432

- URL: https://www.linkedin.com/posts/outboundphd_2-years-of-effort-went-into-the-prospeoio-activity-7416516521214226432-nqCC
- Author: 🦾Eric Nowoslawski (@outboundphd)
- Posted: 2026-01-12T16:28:50.871Z

#### Verbatim Content

2 years of effort went into the Prospeo.io Company/Contact API and this is why it was WELL worth the wait.

2 years ago Jon asked me, "If there were a database that could do anything you want, what would you wish it would do?"

I had a laundry list of ideas from

- Data accuracy and transperancy
- Organization tools for the team
- API capabilities

And then, I didn't hear from them for a little while. We kept using their email finding tool in all our Clay tables as a happy customer of course but I thought they were just focusing on that.

Then Hugo reaches out to me and says, "Hey, we finished the database the way you wanted. Want to have a look?"

I sign in to Prospeo V2 and my mouth is on the floor of everything they built. Good things definitely take time and they crushed it on this one.

I go over my favorite parts in the video down below but here are my favorite features you should check out if you don't watch the video and go straight to the site.

- Data quantity - I did comparisons of searches against Sales Navigator and was extemely happy with the counts that come back for the same filters on people and company searches in sales nav vs. prospeo

- Data quality - Every profile I checked worked at the company Prospeo says that they work at. I tried to be sneaky and check a friend that I know changed jobs a week ago and they already had that in their database.

- Email quality - All emails are SMTP verified with bounceban on top of that.
The API is so great. I just spent the christmas break making a company database we are going to scrap and just use their's.

- When you enrich a person, you get the company data with the API response so you don't have to worry about double spending on companies!

- The platform is so fast to make searches in.

- Your team can visually make searches and then click a button to get all of the JSON of that search to quickly get that search with their API.

I think they are just starting to launch it and I would highly recommend checking it out to help with your company and contact finding workflows. 

We are starting to switch a lot of TAM links internally over to them so we can stop downloading and uploading so many CSVs!

Not paid to post this or anything. 

Just found it last week and thought they did a really great job and other people would like to see it!

Check it out here! 

https://lnkd.in/dJw9eAEy

### 75. 7417588931183022080

- URL: https://www.linkedin.com/posts/outboundphd_my-team-and-i-spent-9-hours-talking-through-activity-7417588931183022080-zuuf
- Author: 🦾Eric Nowoslawski (@outboundphd)
- Posted: 2026-01-15T15:30:13.332Z

#### Verbatim Content

My team and I spent 9 hours talking through every customer we've ever worked with at Growth Engine X, what didn't work, and what worked the most often. 

I wanted to train my account managers to have historical knowledge of what we've done for customers.

The good, the bad, and the ugly. And so that we can take the transcript for some context training as well. 

As we went customer by customer, for every customer we had that we built a crazy outbound workflow that took screenshots of pricing pages and analyzed them to send a custom email, maybe 4-5 other customers would pop up and I'd say,

"Yeah, this was another one that we split test a ton of signals and value propositions and the best campaign was to basically help them imagine what it would be like to work with our customer."

No jiu jitsu workflows and multi table Clay builds. Really it was getting the content of the linkedin profile and the website. Structuring it and letting AI help write parts of the email to help the prospect understand how we could help in their situation. 

We generally would break it down into 2 frameworks of campaigns.

- A Creative ideas campaign - this is something I've posted about a ton but is basically where we send over 3 ideas of how we can help a company and each idea is a different flavor of our customer's offer. Still the best performing campaign for a number of our customers right now. 

Including 3 that are publicly traded companies. 

- An AI specificity campaign - Similar idea but instead of accidentally bloating the campaign with 3 bullet points, we opt for just a singular sentence. 

One of our customer's Donut thought they had everything dialed in before working with us (which they really almost did) and this one line tripled their response rates. 

So when doing your testing, triggers, workflows, awesome complicated emails are great. but don't forget to try plain straight to the point emails and AI emails that don't need anything other than a short company description to power them.

### 76. 7421922491352125440

- URL: https://www.linkedin.com/posts/outboundphd_i-split-tested-multiple-apis-to-find-recent-activity-7421922491352125440-Ilcl
- Author: 🦾Eric Nowoslawski (@outboundphd)
- Posted: 2026-01-27T14:30:14.590Z

#### Verbatim Content

I split tested multiple APIs to find recent company news, case studies, and the most recent blog article of a company and these were my favorite ones. 

During the snow storm, I couldn't resist the urge to figure out what everyone thinks about in a snow storm. 

"Which API should I use to find company news with the best rate limit, cost, and of course quality."

I tried 10 different tools and OpenWeb Ninja Real Time Web Search and Parallel Web Systems were my favorites. 

They each did something a little bit different which will help me pick what I want in the future. 

I manually researched 20 companies and their recent news, case studies, and blog to capture what the answers were and then did a bake off. 

OpenWeb Ninja was 10x cheaper than Parallel and super fast

Parallel caught far more of my signals but of course was more expensive and had some overly enthusiastic false positives. 

So I even ran a test checking if I could stack companies in one Parallel research call so that I could save on cost and I actually found that you could get away with asking about the recent news about 4 companies before the performance started to degrade. 

So if I really need the company news to be super accurate for my list, I'd use Parallel. 

If I'm doing some really wide scraping and I needed something cheap that could get me Google SERP results, I'd go with OpenWeb Ninja.

Hope this helps some other folks doing these tests! 

Below is the output of the test results for anyone who wants to see the raw output. 

 Test 1: Head-to-Head Bakeoff (20 Companies)                                
 ┌──────────────┬──────────┬─────────────┬─────────────────────┐                      
 │   API   │ Hit Rate │ Cost/Search │    Verdict    │                      
 ├──────────────┼──────────┼─────────────┼─────────────────────┤                      
 │ OpenWebNinja │ 35%   │ $0.0006   │ Best value     │                      
 ├──────────────┼──────────┼─────────────┼─────────────────────┤                      
 │ Parallel.ai │ 70%   │ $0.0054   │ 2x signals, 9x cost │                      
 ├──────────────┼──────────┼─────────────┼─────────────────────┤                      
──────┴─────────────────────┘                      
 Test 2: Batching Efficiency (100 Companies)                                
 ┌──────────────┬────────┬────────┬─────────────────────┐                          
 │ Batch Size │ Recall │ Cost │    Finding    │                          
 ├──────────────┼────────┼────────┼─────────────────────┤                          
 │ 1 company  │ 100%  │ $0.50 │ Baseline      │                          
 ├──────────────┼────────┼────────┼─────────────────────┤                          
 │ 2 companies │ 100%  │ $0.25 │ Still accurate   │                          
 ├──────────────┼────────┼────────┼─────────────────────┤                          
 │ 4 companies │ 90%  │ $0.125 │ Starting to degrade │

### 77. 7419808508654624768

- URL: https://www.linkedin.com/posts/outboundphd_building-and-evaluating-claygent-prompts-activity-7419808508654624768-5zDW
- Author: 🦾Eric Nowoslawski (@outboundphd)
- Posted: 2026-01-21T18:30:01.839Z

#### Verbatim Content

Building and evaluating Claygent prompts is getting a lot easier and really anyone can start improving today.       
                                         
 Here's how we're doing it.                           
                                         
 I was reviewing some of our newer Claygent prompts and noticed best practices were missing. We were losing coverage on signals.                     
                                         
 Anyone using Claygent knows you can filter out bad coverage. But that means  
 you're paying for email addresses you won't send to, even though they fit the  
 criteria. 

And I haven't found anything that gets the quality of output of Claygent for the cost. It's just our prompts as an organization need to improve.                               
                                         
 So we're building a Claude skill internally that:               
                                         
 1. Has all the best practices I want every Claygent prompt to start with    
 2. Takes 10 example data points on companies/people that I've manually verified    
 3. Runs Claygent against those same records                  
 4. Saves all results to Supabase                        
 5. Claude reviews accuracy vs. the manually verified data
6. Edits the prompt
7. Sends more tests until we hit 100% accuracy on this small sample size.         
                                         
The Supabase piece is key. We update the skill weekly with what's actually   
 working.                                    
                                         
Now we can see empirically how much more accurate our Claygent prompts are getting over time. 

What models are actually worth paying for for hard tasks versus just better prompts with 4o-mini                           
                                         
 Ours isn't ready for a giveaway yet.                      
                                         
 But I wanted to share that we're thinking about this and already building   
 custom Claygents way faster.                          
                                         
 That said, Jordan Crawford has finished his and it's already so much better   
 than ours that we might stop working on it entirely.              
                                         
 If you're interested in something like this, maybe do what I'm about to do and
 just buy Jordan's version.                          
                                         
 Anyone else thinking about these evaluation loops for Claygent or any other outbound workflows?

### 78. 7425618041176170497

- URL: https://www.linkedin.com/posts/outboundphd_i-used-to-worry-about-silly-things-in-my-activity-7425618041176170497-CBRd
- Author: 🦾Eric Nowoslawski (@outboundphd)
- Posted: 2026-02-06T19:15:02.328Z

#### Verbatim Content

I used to worry about silly things in my cold email campaigns. 5 years later, this is what I worry about and what I don't worry about. 

1. Which tool I'm sending from. My brother and sisters in outbound, the underlying technology of how Smarltead and Instantly send email is the largely the same. Pick who you like the feature set of more and move on. 

2. Does email warmup work and what are the perfect warmup settings. 

If email warmup didn't work, the platforms offering it unlimited for free would be the first people to ask you to shut it off so they could lower their AWS bill. 

I don't think there are perfect email warmup settings. As long as it's sending something and getting replies back it's fine. 

3. Triple Validating Leads

We validate our emails with million verifier before sending and enjoy the usage of bounce ban verified emails by using the Prospeo API. It's all going to be alright. 

4. Spam words in my copy. 

Ok if you send "Bitcoin" or "jesus" or "oprah" you're going to have issues. But I see people asking questions in chats all the time about the word free or something. And it's ok in moderation. If you think you're having issues, pull it out and see if it improves. 

5. The quest for email deliverability knowledge that no one else has. 

Any gimmick or strategy outside of the tried an true things we talk about on here all the time turns out to be a quick fad if it works at all or really not true. 

6. 3rd party tools to tell if you're emails are going to spam

Just check the reply rates of your domains...

7. Focusing on everything all at once in the copywriting vs spending 80% of my time on how I pitched the offer of what I'm helping to promote

8. Wondering if different email providers get better results than the others. Of course we use Hypertide.io and Zapmail.ai but if the provider you're using is fast and gives you access to the admin console, you're all set. 

9. This might not fit as a worry, but you should run more tests than you think and see how the best brands run facebook ads. It's no different. Cold email is just a private ads network. 

10. Working with a client where their website/case studies/social content wasn't dialed in. 

Yes that all helps but we run free tests where people can't look up the company and are just responding to the offer and it works all the time. 

What would you add to the list?

### 80. 7417936175987851265

- URL: https://www.linkedin.com/posts/outboundphd_heres-the-prompt-to-get-you-started-building-activity-7417936175987851265-oiEA
- Author: 🦾Eric Nowoslawski (@outboundphd)
- Posted: 2026-01-16T14:30:02.945Z

#### Verbatim Content

Here's the prompt to get you started building your own google maps scraper with Claude code or Cursor. 

One of my favorite things about LLMs and coding is that my team can build things that impact our business and then everyone can use. Bharatt Arorah built this google maps scraper for us and it's been so easy to use. 

Then when someone else on the team wants to add to it, it'd be really easy for them. 

This scraper is built on a RapidAPI endpoint that gives us 3,000,000 requests per month for $100 a month. 

Here's the prompt I'd give for Cursor or Claude Code to figure this out for you. 

First connect to the Rapid API MCP and then run this because it'll ask you to restart. (I don't use the MCP to actually run the code. I just use it to connect to the documentation to help set everything up). 

If you need the list of zip codes don't ask me or your mother for it. Ask Chat GPT literally asking "Do you know of any GitHub repositories that have all of the zip codes in the United States, or any other public ways that you can download that information easily?" and it'll come up, I promise. 

Long Text not optimized for a linkedin post AT ALL. 

Hey cursor, can you explore this Google Maps endpoint and build a plan for me that would be able to create an input where I can say a Google category, and then you'd be able to scrape it?

Here is a file of all the zip codes around the United States so that you have something to parse by. Search every one of these zip codes with my keyword to then find the companies that are in those areas. Build something into it that includes logic that, if a company is in a different business category that is excluded from my results, I want to be able to filter for the category. Let me know if we could exclude the cities and also run multiple category searches in one run. Set up the database schema to pull the zip codes from SupaBase. Literally go one by one doing a search across each of the zip codes. I also might wanna specify that I want to include certain cities, exclude certain cities, or include and exclude states in the future as well. Let's also add the filters for that to be possible as well. 

Hope that helps and provides a good lead gen project for people trying to get into coding with Cursor or Claude code.

Send those companies over to a Clay webhook to have a contact finding waterfall and you're all set!

### 81. 7439298189049991168

- URL: https://www.linkedin.com/posts/outboundphd_there-is-actually-a-limit-to-cold-email-reply-activity-7439298189049991168-4uK-
- Author: 🦾Eric Nowoslawski (@outboundphd)
- Posted: 2026-03-16T13:15:03.746Z

#### Verbatim Content

There is actually a limit to cold email reply rates.

Knowing this can hopefully free you from testing things that won't matter.

Let me explain.

I have worked with customers in the past that are obsessed with getting the reply rate as high as possible. They think if they send a good enough email, every single person who could possibly see it is going to respond.

We've run tests that show this just isn't the case.

We had the opportunity to work on behalf of Forbes at Web Summit. We had the list of speakers. We had all their emails. We cold emailed them from infrastructure that had warmed up for four months. The pitch was simple: "This is our booth. We want to interview you for Forbes. Book a meeting." And we sent them a link to Forbes.com.

That only got a 30% reply rate. Basically all positive.

When I say "only" 30% — if I were a speaker at an event and Forbes wanted to interview me, I would think the reply rate would be even higher than that.

We also helped a customer email their own customers. We found data that showed they needed a new product, and we could fix the problem for free.

Still only a 10% reply rate.

I say all this because these two examples are pretty undeniably good offers. And they still did not get 40, 50, 60% reply rates.

When you're sending emails, there are so many reasons why somebody doesn't respond. They didn't even see it. It got mixed up with everything else in their inbox. It's just not the right time for them.

When you've run a couple of experiments and the reply rate is staying pretty steady, I would think more about experiments around lists and experiments around offers versus trying to change the copy to see if an AI personalization or a fancier way of saying something is going to move the needle.

List segmentation and changing your offer matter way more.

What experiments have you run that changed your thinking on reply rates?

### 82. 7419050979062358016

- URL: https://www.linkedin.com/posts/outboundphd_this-is-the-type-of-campaign-i-would-suggest-activity-7419050979062358016-mgZ2
- Author: 🦾Eric Nowoslawski (@outboundphd)
- Posted: 2026-01-19T16:19:52.710Z

#### Verbatim Content

This is the type of campaign I would suggest anyone runs if they are new to cold email and automated outbound. 

Internally, we call it the creative ideas campaign and if you watch the video (which I'm soft launching before we put it on YouTube) I go over the past and current success we've had with this campaign. 

If you don't have time to watch the video, the strategy is that we use AI to learn about our customer's offer and how they help their customers. 

So that when we reach out to a prospect, we can analyze their website and understand what they do in order to output 1-3 creative ideas on how we would be able to help them. 

This way we aren't sending AI generated slop compliments but instead, real tangible ways that we can help based on the company and contact's context. 

This is the best campaign for many of our customers including Clay, a PR company we're about to release a huge case study with that gets 80 leads a day with us, and so many of our other customers. 

Here's an example template that we could use 

Hey {{first_name}} - I had some ideas about how we could target {{customer types}} together.

Here's what I'm thinking
{{Idea}}
{{Idea}}
{{Idea}}

We built a platform that lets you get started for $100 a month with everything you need to start campaigns like I mentioned.

Could I show you how it works sometime?

{{sendingAccountName}}

Here's what that could look like if it were filled in. 

Hey {{first_name}} - I had some ideas about how we could target Finance Leaders at US Pharma companies together.

Here's what I'm thinking

- Target CFOs that recently took over their role and show them how you can help them reduce expenses in their first 90 days.

- Use our Website Visitor Identification to uncover purchasing directors researching your site but didn't leave their email.

- Send campaigns like this to VPs of Finance asking if their org is currently evaluating currently cutting software costs with some of the NIH cuts happening.

We built a platform that lets you get started for $100 a month with everything you need to start campaigns like I mentioned.

Could I show you how it works sometime?

And for those that might say this is really long, I get it. 

You can simplify it into one sentence that applies to the prospect which we also helped 3x the reply rates for John Thomas campaigns by just adding this one line to his best templates. 

Check out the video or save this for later to understand more about the strategy.

### 83. 7416916974150086656

- URL: https://www.linkedin.com/posts/outboundphd_a-couple-of-customers-have-asked-me-what-activity-7416916974150086656-iwGT
- Author: 🦾Eric Nowoslawski (@outboundphd)
- Posted: 2026-01-13T19:00:06.291Z

#### Verbatim Content

A couple of customers have asked me what I think about this announcement from Google and here's my humble prediction. 

I'm not going to be naive and say that this won't have any affect on cold email. But also, even the video doesn't explain very well what this will do. 

I think that there is a best case scenario and a worst case scenario. 

Best case - this is an overall summarization of the email meaning that the preview text we optimize our first lines for today won't be as important as optimizing for how the summary will come across. 

A recipient can see the offer (maybe even stated cleaner than what an average copywriter can do) and choose to open or disregard. 

Worst case - it becomes a new spam filter and labels all external emails you haven't received before as spam with no preview. 

I truly think the actual outcome will be somewhere in the middle and lead magnets will become more and more important so you aren't just pitching calls and getting hung up in the spam filters. 

Truly, no one knows but it's not in my inbox yet but excited to see it when it is so we can start playing with it and see how it affects the channel.

### 84. 7420118138316451840

- URL: https://www.linkedin.com/posts/outboundphd_i-have-to-admit-six-months-ago-i-didnt-activity-7420118138316451840-R9ct
- Author: 🦾Eric Nowoslawski (@outboundphd)
- Posted: 2026-01-22T15:00:23.303Z

#### Verbatim Content

I have to admit, six months ago I didn't get the point of MCPs.         
                                         
This post is for people who were like me. Also for my employees who are reluctant to start using these tools.                     
                                         
What is an MCP (Model Context Protocol)?                    
                                         
Think of it as a gateway for an AI agent to understand a tool.         
                                         
Instead of asking an agent to read API documentation (it will miss things),  
you connect it to an MCP. The agent figures out:                
 - What endpoints exist                             
 - What payloads to send                            
 - How to actually accomplish your task                     
                                         
Then you ask it to write the TypeScript or Python to make those API calls   
directly.                                   
                                         
Way more efficient. Way fewer tokens.                     
                                         
 My biggest tip:                                
                                         
 We're not actually doing a lot of work with MCPs as in moving data or doing work with MCP.             
                                         
We use them so agents understand the API documentation better.         
                                         
The MCP helps the agent learn the tool. Then we have Claude write actual code that calls the APIs.                                
                                         
That's the workflow:   
                           
 1. Connect MCP                                 
 2. Agent learns the tool                            
 3. Agent writes the automation code                      
 4. You run the code directly                          
                                        
 If you've been confused about MCPs, hopefully this helps.

### 85. 7420472913343975425

I haven't logged into Make in 4 months.                 
                                         
 And I don't think I'm looking back.                      
                                         
 Here's what happened:                             
                                         
 Every automation I've been building, I write in Cursor or Claude Code. I test 
 it on my computer. When it works, I just ask it to push to Trigger.dev.    
                                         
 That's the whole workflow.                           
                                         
 I was showing an engineering team what I'm building recently. They asked, "Can you show us in Trigger.dev?"                         
                                         
 I had to admit, I've never even actually logged into Trigger.dev other than to setup my account.                                 
                                         
I don't even really know how the UI works.                   
                                         
I just know the outputs are working and I can verify all of the outputs to my liking,.         
                                         
 What we're running:                              
 - Email waterfalls                               
 - Weekly competitor LinkedIn post analysis (finding people in our customer's  ICP engaging)                                   
 - Domain reply rate analysis                          
 - Campaign performance analysis                        
 - Any systematic cron job we need     
- I even messed up a payload and had to edit 815k rows in a supabase that I did with trigger.dev in 10 minutes.                  
                                         
I've never paid Trigger.dev more than $25/month.                
                                         
And we're running more and more automations through it every week.       
                                         
Whether you're building waterfalls, monitoring competitors, or analyzing    
campaign data, you might not need Make/n8n anymore.

### 86. 7426712755078012928

- URL: https://www.linkedin.com/posts/outboundphd_3-real-inbound-sales-requests-i-received-activity-7426712755078012928-AouA
- Author: 🦾Eric Nowoslawski (@outboundphd)
- Posted: 2026-02-09T19:45:02.461Z

#### Verbatim Content

3 real inbound sales requests I received last week that I told should never pay an agency or freelancer to do what they asked. 

I would say about 50% of my sales call requests are disqualified by me sending them an email asking why they would want to pay someone to do what they are asking and I just send them a video instead. 

Here are some of those examples.


1. Hey Eric, I'm new at my sales org, we have no sales people and have traditionally purchased leads and gotten referrals to grow up until this point. I'm the only sales person. 

🚩🚩🚩🚩 

These are the people that shouldn't think about hiring an agency. 

You have no understanding of 

1) how hard marketing is
2) how to convert cold traffic
3) how to deal with an outbound lead even if I am successful 

Too much risk reputationally for us to take this on and this is an expensive bet for them to take on. Even if you argue "well maybe it's not worth their time."

$500 in tech
2 weeks of email warmup
1 hour with claude to write your email content (Just tell it to use Josh Braun frameworks)

Too expensive of a bet when compared with an agency. 

2. We have this list of attendees from this event that we want to find emails for. We are willing to pay someone to do it. 

🚩🚩🚩🚩 

Don't pay a freelancer to do this. Get a Clay account and enrich to your heart's desire there or a Prospeo.io account. Their search API works off of name and company name or name and job title. 

Claude Code and these tools make it so easy, don't pay someone to do it. 

3. We want to enrich our CRM and do some analysis arount what employee headcount we sell to and then find companies and contacts based on this. 

🚩🚩🚩🚩 

Similar reasoning as above but a different request. A smart agency would just do this for you for free because it's so easy to do in Clay, Prospeo, or really any tool of your choice. Clay brings the unlimited choice of integrations and AI research. Prospeo is just so simple and their API is so fast.

### 87. 7424157166955188225

- URL: https://www.linkedin.com/posts/outboundphd_342-to-find-49k-instagram-profiles-including-activity-7424157166955188225-oz0U
- Author: 🦾Eric Nowoslawski (@outboundphd)
- Posted: 2026-02-02T18:30:02.772Z

#### Verbatim Content

$3.42 to find 49k Instagram profiles including their emails and some light ICP building ready to go? Let's break it down. 

Scraping Instagram is notoriously difficult but so many people want to target creators/contacts that really aren't on LinkedIn (thank goodness the LinkedIn engineers aren't as good at blocking scraping as Instagram). 

So we have to go to our favorite scraping partner on the internet. Hey there Google! 

Google has built you a search scraper that everyone company must let it in in order to be discovered. 

In this process, we used OpenWeb Ninja's google search api to do it. 

Let's try to break this down. 

Google scraping is great but you can only get a subset of searches at a time. 

So if you want a ton of contacts, you're going to have to run lots of different searches programmatically. 

For this search we were targeting CEOs, authors, founders, etc. Not necessarily professional content creators. 

I had Claude code build me searches for every state, every city, and then 200 keyword searches to pair with each base keyword. 

We would scrape Google searches where the search would be 

site:instagram.com "founder" AND "@" AND "Los Angeles" AND "Finance"

vs. 

site:instagram.com "founder" AND "@" AND "Los Angeles" AND "MedSpa"

(the @ is to have the highest chance that someone has an email in their bio!)

If we only search for site:instagram.com "founder" no way we get the whole search. 

Then Claude code ran with the OpenWeb Ninja API and completed the searches for me while deduping in the process. 

Going to throw it in Clay to orchestrate some final mile Apify enrichment and email validation before launching the campaign!

### 88. 7420178471286644736

- URL: https://www.linkedin.com/posts/outboundphd_if-youre-finding-claude-code-and-cursor-activity-7420178471286644736-9rFg
- Author: 🦾Eric Nowoslawski (@outboundphd)
- Posted: 2026-01-22T19:00:07.804Z

#### Verbatim Content

If you're finding Claude Code and Cursor daunting, here's how to get started. 
                                         
My employees were reluctant too. So we created some exercises.         
                                         
 Tip 1: Bring it into your regular workflow                   
                                         
 When you're assigned a task:                          
 - Use Wispr Flow to fully explain what you're about to do           
 - Paste that into Cursor's plan mode                      
 - While it's generating, start working on the task manually          
 - When Cursor pings you with the plan, read through it             
 - Let it execute                                
                                         
 The reason you work manually while waiting: you have to get the task done   
 anyway. Might as well see how Cursor approaches it too.            
                                         
 Tip 2: Start with one-step workflows                      
                                         
 When you're first building, only create one or two-step automations.      
                                         
 Give an input. Call one API. That's it.                    
                                         
 Don't try to build something complicated on day one.              
                                         
 Tip 3: Let agents read the docs for you                    
                                         
 My team didn't want to sift through API documentation.             
                                         
 So we spin up agents whose entire context window is just reading the API docs 
 for Smartlead or whatever tool they're using.                 
                                         
 The agent learns it. Then it builds the automation.              
                                         
 Tip 4: Start by doing something simple like changing emojis                        
                                         
 We have an internal app. Everyone's first task is literally just changing   
 emojis in the UI to get comfortable with the workflow.             
                                         
 Tip 5: Screenshot your errors                         
                                         
 Every time I hit a problem, I screenshot it and paste it back into Claude   
 Code.                                     
                                         
 "Hey, why am I seeing this? How do I fix it?"                 
                                         
 It always fixes it.                              
                                         
 If you're reluctant to start, these are just some ways to ease in.

Been testing BlitzAPI for contact finding.

The good:
→ When it returns a contact, confidence is high
→ Right person, works there, correct data
→ Better accuracy than other platforms I've tested

The catch:

We're pushing it into our workflows because the accuracy is worth the extra setup.
