### 91. 7413949935047307264

- URL: https://www.linkedin.com/posts/outboundphd_because-of-claude-code-and-cursor-this-christmas-activity-7413949935047307264-Byuy
- Author: 🦾Eric Nowoslawski (@outboundphd)
- Posted: 2026-01-05T14:30:09.044Z

#### Verbatim Content

Because of Claude Code and Cursor, this Christmas break was easily the most productive break I've ever had. Here's a snapshot of what I got done for our customers at Growth Engine X. 

1. Internal Company database beefed up with more enrichments. Over 8 million US companies stored with tons of data points to filter on and use for ICP verification.

2. Claude Skill to query that database, make a prompt to validate each company fits ICP. Run on 500 companies. Make adjustments, ship to the Batch API when ready for the best possible efficiency.

3. Everything hooked up to BlitzAPI to find contacts.

4. Got some other contact data providers from Rapid API documented into skills so we can find more local business and fresh linkedin data

5. Built an API with an open source html to text library so we can get any company website page we want and scrape it.

6. Automatically running a reply rate analysis every Friday to know which domains are healthy and which domains need to be removed. 

7. After I manually approve that, it pulls out all the domains and replaces them with fresh domains and tells my friend Omer M at Hypertide.io to cancel and place new orders.

8. Automatically set up campaigns and add inboxes into all of our campaigns no matter the platform. Logic built into to not have one inbox in more than one campaign. Redistribution logic always built in.

9. Dashboards...so many dashboards

10. API to query our email database cache quickly to -

Find if we have found the email in the past. If validated 90 days ago, keep it. If more than 90 days ago. Revalidate it. Waterfall (on the cloud with trigger.dev) 

LeadMagic -> Prospeo.io -> Icypeas -> BlitzAPI to find emails. 

11. Make a request to query our internal database for companies and find contacts. Store in a Supabase to process leads to a campaign level Clay webhook for ocntacts to automatically be added to campaigns every day at 2 am

12. Automatic Claygent builder (but Jordan Crawford has probably beat me with his. stay on the lookout for it).

13. Claude browser automation to create pesky company/contact searches automatically in Clay.

14. Sending calculations for each customer at 12, 3, and 6. Diagnoses why it missed sending. Inbox problem or leads problem. Rebatches all inboxes if it's an inbox problem. Sends 5x the amount of daily requested leads from the daily dispatch to the campaign to make sure we have a ton. 

15. Made a claude skill to connect to a rapid API MCP that can help find people engaging with your current customer's content so you can find ICP that you'll have a great social connection with. That'll be it's own post for sure.

16. A skill to help us make regular messaging prompts that optimize for cached inputs 

17. A workflow and a claude skill to launch free tests from just a company input and use our internal batches of inboxes to do everything end to end. 

And that's the character limit. More detail coming this week on everything.

### 92. 7417211438831738880

- URL: https://www.linkedin.com/posts/outboundphd_this-workflow-i-made-with-cursor-and-hosted-activity-7417211438831738880-Mt-r
- Author: 🦾Eric Nowoslawski (@outboundphd)
- Posted: 2026-01-14T14:30:12.142Z

#### Verbatim Content

This workflow I made with Cursor and hosted on Railway saves me 6 hours a week on email deliverability tasks and our customers get much more consistent results from it. 

Over the last 6 months, we really systematized our email deliverability infrastructure. 

No more guessing and then ordering domains that we thought felt right. I built an SOP for everyone that I can give away at the end of the post. 

We would miss customers. Over order for some, under order for others. It was crazy. 

So we got extremely scientific about it. 

We keep all domains marked as status, Active, Insurance, or Cancel in Supabase. 

We always want to keep AT LEAST 50% of our Active daily sending volume in Insurance so that we can always rotate our domains. 

Every Friday, I would use a python script that would get the reply rates of all the domains in our campaigns so I could make decisions of what should be cancelled and what should be rotated from Insurance to Active. 

Once I had the numbers, spreadsheet warfare would begin. 

Making sure that every customer had enough and we didn't cancel too many domains. Numbers wouldn't look right so I'd check them manually. It was a mess.

Now, every Friday, I run this little Preview Global Rebatch and it send me a slack message with every customer, their daily send goal, how many domains are active, how many domains do we suggest to cancel, how many domains I need to buy more of, etc. 

And I hit accept and not only does it change all the statuses in our Supabase, it also pulls all of our customer's campaigns and swaps out the old domains for new domains. 

Easily my favorite workflow for deliverability. 

If you want our email deliverability SOP that goes over how we do this and how we work with Hypertide.io and Zapmail.ai to get it done, comment "primary" and I'll comment it back to you!

### 93. 7420559718449840128

- URL: https://www.linkedin.com/posts/outboundphd_i-think-weve-cracked-a-list-building-process-activity-7420559718449840128-S4FF
- Author: 🦾Eric Nowoslawski (@outboundphd)
- Posted: 2026-01-23T20:15:04.211Z

#### Verbatim Content

I think we've cracked a list-building process that gets new customers started with really high-quality lists right after they onboard with us.

Here's the problem:

I'm the best list builder on my team. But when onboarding a new customer,
 we're not trying to build a 100% TAM list.

We need a really good sample, enough data to run campaigns for 1-2 months.

We can always rerun for full coverage later.

Here's the process:

Step 1: Analyze their existing data

Take their do-not-contact list or dream customer list. Enrich in Clay to find:

Industries
Department headcounts
Website keywords
Pricing strategy
Any other data points they claim matter

Clay's derived data points make this analysis deep. I LOVE the data that comes with the enrich company integration (cache it in Supabase for everyone that is going to be in my comments that it's expensive)

Step 2: Use AI to find patterns

Run it through Sculptor or GPT 5.2 to analyze:

Best industries
Most common industries
Key details beyond industry/headcount/location
What actually correlates with being a good fit

Sometimes our customers will say that a signal was important when the company purchased and that might not always be the case.

Someone thought that the ratio of SDRs to AEs on a sales team was the most important signal and all of their buyers actually all had Sales Ops on the team and the ratio of SDRs to AEs was all over the place.

Step 3: Pull companies via Prospeo.io

Now we know: "We're looking for companies in X industry because of Y reasons."

We use Prospeo to pull all companies matching the top level industry, headcount, location criteria.

Why not Clay? They are working on this but I just don't want to be messing around in a UI if I can help it now. Prospeo's search API gets what we need. 

Step 4: Enrich final data points in Clay and AI validates the list

Build a prompt that looks at their actual customers, then checks: "Are these new companies good fits?"

This also builds out the ICP process.

Step 5: OpenAI batch API

For anything custom, run through Claygent. Then batch API through GPT to verify fit.

Cache the input for 90% off. Batch API for 50% off the call.

The result:
When we onboard a customer, we throw as much data and AI cleanup at it as possible to get a really strong starter list. 

Between Clay's derived data points and the Prospeo API, you can get really far, really fast, with really high quality.

What a time to be alive in B2B go-to-market because of the availability of data and the cost of GPT processing going down. 

You can use an open source model on something like Vast(.)ai if you want this to be even cheaper (hat tip to Anurag Phadke for that).

### 95. 7401621304048513024

- URL: https://www.linkedin.com/posts/outboundphd_built-a-workflow-this-weekend-that-completely-activity-7401621304048513024-go1d
- Author: 🦾Eric Nowoslawski (@outboundphd)
- Posted: 2025-12-02T14:00:34.359Z

#### Verbatim Content

Built a workflow this weekend that completely changed how we process leads for our customers..

The problem: Downloading CSVs from databases and manually uploading them into Clay

We process data from 3 main places. 

→ We collect company and contact data in Clay
→ Bulk Data in Apollo as well
→ Custom Scraping with RapidAPI scrapers

Every week, we process about 20M rows of CSV data in Clay.

What I built on Black Friday with Cursor automates the following

→ One form submission kicks off the workflow from Clickup
→ Finds all qualifying companies from our internal database OR RapidAPI scrapers → saves to Supabase
→ Drip companies into contact enrichment every day at 2 AM (we use Clay and BlitzAPI ($500 per month for 10,000,000 contacts tell them I sent you)
→ Check our email cache (23M+ emails) to avoid re-buying data. Revalidate if over 30 days old. 
→ Works with Apollo, Clay, OR custom Rapid API/Apify Scrapers

Over the weekend I successfully processed lead refills for 8 of our customers and now we will roll it out to more of them! 

 it even looks up daily how many contacts we have in total and how many we have left to process, so we know when we have to get more or restart the list 

The magic: Our database tracks every campaign, daily lead quotas, and Clay webhooks.

Every morning at 2am, it auto-sends contacts to refuel campaigns.

No more CSVs. No more manual uploads. All hosted on Railway and built by Cursor.

### 96. 7402723570092777472

- URL: https://www.linkedin.com/posts/outboundphd_we-have-8-customers-sending-over-25k-emails-activity-7402723570092777472-Uu5g
- Author: 🦾Eric Nowoslawski (@outboundphd)
- Posted: 2025-12-05T15:00:35.063Z

#### Verbatim Content

We have 8 customers sending over 25k emails per day (one of them sending over 100k per day). Here's how we keep their campaigns full of leads completely automatically with Clay and Supabase.

And if you clicked learn more because of the volume, yes the numbers are good. That sales team sending 100k per day gets about 125ish leads per day and their TAM has 6M people in it. They're fine. 

In order to send this much volume, you can't rely on CSVs. We tried and epically failed. 

So here's what we do instead. 

1. We get the whole company TAM in Clay. 

We break a part the searches to stay under the 50k threshold by changing filters that don't matter. For instance, everyone targets the US and we can filter by headcount and states to make the list smaller.

2. We stage that company list in Supabase.

3. Using some code we made for an edge function in Supabase, every day at 2 am, we process more leads from Supabase back into Clay to find contacts. The tables auto delete so we stay under the 50k row limit. 

People sleep on the fact that on whatever plan you have, people searching is unlimited in Clay. Auto deleting rows is an enterprise feature but you could get around it if you need.

4. After contacts are found they are pushed to another table for enrichment, qualification, AI message writing and finally, being dropped into a Smartlead campaign.

5. We manage it all from Clickup with analytics on how much of their TAM they have left and forecasts on when we need to find more companies so we can try to hit their TAM with 1 email every 3 months. 

This way we can have it on repeat every day. 

Sometimes, I even get a sale on email credits or something and we process a big batch in one go. I'm processing 1.8M leads for a client right now actually. 

With tools like Cursor and Clay, doing repeated things manually is just wasted time.

### 98. 7402349722172297217

- URL: https://www.linkedin.com/posts/outboundphd_we-just-finished-up-some-testing-and-this-activity-7402349722172297217-_Dwu
- Author: 🦾Eric Nowoslawski (@outboundphd)
- Posted: 2025-12-04T14:15:02.773Z

#### Verbatim Content

We just finished up some testing, and this cold email is outperforming 6 other tests on behalf of one of our customers. 

This customer is one of the largest cleaning equipment companies by market share in the United States. 

They want to target hotels to of course sell cleaning equipment but there's one hang up. 

Most people who have cleaning equipment don't feel that it needs to be replaced for a couple years. So many of the other emails are getting objections that they're good right now. 

I think there are a couple of reasons why this one breaks through the noise. 

Subject line: starts off with something it's possible a customer could have sent them. 

Preview text/first line all about a real google review we scraped with a Rapid API endpoint. We search for the top reviews that mentioned cleaning type things and go about 40 reviews back. 

If we don't find anything related to cleaning (which we almost always do), we just have a fallback referencing another good review. 

Then we ask who's in charge instead of a direct pick because there's really no way to be able to tell if they are the ones that make the purchasing decision. 

Leaning into the objection by calling it out and handling it. 

Some of the biggest issues for hotels is the churn of their cleaning staff. So we leaned into that and how truly the equipment can get them done with rooms faster so they don't need as many people. 

This company is genuinely partnered with every Hilton in the country. So we find the closest one and get it's address to get a nice social proof point with a  location they might know for each recipient. 

Easy CTA again hammering on the efficiency of the equipment. Not if they need something new.

### 99. 7401338054264725505

- URL: https://www.linkedin.com/posts/outboundphd_heres-everythingibuilt-withcursor-over-activity-7401338054264725505-aP-M

Here's everything I built with Cursor over Thanksgiving weekend. Some things were done in one prompt, some things took me a whole day. 

1. Automated our entire company/contact workflow
One form → Companies to Supabase → Contacts on-demand → Campaign auto-refill at 2am

2. Overhauled analytics

We already had pretty good analytics, but everything would run at midnight, and I wouldn't get snapshots during the day. So, I built this. 

- Snapshots of sending at 12pm, 3pm, 6pm EST
- Warnings if anything looks wrong
- Faster action on issues

3. Built our own email lookup API

One endpoint: first name + last name + domain
Checks our database cache first
Then LeadMagic → Prospeo.io → Icypeas → MillionVerifier guess → No duplicate paying for emails

4. Souped up our Google Maps scraper
- Now finds contacts mid-scrape
- Maps + contact data in one run

5. Automated campaign setup
- Settings done automatically
- Reads copywriting
- Suggests how to structure the Clay table

6. Batch API + cached inputs for scoring
- 50% off a request so we can make the prompts longer with more detail.
- 90% off GPT-5 token costs when you use a cached input on top of the batch API discount. 
- Company scoring at scale

7.  AI Lead Categorization 

- Uses the Smartlead API to pull all unassigned category leads. 
- We use the same prompt with GPT-5 Nano, GPT-40 Mini, and GPT-4.1 Nano. As long as they all agree on what category it automatically uploads the category. 

And now we only have to manually categorize what it doesn't agree on. This was a way harder issue than I thought, which is why I kind of hammered it with the three APIs, and we only have to filter 30-50 replies a day manually.

8. Bulk Contact Enrichment Flow for our biggest senders. 

We have 6 customers sending 50k+ emails per day (1 sending 120k emails per day) that just need a ton of data. 

I'm right this second processing 14M leads to be enriched, qualified, and distributed to the right campaigns. 

Can't wait to see what I can get done this week in Cursor.

### 100. 7401998719056920576

- URL: https://www.linkedin.com/posts/outboundphd_save-on-openai-costs-with-cached-inputs-activity-7401998719056920576-9ucL
- Author: 🦾Eric Nowoslawski (@outboundphd)
- Posted: 2025-12-03T15:00:17.109Z

#### Verbatim Content

There's only 2 rules you need to follow to be able to save anywhere from 50%-90% on your OpenAI input tokens by using cached inputs. 

I made a post recently about how you could save on your OpenAI calls with cached inputs and I did some testing to make sure it works.

Jeff Barg explained to me that the integration in Clay with OpenAI is ready to do cached inputs. We were prompting wrong (and so is the Clay metaprompter). 

The 2 rules you need to follow to have the highest chance of hitting cached inputs is to 

1. Make sure your prompt is over ~1,024 tokens. You can just ask chatgpt how many tokens a prompt is and it'll tell you. This is nothing for most of our prompts. 

2. As much of your prompt needs to be the same from the beginning of the prompt to whenever you make a change. 

That's what the caching of the input is. They already computed the beginning of the prompt and stored it. They recognize that it's been computed before and pass that saving to you. 

We were messing it up with putting custom parts of the prompt in the second or third sentence. 

Put all that custom stuff at the end and you will have a really high chance of hitting cached inputs. 

It feels like a black box because the Clay API at the point of this post doesn't tell you if you used cached inputs but they'll add that in soon!

Check out this video to see my experiment and proof that it's working with these two rules. 

https://lnkd.in/eFgRrhVK

### 102. 7397733050425434113

- URL: https://www.linkedin.com/posts/outboundphd_the-tests-are-in-and-we-are-not-allowing-activity-7397733050425434113-TfmC
- Author: 🦾Eric Nowoslawski (@outboundphd)
- Posted: 2025-11-21T20:30:02.455Z

#### Verbatim Content

The tests are in and we are not allowing generic AI personalizations based on company descriptions as first lines anymore. I could prove this raised reply rates but recently, in most markets, it falls flat.

We try to always lead with some kind of signal or pain point that we research but if we can't do that, we would fall back to a company description based personalization. 

Something like, "I saw you help fitness coaches retain clients and I wanted to get connected."

We used to see increased reply rates when we personalized first lines with AI-generated content scraped from company websites.

Now the data is pointing the opposite direction.

Sophisticated buyers recognize generic AI personalization instantly which hurts your message:
- "Hey, I saw how you helped fitness enthusiasts breathe better..."
- "I noticed you're focused on helping local businesses..."
- Generic references to their company description

They've seen it a thousand times.

Here's what we do instead:

If we have a real signal: We use it. Something specific, custom-scraped, that ties directly to their problem and our value prop.

**If we don't have a signal:** We treat cold email like a banner ad.

Put the offer in the preview text.
Make it punchy.
No fake personalization.

Think about it: When someone's scrolling on their phone or web browser, they see the subject line + preview text together.

That's your click opportunity.

Stop wasting time generating first lines from company descriptions.

Unless you have custom research that goes well beyond what's publicly available on their homepage, foregoing AI personalization and writing punchy, split-tested first lines performs better.

We've seen this deteriorate over the past six months across our customer base and as someone that would suggest it based on the data, I want to correct the record and say we're out of it now.

### 103. 7399084460085891074

- URL: https://www.linkedin.com/posts/outboundphd_ive-been-completely-wrong-about-ai-prompting-activity-7399084460085891074-sfG1
- Author: 🦾Eric Nowoslawski (@outboundphd)
- Posted: 2025-11-25T14:00:03.626Z

#### Verbatim Content

I've been completely wrong about AI prompting, and it's cost me thousands of dollars and it might be costing you money too. 

For months, I've been telling my team (and all of you) to structure prompts like this:

1. Tell the AI what you want it to do
2. Give it the data to analyze and reference
3. Provide examples of good outputs

I didn't realize that this approach wastes tokens. Especially when you're running outbound campaigns at scale.

I know this may seem obvious to technical people out there but I didn't even consider making a change. 

I had a call with my enterprise rep from OpenAI last week and they asked me why I wasn't using cached inputs based on my use case. 

I didn't even consider it because we use the Clay OpenAI integration.

Cached inputs save 50% on input tokens for GPT-4o mini, and 90% on GPT-4 when the input stays the same.

For those of us using AI for outbound messaging, this is exactly what we're doing. Running the same prompt structure over and over with different prospect data.

But the prompt needs to stay the same for as long as possible for the inputs to cache.

My old approach was mixing up the prompt by putting variable data (company descriptions, prospect info) in the middle.

The correct way:

Put everything that changes at the very bottom of your prompt.

Keep the instructions, examples, and framework identical at the top.

Only vary the prospect-specific data at the end.

I met with Jeff Barg on the Clay team directly about this and you shouldn't have to change all your integrations. The prompt will cache automatically with how they have the integration set up. 

This might seem obvious to technical people, but this is the ignorance debt you pay when you're not technical.

I'm sorry I made the wrong suggestion for so long.

If you're running AI-powered outbound campaigns and not using cached inputs correctly, you're burning money.

### 104. 7397634961580908544

- URL: https://www.linkedin.com/posts/outboundphd_heres-how-we-custom-coded-a-process-to-make-activity-7397634961580908544-89JC
- Author: 🦾Eric Nowoslawski (@outboundphd)
- Posted: 2025-11-21T14:00:16.252Z

#### Verbatim Content

Here's how we custom coded a process to make sure all of our cold email campaigns are full of leads and get warnings when they aren't.

For years, it was the same manual process: download CSVs, upload into Clay, repeat.

So we built a system using Cursor, Supabase, Clay, and Railway.

Here are the inputs:

- Campaign ID
- Link to TAM list
- Webhook to Clay

Supabase stores:
- Campaign record
- Job record
- Scraped contacts

Cursor handles the scraping:
- Scrapes all contacts from the provided link
- Stores them in Supabase

The nightly worker (runs on Railway at 2AM ET):

Checks all campaigns + jobs.

Decides how many leads to send to Clay webhook.

Batch size options: 500 / 5,000 / 50,000 per day. really anything that you want

Tracks whether each contact was processed or used before.

Clay staging → processing:

Staging in Clay ensures the contact has never been used for that client.

Approved contacts move to the processing table (cleaning + enrichment).

Auto-delete on the Clay table keeps the system running autonomously.

Smartlead:

Leads flow into active campaigns automatically.

The alerts:

Supabase sends a Slack message when all contacts are processed: "All contacts processed" warning.

This tells us it's time to add more leads or the campaign will run dry.

Optionally for you, we use ClickUp analytics to verify leads are being added.

The outcome: we never have to manually upload CSVs again, and we get proactive warnings before campaigns run out of leads.

How are you currently managing lead intake for your cold email campaigns?

### 105. 7398725868715606016

- URL: https://www.linkedin.com/posts/outboundphd_heres-how-you-can-train-a-claude-skill-to-activity-7398725868715606016-q-cT
- Author: 🦾Eric Nowoslawski (@outboundphd)
- Posted: 2025-11-24T14:15:08.781Z

#### Verbatim Content

Here's how you can train a Claude Skill to replace your knowledge work in less than an hour.

Most people haven't tried this and it's far easier than you think. 

Step 1: Record tons of context on what you want

I create a Loom video or voice memo (usually while walking my dog) explaining:

- What I want the skill to do
- What decisions it needs to make
- What outputs I'm looking for

Takes 10-15 minutes max.

Step 2: Give it real training data

This is the key. Don't create hypothetical examples.

Use actual work you've already done that represents good outputs.

For my cold email reviewer skill, I used:
- Real prompts we've created for customers
- Actual Clay tables we've built
- Campaign copy that's performed well

Step 3: Test and iterate

If you don't like the outputs, just edit the Claude Skill. Again, using something like Wispr Flow to speak directly to the model so you don't waste time typing. 

Add more training data.
Adjust your instructions.
Don't overthink it.

Step 4: Replace yourself

Now you have a skill that makes decisions the way you would, trained on your actual methodology.

I've made Claude Skills for:
- Campaign strategy generation
- Apollo List filter building
- Suggesting Clay AI prompts based on use cases
- A skill to help write LinkedIn posts

You don't need to be technical. Just talk to Claude about what you want, give it real examples, and iterate.

What knowledge work are you going to train a skill for?

We've recently been running free tests for people that want to work with us to prove that our system can generate leads for them. This free test was a fun one. 

We helped a custom shelf design company book sales calls with Interior Designers. 

Here's the process we used to launch everything in under 15 minutes. 

First, the copywriting

__________________

Hi {{first_name}} - saw {{company_name}}'s {{googe rating}} star Google review for Interior Designs and wanted to reach out. 

We’re the U.S. team for a custom-engineered modular closet system called ____________- designers use us to get a non–cookie-cutter look, but “custom without being custom.”

I saw that project you did with {{case study project}} and thought you might have other {{type of project}} projects that could use custom closets. 

Would it be worth a chat to see how we can partner on projects together? 

%signature%
_______________________

We used a RapidAPI endpoint to do the Google Map Scraping for the reviews. 

Once we had the list of companies, we used Claygent to find the contact of the owner or just the owner's name. 

Then we used Claygent to find a recent project on their site to put the rest of the email together. 

This whole campaign was launched from a 15 minute call and then maybe 20 minutes of Clay table building. 

This allows us to give people a chance to see what a campaign ROI could possibly look like before they pay us a dime. 

If you have a TAM of over 50k contacts, have an LTV of $5,000, and want to try some outbound, connect with 🎯 DK Kim to set up a test with us on the house!

### 107. 7396913907807862784

- URL: https://www.linkedin.com/posts/outboundphd_i-dont-see-enough-people-in-marketing-talking-activity-7396913907807862784-Clpz
- Author: 🦾Eric Nowoslawski (@outboundphd)
- Posted: 2025-11-19T14:15:03.636Z

#### Verbatim Content

I don't see enough people in marketing talking about Claude Skills and I think it's wildly impactful for knowledge work. I'll give you one of my favorites in this post. 

Claude Skills is basically a really easy way to create training for an AI model. 

Explain what you want to accomplish, give it data to train off of what you would do manually, and it creates a markdown text file to keep doing the skill. 

For months, I've been trying to build an email copywriting bot. It worked great for some customers, but inconsistent for others. Frustrating as hell.

Then Claude Skills dropped.

We trained a Claude Skill on our internal campaign strategy docs, my favorite writers, and our best-performing campaigns.

Now here's what it does:

You give it a website URL as input.

It scans for signals that indicate the company has a problem we can solve.

Then it ranks campaigns:
- Best signal (most exclusive, highest intent)
- Second best signal
- Third best signal
- Fallback campaign (for everyone in your ICP without specific signals)

It scores each campaign and gives you a mini-analysis on how to approach the copywriting.

It also includes the first draft of the email for each signal as well as follow up email ideas. 

This is what I mean when I say Claude Skills is a powerful replacement for knowledge work.

It's not just generating generic outputs. It's making strategic decisions based on your specific methodology.

Comment "skills" if you want me to send you the campaign strategy file to see how it works. If you don't like the way my copywriting is trained, upload your own copy to Claude and retrain it yourself!

What knowledge work are you still doing manually that could be trained into a skill?

### 108. 7397000742852792320

- URL: https://www.linkedin.com/posts/outboundphd_how-we-ensure-were-always-using-healthy-activity-7397000742852792320-tDW6
- Author: 🦾Eric Nowoslawski (@outboundphd)
- Posted: 2025-11-19T20:00:06.724Z

#### Verbatim Content

How we ensure we're always using healthy domains in all of our campaigns  automatically with Cursor.

Here's the problem: domains get flagged as unhealthy (from the audit in my last post), but they're still sitting in active campaigns.

Manually removing them and swapping in new ones? That's hours of work across dozens of campaigns.

So we automated it with Cursor.

Here's the workflow:

First, we run the deliverability audit (I covered this in my previous post).

This gives us three domain statuses:
- Active (healthy, ready to use)
- Insurance (backup domains)
- Canceled (unhealthy, needs removal)

Cursor hits the Smartlead API to:
- Pull all active campaigns
- Pull all domains/inboxes attached to each campaign

Then Cursor checks Supabase to:
- Get the status of each domain (Active/Insurance/Canceled)
- Find unused Active domains available for swapping

The swap logic:

Cursor removes any domains marked Insurance or Canceled from campaigns.

Counts how many were removed.

Replaces them with Active-unused domains.

If there aren't enough Active-unused domains, Cursor:
- Redistributes domains from other campaigns
- Or flags that new inboxes must be purchased

We have a human approval step before any changes go live.

Then Cursor writes updates back to:
- Smartlead (campaign domain assignments)
- Supabase (notes + final state)

The result: every campaign is always running on healthy inboxes without manual swaps.

How do you currently handle inbox rotation across multiple campaigns?

### 109. 7396196675159085057

- URL: https://www.linkedin.com/posts/outboundphd_heres-how-we-use-cursor-to-know-which-domains-activity-7396196675159085057-sxMC
- Author: 🦾Eric Nowoslawski (@outboundphd)
- Posted: 2025-11-17T14:45:02.047Z

#### Verbatim Content

Here's how we use Cursor to know which domains are healthy vs. unhealthy and remove them automatically and replace them live in our campaigns.

Most people wait until their cold email campaigns tank to check deliverability. By then, it's too late.

We built an automated health scoring system that flags problems before they kill reply rates.

Here's how it works:

The Smartlead API pulls our metrics:
- Sends last 2 weeks
- Sends last week  
- Sends this week
- Reply rate last 2 weeks
- Reply rate last week
- Reply rate this week

Cursor uses Python to:
- Compare reply rates to our baseline
- Flag domains as "suggested cancel" if they're consistently underperforming
- Flag "warning" when reply rate drops sharply week-over-week

Then we have a human review checkpoint.

Once approved, Cursor updates Supabase with domain status:
- Active (healthy and running)
- Insurance (backup domains ready to swap in)
- Canceled (removed from rotation)

The insurance logic is key:

We keep at least 50% of our active volume in insurance domains. Goal is 100%.

Cursor even suggests how many inboxes to buy based on current health trends.

The outcome: fully automated health scoring with team-wide visibility in Supabase.

No more guessing which domains are working. No more manual spreadsheet hell.

We see the health of every domain in real-time and act before campaigns suffer.

What's your current process for monitoring domain health?

### 111. 7394014800500142081

- URL: https://www.linkedin.com/posts/outboundphd_made-this-video-explaining-how-you-can-use-activity-7394014800500142081-QZSO
- Author: 🦾Eric Nowoslawski (@outboundphd)
- Posted: 2025-11-11T14:15:02.591Z

#### Verbatim Content

Made this video explaining how you can use Cursor to scrape Google Maps and stop messing with custom coding it yourself. Here's the overall guideline to get this done below. 

Why Scrape Google Maps?

LinkedIn isn’t enough: Some businesses, especially local ones, lack a LinkedIn presence.

Google Maps is universal: Almost every legitimate business is listed here.

This method is ideal for local businesses or industries with limited LinkedIn activity.

Tools You'll Need:
Cursor: An AI-powered coding assistant and development environment.

Google Maps Extractor API:
RapidAPI version: Affordable and reliable.
Serper.dev, Outscraper, or similar APIs are also compatible.

CSV of ZIP codes or locations: Optional, but useful for targeted searching.

Clay or CRM: For storing and using your scraped data in email workflows.

Step-by-Step Instructions
1. Get Your API Access Set Up
Visit RapidAPI.com's Google Maps Extractor page (or your preferred API provider).

Subscribe to a suitable plan (e.g., $100/mo for 500,000 requests).

Copy your API key from the provider.

Note: One API request = 1 Google Maps page of results.

2. Open Cursor and Start a Project

Launch Cursor and create a new workspace.
Use natural language prompts like:
"Help me set up a workflow to search Google Maps for businesses in a CSV of ZIP codes. Use the RapidAPI Google Maps Extractor endpoint."
Cursor will write the necessary code for you (this might be Python, JavaScript, etc.).

3. Teach Cursor the API Endpoint
In Cursor, paste in the API documentation from RapidAPI (e.g., the search or locate endpoint).

Show it the input parameters and expected response format.

This improves Cursor’s ability to generate valid code.

4. Build Your Scraping Workflow
Provide prompts like:
"Loop through this list of ZIP codes, perform a Google Maps search for 'restaurants', and save the business name, address, category, and phone number to a CSV."

Modify as needed: e.g., "Go 3–5 pages deep" or "also fetch review data".

Let Cursor adjust the code and test locally within the tool.

5. Once you’ve scraped your data:

Export it into a clean CSV.
Import it into Clay to find owner email and contact information.

### 114. 7392214260241829890

- URL: https://www.linkedin.com/posts/outboundphd_when-targeting-enterprise-companies-with-activity-7392214260241829890-AGbM
- Author: 🦾Eric Nowoslawski (@outboundphd)
- Posted: 2025-11-06T15:00:20.341Z

#### Verbatim Content

When targeting Enterprise companies with automated outbound, this playbook has been our go to for over 2 years and still performs to this day. This is the line we make in Clay. 

Hey {{first_name}} - reaching out because your profile says {{custom reason based on their profile and your solution}}. Should I be reaching out to {{other team member in same department 1}} or {{other team member in same department 2}} instead? 

I love it for multiple reasons.

1) makes a great "break up email"
2) adds in that same thing you would normally do if you were manually writing the message and explaining why you picked them in the first place. 
3) If you're trying to target the marketing person that owns the website at an enterprise company, how on Earth would you ever distinguish which "Head of Marketing" is correct. 

This allows you to still send custom messaging (usually in follow up emails) to your prospects and usually 1 of 4 things happens.

1) They are the right person and they respond because they saw the extra care in the email.
2) They confirm that one of those 2 people are the right people.
3) They say "actually it's neither of them, reach out to this person"
4) They ignore you which was what they could have done anyway. 

Some things to keep in mind. If you run this for small companies, you can go outside their department. 

If you do this for large companies you should really stay within the same department and as much as you can name drop people in the same state to increase the odds that they know their names.

### 116. 7391836819241476096

- URL: https://www.linkedin.com/posts/outboundphd_not-every-cold-email-needs-to-be-personalized-activity-7391836819241476096-lvwR
- Author: 🦾Eric Nowoslawski (@outboundphd)
- Posted: 2025-11-05T14:00:31.394Z

#### Verbatim Content

Not every cold email needs to be personalized like crazy. Take this campaign for example where we were getting pretty good traction with only 1 AI snippet. 

We mainly focused on a couple of things. 

1) Making sure the preview text was something that would get them to open. We opted to ask a question that definitely was relevant to them (and if it wasn't they could just ignore us). 

Don't mess with taking a chance on AI when options like this are available. 

2) Oren Klaff talks in his books about how important it is to use a buyer's own language so they know you understand the problem. Increasing NOI is something these managers are always worried about for their investors. 

3) Light touch of AI that if they read this far, might as well give them something interesting and get them to think about how the platform could help them and drop a local mall that people could spend their gift cards at.

### 118. 7391112049906438144

- URL: https://www.linkedin.com/posts/outboundphd_how-were-thinking-about-getting-the-reachable-activity-7391112049906438144-IbNB
- Author: 🦾Eric Nowoslawski (@outboundphd)
- Posted: 2025-11-03T14:00:32.919Z

#### Verbatim Content

How we're thinking about getting the reachable TAM with Clay and OpenRouter, Inc on behalf of our GEX customers. 

Many of these new tools popping up do a really great job of getting 25, 100, or even 1,000 companies in your TAM but they can't get EVERYTHING and if they do it's really expensive. 

Here's how we are thinking about it. (And when I say everything, I mean the 80% of your TAM that is collectible with LinkedIn or Google Maps data). 

The main issue with making any list is most inputs for your list are scraped and self reported by the company setting up their profile. 

On LinkedIn, a CEO (if you're lucky) or most likely the marketing intern creates the LinkedIn company page and self-selects what industry they're in, causing a ton of problems in industry filtering down the line. 

The same goes for Google Maps but not as bad. 

SaaS companies that serve the real estate industry mark themselves as real estate industry companies. And real estate companies do things like incorrectly put themselves as investment firms all the time. 

So what to do? 

Clay actually makes it really easy to find a huge list of companies and filter by a ton of LinkedIn Industries. 

So what we've been trying is to 

1) score their Do not contact list (their customer list) for all industries that their customers self report that they are in. 

Use AI to also analyze the company descriptions that keep coming up to make a list of good keyword fits as well. 

2) Put any industry filter that a company self selected as their linkedin company industry as one search. 

Another search that uses the keyword matching in case that's broad enough. 

Another search with the Clay AI sub industry and derived data filters. 

3) Manually review which looks the best that is broad enough but still high quality. 

4) Using the best list source, clean the companies with Open Router OSS 20B model (1/3rd price of 4o mini for the intelligence of o3-mini). 

Filter for a company TAM that we can then find contacts from. 

The AI needs some back and forth for sure and to make the AI prompt, we use the Clay sourced data to manually find good fits and bad fits and use those examples in a ChatGPT window that can create the Openrouter json request for us. 

That way we can try to capture as much TAM as possible that is still qualified with an AI that has reasoning capabilities.

### 119. 7389730313494749184

- URL: https://www.linkedin.com/posts/outboundphd_everyone-in-our-bubble-is-on-the-lookout-activity-7389730313494749184--cNL
- Author: 🦾Eric Nowoslawski (@outboundphd)
- Posted: 2025-10-30T18:30:01.287Z

#### Verbatim Content

Everyone in our bubble is on the lookout for the outbound strategy that will give them the edge in cold email, but some industries are receiving emails like it's 2016 and old tactics still crush. 



For industries like restaurant owners, property managers, manufacturing businesses, warehouses (basically NOT SaaS and Ecom founders) we still see increases in reply rates when you 

- Drop a local restaurant or family friendly venue into the messaging. 
- Name other people on the team in the same department
- Drop a PS about their top review and give them kudos
- Lead the email with their company phone number if you're targeting home services businesses. 

And other silly things that crushed back in the day but are trite now. 

There's some kind of email sophistication curve based on the general technical skill of the industry/who is targeting them that can still be exploited.

### 120. 7391466822472138752

- URL: https://www.linkedin.com/posts/outboundphd_i-was-just-thinking-about-how-far-we-have-activity-7391466822472138752-sK9g
- Author: 🦾Eric Nowoslawski (@outboundphd)
- Posted: 2025-11-04T13:30:17.290Z

#### Verbatim Content

I was just thinking about how far we have come since AI. Here's a list of data points that used to be cutting edge and are now so easy with AI. 

When I first started my agency, one of the things we were asked to do was score a company for any signs of GDPR or SOC II compliance. That used to be a crap ton of google search operators (that each cost .01 per call). 

Now you can use a website markdown like Firecrawl to get what you need and score it with AI.

I had another customer that wanted to know 

1) if a company offered phone support and if so
2) what times were they available. 

Again, a google search nightmare and running tons of if/then formulas to parse out the times that they were available on the phone. 

Now you can easily just ask Claygent to get this information for you from the homepage. 

We also had someone that wanted to take a screenshot of an ecom companies sustainability page and put it in the email. 

Wow so many problems. Finding the page, taking the screenshot, saving the screenshot, finding screenshots that had pop ups and deleting them, etc. 

Now browser automation companies can take care of all that. I'd still find the page with Claygent to get down on that compute time and then have Browserbase navigate to the page, make sure there are no pop ups and then take a screenshot. 

What's possible for sales and marketing teams is just getting crazier and crazier with all the new AI tools.

### 121. 7389662376477949953

- URL: https://www.linkedin.com/posts/outboundphd_serper-and-clay-make-it-too-easy-to-filter-activity-7389662376477949953-ro4a
- Author: 🦾Eric Nowoslawski (@outboundphd)
- Posted: 2025-10-30T14:00:03.840Z

#### Verbatim Content

Serper and Clay make it too easy to filter and personalize emails based on a business's Google Reviews. This is the step by step on how we do it. 

When you pull your lists, everyone will give you the company website, but for most platforms to get the company reviews, you need a Google CID. 

Since we are usually targeting local businesses for this, we will use the Serper Google places scraper and literally just enter the website of the domain we are looking to find review for. 

Then we filter the json for the output that has the same exact website as the input. Even when searching just for the company website, you'd be amazed at how often some company's SEO is so good it beats the other company even for a direct website look up. 

Now this gives us the CID. With that we make another Serper call that gets us the reviews. 

They have filters in the API to make it easy to sort by latest, highest rated, lowest rated, etc so if you're are trying to mention the most recent review or the lowest review you can do it in one API call. 

Really nice workflow to reach out to local businesses.

### 122. 7389296216779468800

- URL: https://www.linkedin.com/posts/outboundphd_this-is-what-i-wish-i-knew-about-bounce-rates-activity-7389296216779468800-pYrq
- Author: 🦾Eric Nowoslawski (@outboundphd)
- Posted: 2025-10-29T13:45:04.565Z

#### Verbatim Content

This is what I wish I knew about bounce rates when I first started. When I first started, I thought all bounces happened because the email address didn't exist. 

But there are so many other reasons for bounce issues and all can help you understand if email deliverability is a problem for your campaigns. 

1) Erol Toker and I talk about this all the time that people marking your email as spam is by far the worst thing for your sender reputation. Repeated bounces is second worst BUT Erol and I in different experiments purposely bounced a ton of emails and landed in inbox the next day. 

So still goes to show that getting marked as spam is by far the worst. 

2) Bounces happen from hard bounces and soft bounces. Hard bounces are those bounces where the email is incorrect. Soft bounces can happen for a number of reasons. But if you see status code 550, that means your inboxes are being flagged as spam and the recipients aren't letting it come through (even to land in spam). 

3) another reason you could be getting bounces (that can get up to literally 100%) is if you're sending from outlook and they throttle your sending because they think as an email sender that you're sending spam. 

So in your email sending platform like Instantly or Smartlead, the bounces will sky rocket which is why you want to keep the sender bounce protection and domain threshold rate limit turned on to avoid this. 

Bounces aren't just incorrect emails, they help you know which domains are fried and which are still healthy.

### 123. 7390024911999778816

- URL: https://www.linkedin.com/posts/outboundphd_using-claygentzenrows-to-grab-pricing-page-activity-7390024911999778816-lfdd
- Author: 🦾Eric Nowoslawski (@outboundphd)
- Posted: 2025-10-31T14:00:39.044Z

#### Verbatim Content

Using Claygent/ZenRows to grab pricing page information is a ton of fun and adds some cool touches to outbound campaigns. 

We made this workflow for a product video company targeting SaaS founders. 

We knew that they were getting pitched by a ton of other agencies so we wanted a way to be able to stand out. We thought that tying the video to plan upgrade revenue would justify the cost of the video and stand out from the crowd. 

The way we did it was to 

1) - use Claygent to find the pricing page. You could simplify this workflow and have Claygent summarize the pricing for you as well, but sometimes Claygent doesn't get all of the nuance of the page. It gets a summary.

So, what we did instead was have Claygent find the pricing page and then scrape it with Zenrows so we get all of the text from the pricing page that we needed. 

2) Use a regular AI column with some prompts and 3-4 manually written examples to write the sentences you see in the screenshot. 

Every single line actually came from the linkedin company description and that first scrape of the pricing page. It wasn't complicated at all to do the data sourcing. 

I think if you wanted to do this even cheaper you could use Clay's sitemap tool to find any page with pricing in it and then scrape that with Zenrows, but Claygent is already so cheap that this worked perfectly. 

All we had to do was load the campaign up with the custom snippets and catch the positive replies. 

Just like a claygent to find case studies, I think finding pricing information about a company has broad implications for helping tailor your messaging.

### 124. 7388922561776214016

- URL: https://www.linkedin.com/posts/outboundphd_this-email-is-an-example-of-how-personalized-activity-7388922561776214016-AG9L
- Author: 🦾Eric Nowoslawski (@outboundphd)
- Posted: 2025-10-28T13:00:18.270Z

This email is an example of how personalized your messaging can get and you don't have to spend a ton of Clay credits to do it. 80% of what you see here is generated just from the prospect's website content. 

We collect the data using ZenRows and Claygent. 

The real work is training the AI prompt with 3-5 examples of what we would say when presented with the information manually so it can look great in the output.

### 125. 7389020645088702464

- URL: https://www.linkedin.com/posts/outboundphd_scraping-instagram-profiles-and-changing-activity-7389020645088702464-uLcW
- Author: 🦾Eric Nowoslawski (@outboundphd)
- Posted: 2025-10-28T19:30:03.154Z

#### Verbatim Content

Scraping Instagram profiles and changing your messaging based on how much engagement, followers, and content a local business has is simple with Clay and Apify. 

We're doing this for a couple of businesses targeting more local businesses that don't have a LinkedIn presence. 

If they have low engagement, like under 2,000 followers or under 5 likes per post, they get one message. 

If they are hiding their engagement and have less than 5,000 followers then they get a different message and if they have over 10,000 followers and lots of good engagement they get a completely different message. 

Helping us get one positive response for every 172 people we reach out to. 

Step 1: After you have a Clay and Apify account, load up your data. Now most databases don't have an Instagram account profile url so we used Claygent to find it from the website. We also were using ZenRows but we are blowing up our account right now so the concurrency limit was killing us but you could use them too if you're not in a rush.

Step 2: We connected an Apify Actor that can scrape the IG profile of anything in the input and get us their last 2 posts. 

Step 3: We use formulas in Clay to make a determination on what message they are going to get. Literally, just looking at the math of what kind of engagement that they get and using if-then statements to pick the messaging so we keep our AI cost down and there's no room for AI to make a mistake. 

Perfect use for formulas in clay rather than AI.

Then it's as easy as loading the campaign into Smartlead where every prospect is getting a different messaged based on their IG engagement and of course AI generations based on their current posts.

Super fun one and very impactful for this customer.

### 126. 7388560240247480320

- URL: https://www.linkedin.com/posts/outboundphd_a-scheduled-task-i-have-chat-gpt-run-every-activity-7388560240247480320-CkIM
- Author: 🦾Eric Nowoslawski (@outboundphd)
- Posted: 2025-10-27T13:00:34.086Z

#### Verbatim Content

A scheduled task I have chat gpt run every day that saves me money is checking the domain registrars for sales. 

It doesn't happen all the time, but every now and again, domain registrars like Porkbun and Dynadot will have sales (we only buy .co and .com domains) on domains that are usually $13 per domain. 

We use these sales to beef up the domains for our customers so even if we don't have inboxes on the domain we at least own the domain and it's starting to age in our account. 

Scheduling that task in chatgpt to get a daily report if any of the top registrars are having sales alerts me so I know when the sales are happening and I don't have to check all the time. (p.s. Godaddy NEVER has sales). 

Might be helpful for those of you running outbound and know that your domain expense can get out of hand here and there.

### 128. 7387197374445813760

### 129. 7381682291540008960

- URL: https://www.linkedin.com/posts/outboundphd_one-niche-tip-weve-found-for-claygent-tell-activity-7381682291540008960-Csql
- Author: 🦾Eric Nowoslawski (@outboundphd)
- Posted: 2025-10-08T13:30:03.328Z

#### Verbatim Content

One niche tip we’ve found for Claygent: tell it not to use its full capabilities.

Claygent works broadly by creating a Google search, finding links, then visiting websites to complete tasks. Normally that’s great, but sometimes it stops too early or gets distracted on the wrong page.

We found that if you tell it not to go to the website and just keep researching via Google searches, results are actually better.

Why? Google has already built the most powerful scraper in the world. Claygent tapping into it gives you clean, surface-level data fast.

Example: A customer asked us to find school contacts (Principal, Superintendent, Athletic Director, etc.). 

If Claygent goes on the site, it might grab just the Principal and stop. But if you force it to stay on Google and try multiple search operators, it surfaces many more of them.

Tip: Use ChatGPT or Metaprompter to help you build prompts with multiple Google search operators baked in.

This method is especially useful for finding contacts that don’t have LinkedIn profiles, schools are a perfect use case.

### 130. 7384581480993275904

- URL: https://www.linkedin.com/posts/outboundphd_3-things-we-have-changed-in-our-email-deliverability-activity-7384581480993275904-aRlj
- Author: 🦾Eric Nowoslawski (@outboundphd)
- Posted: 2025-10-16T13:30:23.958Z

#### Verbatim Content

3 things we have changed in our Email Deliverability workflow at Growth Engine X and 2 things we are keeping the same that I think everyone should think about doing. 

1) Cleaning up bad domains with Smartlead API and reordering on Hypertide.io

Smartlead upgraded their API. Now we can get the exact reply rate statistics on a domain level for any time period that we want. 

We're sending this data to Supabase and then writing SQL queries to figure out which domains we should be canceling and which domains we should be keeping. 

2) Sending to Google first and then harder to reach inboxes when campaigns are falling flat. Leadmagic just made a tool called inboxriskscore(.)com you can use to identify easy vs. hard domains to send emails to. 

We've also been seeing that Outlook/Proof Point is harder to break into. Nick Abraham released a report that 10% of his replies at his agency are coming from Outlook. 

The only thing that we've seen consistently combat these low inbox placements on these harder-to-reach inboxes is letting the domains age for a longer time.

 But customers can't usually wait for 4-8 weeks to start an email campaign.

Start with a regular 2-week warmup, sending to Google inboxes, and then start bringing in the Outlook proof point inboxes if necessary. 

3) Auto batching inboxes into campaigns to keep within domain rate limit threshold. Smartlead implemented a domain rate limit threshold to protect Azure sending accounts but it messed up our sending volume. 

We easily use Cursor now to plug into our Supabase to find relevant accounts and divide them appropriately across campaigns so that ever hypertide domain is dedicated to one campaign and one campaign only. 

Things that are staying the same. 

1) Continue to keep open tracking off. Cancelling open tracking test and moving to just reply tracking. 

I'm still not completely against open tracking, but for the past four to six weeks that we've been trying the experiment, we saw positive responses go down on the days that we were tracking opens, and reply rate was just as good of a metric to be watching. 

So we'll cancel using this test as a systemic thing that we automatically do every week and only turn it on as needed. 

2) Buying capacity with Hypertide to send 5,000 emails per day even if a client needs 1,000 per day just to make sure we always have reserve and they age much longer. 

Still keeping 50% of sending capacity in warming reserve at full volume.

Always keep more than you need in reserve so you can try as best as possible to not get caught with no warmed up healthy inboxes. 

Anything you're trying lately that you would add?

### 131. 7384272785747116033

- URL: https://www.linkedin.com/posts/outboundphd_this-is-how-can-are-trying-to-make-landing-activity-7384272785747116033-NyKs
- Author: 🦾Eric Nowoslawski (@outboundphd)
- Posted: 2025-10-15T17:03:45.276Z

#### Verbatim Content

This is how can are trying to make landing in the inbox inevitable for our customers at Growth Engine X.

Some first principles that guide me in this process decision making. 

1) no matter how good your campaign is, people are going to mark you as spam and keeping back up infrastructure is necessary. 

2) Some providers like proofpoint and outlook can't be beat with just 2 weeks of warmup. Longer warmup times for these inboxes are necessary. 

3) We need to be catching bad domains as they happen so that we can cycle in our back up infrastructure, cancel those bad domains, and reorder new ones to keep a healthy stack of domains. 

What we do about each below.

Every customer that joins us gets an infrastructure that can send 5,000 emails per day off the bat. Most of our customers start by sending 500-1500 emails per day. Setting up more than necessary from the beginning allows us to 

- Scale volume immediately if a successful campaign is found
- Always have back up infrastructure in case the first batch of inboxes goes wrong. 
- Allow for inboxes to reach a 4 week warming to have a higher chance of breaking into Outlook/Proofpoint inboxes. Some inboxes even get 2 months of warming before we ever use them. 

This investment early on allows us to be prepared for 1 and 2. 

If a customer does ramp up to 5,000 a day, we buy another batch of inboxes capable of sending 2,500 emails per day and start that warmup. 

Hypertide.io is our main partner here.

We leverage the Smartlead API and Supabase to catch bad domains. 

Every Friday, we pull a report of the last 3 weeks of sending per domain. The bottom 10% of domains based on reply rate are cancelled and reordered with fresh domains from Hypertide. 

If we think a customer is having more deliverability issues than just the bottom 10% of domains, we will go even further. 

Basically, investing early, keeping inboxes in reserve, and weekly reply rate monitoring are some recent things we have implemented to try to stay ahead of deliverability issues. 

And because Hypertide makes it so easy to order inboxes, anyone of any size can follow this with an automated system or just a weekly manual report.

### 132. 7380957553440223232

- URL: https://www.linkedin.com/posts/outboundphd_i-was-asked-by-one-of-those-fancy-four-letter-domain-activity-7380957553440223232-Q8x0
- Author: 🦾Eric Nowoslawski (@outboundphd)
- Posted: 2025-10-06T13:30:12.300Z

#### Verbatim Content

I was asked by one of those fancy four-letter-domain tech companies: “What are other people using AI for in outbound?”

Here’s a better question:

 👉 What is your team manually doing in 10 minutes of research on a prospect, and how does that change your messaging?

Here are some AI workflows we’ve built for other companies:

Using Serper to pull Google Maps reviews and adjust messaging based on good/bad reviews.

Using Screenshot(dot)net + Bannerbear to send personalized screenshots of a company’s sustainability page.

Analyzing if a company has employees overseas and adjusting messaging accordingly.

Pulling pricing/plans pages to create product video ideas.

Clicking through a site with Browserbase to see if retail products have insurance protections.

And that’s just a handful.

If your workflows can be copied easily by someone who isn’t your competitor, they’re not deep enough to be valuable in outbound.

Think: what would you research manually about a company in 10 minutes? Then automate that.

### 134. 7378413264277282816

- URL: https://www.linkedin.com/posts/outboundphd_one-of-my-favorite-linkedin-influencers-received-activity-7378413264277282816-a88S

### 135. 7382037136742563840

- URL: https://www.linkedin.com/posts/outboundphd_after-running-millions-of-outbound-emails-activity-7382037136742563840-b78T
- Author: 🦾Eric Nowoslawski (@outboundphd)
- Posted: 2025-10-09T13:00:05.017Z

#### Verbatim Content

After running millions of outbound emails, these are the patterns I see that kill meeting rates.

1. Using a calendar link as your CTA.

 Calendar links feel convenient, but security filters + human behavior both work against them. People are more likely to reply “Sure, let’s chat” than click a random link. Earn the reply first, then offer booking options.

2. Not following up fast enough.

 Speed is leverage. The longer you wait, the colder the lead gets. Reply the same day if you can — it signals you’re paying attention.

3. Not following up enough.

 There’s a balance: don’t spam, but if someone raised their hand once, don’t stop until they book or say no. I positively responded to Rippling and it took that poor soul 8 follow ups for me to book. I'm still sorry but it wasn't my biggest priority. 

4. Not adding value in follow-ups.

 Repeating “just checking in” is lazy. Each touch should reinforce why the call matters: ROI, risk avoidance, case study, new idea. After a few tries, you can even add a kicker (we’ve seen success offering a gift card to qualified prospects at this stage).

5. Not giving booking options.

 Don’t force a link or a back-and-forth. Do both.
 “Would Tuesday at 9am ET or Thursday at 2pm ET work? Or if it’s easier, here’s my calendar.”

 You’re reducing friction while respecting preferences. Some people like booking with a calendly, others are offended by it. I don't get it but appease both people. 

6. Not following up across channels.

 A reply rate isn’t the same as a meeting rate. Sometimes an extra LinkedIn DM or quick phone call takes a warm response over the finish line. People book with people, not just with inboxes.

Just a couple of things we've seen make a difference that are obvious but sometimes we needed to be reminded more than we need to learn.

### 136. 7377023494565564417

- URL: https://www.linkedin.com/posts/outboundphd_one-way-we-closed-6-deals-in-3-months-with-activity-7377023494565564417-pwFK
- Author: 🦾Eric Nowoslawski (@outboundphd)
- Posted: 2025-09-25T16:57:39.573Z

#### Verbatim Content

One way we closed 6 deals in 3 months with 90 minutes of work was to use cold email to expand traditionally inbound channels. Here’s our playbook. 

Admittedly, I’m not the biggest fan of talking to dead cold prospects for our offerings.

Too many people are sending the same “I’m so desperate we will work for free until we get results” kind of message that changes the expectations for prospects when they talk to us and they expect everything to be free. 

I thought, “If we could just get more people into watching my YouTube (where our best customers come from) I could warm them up there and then they’ll reach out when ready.”

So the question is, how do we get a dead cold prospect to see my YouTube. 

It starts with what does everyone who would be serious customers want? 

They could want a number of things, but our best customers want some kind of signal to know they should be reaching out. 

By far the broadest and most useful signal for any b2b company is knowing when a new department leader joins a new company or gets a promotion.
 
So we made a list with Clay of every person new in their role in the past 30 days and included their job title, industry, company headcount, etc. so we could send an email that looked like. 

"Hey, our customers love to target people new in their role. Here’s a list of all of the new people in the United States with a director and above position. I also included the company industry, headcount, description, and state so you could filter for what you want. Just request access to the list so that I know you got it."

Then anyone that would request access to the list we would follow up saying “Hey we actually give this list away every other Monday. Want to be added to the newsletter so you always get this list?”

4/5 people say yes to this in which we add them to the newsletter and send the list as well as sending one of my YouTube videos to get them into our content. 

We used beehiiv to keep the newsletter pumping and of course Clay to get the new hire data on repeat. 

If you have the ability to wait for really great leads to warm themselves up in your other channels, using outbound to get them there can be a great way to leverage cold email.

### 137. 7382418390143123456

- URL: https://www.linkedin.com/posts/outboundphd_databases-getting-contact-data-from-this-activity-7382418390143123456-IKcm
- Author: 🦾Eric Nowoslawski (@outboundphd)
- Posted: 2025-10-10T14:15:02.911Z

#### Verbatim Content

Databases getting contact data from this very platform usually can't build an infrastructure that rescrapes every profile every day. Another thing that Clay helps us with. 

Some data decay is expected from the main data providers. But Clay is using multiple data providers under the hood with their contact profile enrichment. This helps us cut down on reaching out to people that have already moved on to other jobs or received promotions. 

Here's our step by step guide to confirm as best we can that someone works at the company we thought they work at (only 1 Clay credit):

1. Enrich contact for their past experience. 
2. Filter for current experience only with the Clay "filter list of objects" integration
3. Filter by LinkedIn company URL slug (everything after /company/). If it matches, output “work confirmed.”
4. If no match, filter by company domain.
5. If still no match, filter by company name.

This triple filter confirms most contacts at low cost. You want to do the company url, the company domain, and the company name because not everyone diligently keeps their LinkedIn profile updated at all times.

### 138. 7386023478161997824

I've been digging into Cursor and I feel like my life won't be the same without it. Here are 8 things I've built with it just in the last week so maybe you can get building too. 

If you've been following me for a while you know that we use a lot of API calls to make our email campaigns happen. Most of this is done thorugh Clay so I wasn't too keen on trying another tool. 

At Sculpt, I spoke to Brandon Charleson about it and he convinced me to give it a try. And now I can't look back. 

Key Highlights of the Documentation:
1. Campaign Management Workflows to automatically add proper Hypertide.io inboxes to Smartlead campaigns while taking the poor performers out. 

2. Automated Spam Test creation in smartlead on campaigns that drop below 1% overall reply rate not counting out of office. 


3. Analytics and Reporting Pushing Inbox Health Data to Supabase

4. Using Cursor to build instructions for tools like Gumloop or Vercel to host what we just made. 

5. Made a workflow to use the Browserbase API to do a couple of things that Smartlead didn't have APIs for. 

6. Create CSVs automatically of every healthy domain per client for reporting. 

7. MCP Integration with Smartlead (BETA), Airtable, Slack, Supabase, Exa, and Make

8. There's a csv on my computer with 455.6 MB of data in it and I just filter it with Cursor. I'm never opening a large file again. 

This week, we are going to pilot having some of my team using it too. 🦾Lohit Boruah already built a google maps scraper.
