- URL: https://www.linkedin.com/posts/outboundphd_getting-access-to-the-oss-models-in-openrouter-activity-7383856778817617920-bsJW
- Author: 🦾Eric Nowoslawski (@outboundphd)
- Posted: 2025-10-14T13:30:41.495Z

#### Verbatim Content

Getting access to the OSS models in OpenRouter has been a huge unlock for our company and contact scoring. 

Yes I love 4o mini as the cheapest best all around model right now, but when you see the stats of OSS models, it's undeniable you have to at least check it out. 

Pricing for OSS 20B is about 1/3rd per token compared to 4o mini and benchmarks at o3-mini intelligence.  
Pricing for OSS 120B is about 1/2 per token compared to 4o mini and benchmarks at o4-mini intelligence. 

Even counting the tokens that are used for reasoning, OSS 20B has really been great for all of our use cases. We are using 120B only when we feel like we really have to get something right that might be a little complex. 

We just use the HTTP API request in Clay to get access. I emailed them about rate limit constraints and they told me about their flexibility and as we scaled up that has proven to be true.

Even if it didn't, we could just make accounts for each customer as the platform is completely usage pricing based.

### 141. 7386385774780325888

- URL: https://www.linkedin.com/posts/outboundphd_heres-a-workflow-you-can-build-with-clay-activity-7386385774780325888-pzX5
- Author: 🦾Eric Nowoslawski (@outboundphd)
- Posted: 2025-10-21T13:00:01.119Z

#### Verbatim Content

Here's a workflow you can build with Clay/Cursor to scrape a list of everyone engaging with your social posts (or your competitors)...

I'll go over the step by step workflow and then you can take this post to a cursor, claude code, or open ai codex to do the planning and build it for yourself. 

The steps remain the same to be used in Clay, you just have to set up the columns for yourself. Huge benefit of Clay is that it can be pushed to all of your other workflows!

Step 1: Go to RapidAPI(dot)com and make an account. If Apify is the gateway drug of scraping, RapidAPI is the next step. So many amazing APIs that sometimes in my free time I'll just poke around on there. 

Step 2: Check out some of the social platform scrapers that they give you access to. The one I am going to use in this example is the one from Rock APIs. If you search Rock APIs you should find what I'm talking about. 

Step 3: First we need to get the company's post (works just as easily for people too!) You can use this endpoint. 

url 'https://{{NAMEOFAPI}}.p.rapidapi(dot)com/get-company-posts?username=microsoft&start=0

Step 4: That endpoint above will get the posts. Now you just need to get the engagers with the other endpoints they have listed. 

https://{{NAMEofAPI.p.rapidapi(dot)com/get-post-reactions

If you're in Clay you just need to break up the JSON payload into separate rows and enrich from there and if you're on Cursor or something, you can probably take these instructions and dump it into the planning part. 

Go to Rapid API and search Rock API because once you see what I mean, you'll see why I didn't want to post here the name of the API. 

It's super cheap to run and can help you mine social platforms for your ICP that's already engaging with your content, you just need to make a list of them.

### 142. 7379504224256172032

- URL: https://www.linkedin.com/posts/outboundphd_unpopular-opinion-the-best-outbound-campaigns-activity-7379504224256172032-ujnX
- Author: 🦾Eric Nowoslawski (@outboundphd)
- Posted: 2025-10-02T13:15:11.621Z

#### Verbatim Content

Unpopular opinion: the best outbound campaigns that we have seen usually work right away. If they don’t, something needs to change drastically. Here’s what we look at first in all cases:

1. Is our list as correct as we thought it was?

 We do a lot of checks internally: inclusion/exclusion keywords, AI checks, job title matching. Did something slip through the cracks?

2. Is our list correct but our decision to target them with this messaging wrong?

 We talk to our customer and use ChatGPT for deeper analysis. We even look at open job postings to understand what roles are being asked to do.

3. How can we change the messaging of what’s in it for the prospect?

 The overarching value prop isn’t always the best outbound message. Break down individual features and test what resonates.

For example, Clay’s main value prop is connecting to 150+ integrations. That doesn’t resonate in outbound. The best campaign we ran focused on name-dropping customer case studies and showing how to find more companies like them automatically.

4. Are there other buying signals we can add?

 Signals reduce list size and add complexity but they sharpen targeting. Think: what would we research for 10 minutes if we were reaching out manually, and how does it change the message?

5. Have we tried lookalike campaigns?

 They add two benefits:

Messaging comes directly from a case study, so it’s less hypothesis and more storytelling.

Prospects always react better when they know something has already worked for a peer.

From there, it comes down to the biggest insight from Facebook ads: the companies making the most money were doing the most split testing.

Different channel, same principle, keep split testing between lists, messaging, and lead magnets until you find what works.

### 143. 7379870413079744512

- URL: https://www.linkedin.com/posts/outboundphd_there-are-2-reasons-to-hire-an-outbound-agency-activity-7379870413079744512-EBjR
- Author: 🦾Eric Nowoslawski (@outboundphd)
- Posted: 2025-10-03T13:30:17.840Z

#### Verbatim Content

There are 2 reasons to hire an outbound agency. If you don’t know which one you are, that’s where problems occur.

Reason 1: To learn about the channel faster and experiment with new ideas. You want to get started quickly, tap into expertise, and have someone take care of everything while you learn.

Reason 2: You already have something working and want to scale. An agency can give you better economies of scale, automation, error fixes, time savings, and insights from the broader market.

Every company at one point needs to learn about the channel whether they use an agency or not — but not every company is ready to scale it.

The biggest problem in agency relationships? Customers not understanding what they’re paying for if they’ve never done outbound or marketing before. (Founder-led sales transitions are often the scariest).

If you haven’t run campaigns before and think “This company is just going to fix our pipeline issues,” maybe they will, maybe they won’t. You might not know your ICP as well as you think, or you might not have communicated your messaging clearly enough.

If you’re looking to work with any agency and you don’t have the learning mindset first, the relationship could be doomed from the beginning.

### 144. 7381312367307038721

- URL: https://www.linkedin.com/posts/outboundphd_heres-how-to-dynamically-change-the-case-activity-7381312367307038721-h5Is
- Author: 🦾Eric Nowoslawski (@outboundphd)
- Posted: 2025-10-07T13:00:06.518Z

#### Verbatim Content

Here’s how to dynamically change the case study in your outbound emails, without burning OpenAI tokens.

Mentioning the right case study for the right type of company (CPG with 100 employees vs SaaS with 700) is a no-brainer addition to your campaigns. 

I just saw someone uploading all of their case studies to Google Gemini Flash 2.5 (which was a good idea since it does well on the needle in the haystack benchmark). 

But it's still rolling the dice in my opinion and if you have a lot of case studies, it might not output precisely what you want. 

I'd rather use if/then statements to lookup and find the one I want to make my messaging. 

How we do it in Clay:

1. Enrich case studies: Get domain, company name, industry, headcount, and add the sentence you want to output if there’s a match.

 1A. Generate an AI column of keywords so you can distinguish between “retail apparel” vs “retail electronics.”

2. Upload to Clay in the same workbook as your workflow.

3. Enrich your prospect list with the same parameters.

4. Create a lookup key like {{Industry}} :: {{Headcount Range}} for both tables.

5. Lookup multiple rows between tables. Use your custom keywords to break ties.

6. Filter overlaps with AI if needed to pick the best match.

Result: the right case study sentence appears in your outbound automatically without hallucinations or wasted tokens.

### 145. 7386091330138972160

- URL: https://www.linkedin.com/posts/outboundphd_lessons-on-using-claycom-from-their-largest-activity-7386091330138972160-_nxL
- Author: 🦾Eric Nowoslawski (@outboundphd)
- Posted: 2025-10-20T17:30:00.046Z

#### Verbatim Content

As Clay's largest user, we've learned a ton about scaling with the platform.
Here are the 5 lessons I talked about on stage at Sculpt in San Francisco recently. (Link to video at the end). 

Clay Rules & Best Practices

1. Always Always Always Use Conditional Formulas on Paid Integrations.

Seems super simple and obvious but getting your team in this habit helps to prevent unnecessary API calls and only trigger paid integrations when data truly needs enrichment.


2. Save Paid Data Externally

Store enriched data in CRM or Supabase, not inside Clay only.
I pay $30 a month for Supabase and just one of our databases amongst over 20 has 11.4M records.

3. Leverage Scheduled Sources

Automate evergreen campaigns that continuously refresh and send new leads into workflows without manual input. This features is still under used by people that have cracked an outbound campaign for themselves and need to keep it on all the time without forgetting. 

4. The formula generator can really do a lot. If you want the same thing to happen every time it sees an input, try this before using AI. 

Use formulas for:

Cleaning and combining data
If/Then logic
Lead scoring
State abbreviation

Reminder: Clay formulas run on JavaScript, so almost any data transformation is possible.

5. The GEX AI Prompting Framework

Prompting Rules

The 10-Minute Manual Research Rule:
Don't steal other people's playbooks. Think about when you are manually researching a company. What are you looking for if you had 10 minutes to research them? And how does that change your message? 

One Thing at a Time:
Give AI a single, focused task, avoid multitasking prompts.

Safeguards:
Build filters for low-context outputs (e.g., return “purple” when uncertain).

Use Examples:
 Always include examples in prompts for better accuracy.

Meta Prompting:
 Ask ChatGPT “What questions do you have to improve this prompt?” before finalizing.

Reasoning First:
 For high-stakes tasks (like industry classification), ask AI to output reasoning before its final answer.

Handle Edge Cases:
 Be ready to prompt around specific constraints (e.g., tone, banned words, output format, length).

Mindset Takeaway
“AI is the smartest thing in the world built to solve Calculus problems.
 I promise it can write your emails, you just need to give it some TLC.”

Overall: 
 → Think like a system designer, not a button clicker.
 → Use Clay, Supabase, and AI together to build repeatable, data-rich workflows that scale outbound.

https://lnkd.in/eMtqW3Ut

### 146. 7383490469990670336

- URL: https://www.linkedin.com/posts/outboundphd_i-first-posted-about-what-we-were-doing-with-activity-7383490469990670336-Mes1
- Author: 🦾Eric Nowoslawski (@outboundphd)
- Posted: 2025-10-13T13:15:06.665Z

#### Verbatim Content

I first posted about what we were doing with Supabase a couple of months ago. This is the data we are storing since we started storing valid emails. 

1. home page content of a website. We are constantly using this for company scoring, AI generated content, aligning value props and problems, etc. 

2. Catch all and invalid emails. We used to just store the valid emails but have since started keeping a list of domains that are catch all and combinations of name and domains that we ran through our waterfalls already and didn't find an email. Saving bandwidth for net new contacts. 

3. Claygent Case Study data. I love finding case studies on customers or prospects that we're reaching out to, and we are now storing this in Supabase because we found we're still running this over and over in multiple campaigns. So we'd rather just store the data and timestamp it. 

4. Lists of contacts that respond with keywords like "can spam" or curse words. These people will never respond positively, and we just never want to reach out to them again. 

5. Company tagging and summaries. We've been using Open Router a ton to get access to the OSS models for cheaper analysis of companies. We will store that analysis so that we can better differentiate companies that aren't ICP fits. 

Just some more additions to what we're doing so we could keep data that many of our customers would find useful in the future or reduce our costs.

### 147. 7378779468238135297

- URL: https://www.linkedin.com/posts/outboundphd_after-running-outbound-campaigns-as-a-service-activity-7378779468238135297-fVPl
- Author: 🦾Eric Nowoslawski (@outboundphd)
- Posted: 2025-09-30T13:15:16.321Z

#### Verbatim Content

After running outbound campaigns as a service for 4 years now, most of the glitter of new tech is gone for me and we keep coming back to the same principles.

In order to get a positive response in cold email you need 3 things.

An infrastructure to send emails from, a list to send emails to, and a message to send them.

If you’re starting your first outbound campaign, here’s how I’d get set up quickly for myself.

For inbox infrastructure, this is a solved problem. Get an Instantly or Smartlead account and either get inboxes set up through them or go to Zapmail / Hypertide. (Be on the lookout for .co domain sales as that can help save a ton of money).

For the list of companies to send to, get yourself a simple B2B database or Clay account to pull lists. If the platform gives email addresses, make sure to validate them and use a third party software to find more emails.

Leadmagic is the fastest provider in our waterfall so they are first but we also use Prospeo, Get Prospect, IcyPeas, and TryKitt to find and validate emails.

When it comes to making a base level message, personally, I love giving ChatGPT a ton of context about what I’m doing and then asking it what Josh Braun would say in an email. Use the transcribe feature to talk back and forth with the copy for fast editing. 

Add some extra spice and use Clay to try to replicate the workflow you do manually when you research a company for 10 minutes and how it changes your message.

From there, you are about 80% of the way to a campaign that many professional agencies would set up for you.

If you have more questions, I wrote a 112-page doc on how we think about doing this that I’ll link in the comments. You can upload it to your favorite LLM and ask questions about it.

### 148. 7379141799002664960

- URL: https://www.linkedin.com/posts/outboundphd_steps-to-running-the-worst-outbound-campaign-activity-7379141799002664960-h4ED
- Author: 🦾Eric Nowoslawski (@outboundphd)
- Posted: 2025-10-01T13:15:02.707Z

#### Verbatim Content

Steps to running the WORST outbound campaign in history:

Don’t validate any of your leads.

For that matter, don’t use any filters, just target all CEOs.

Write an email that’s 12 sentences long talking in depth about how your features are the best.

Only ask to book a call while providing no explanation on why that call would be worthwhile to them.

Don’t even try to send their first name and get it wrong purposely. Call everyone in the campaign “Jives.”

Send from Mailchimp in HTML format.

Ask everyone to book on a calendar link.

Add a request for payment in bitcoin to make the spam filters happy.

Send from a domain you purchased an hour before launch.

Send 3 emails to them in one day and follow up every day for the next 7 days.

While this is kind of funny to think about, it also helps people remember the best practices when we try to think about how we can do worse at something.

Charlie Munger called this inversion thinking and is a frame I think about when campaigns aren't going our way and I I'm troubleshooting why. 

What would you add for ideas on the worst outbound campaign ever?

### 150. 7387125759213019136

- URL: https://www.linkedin.com/posts/outboundphd_last-week-i-explained-to-the-sell-better-activity-7387125759213019136-y5OT
- Author: 🦾Eric Nowoslawski (@outboundphd)
- Posted: 2025-10-23T14:00:27.156Z

#### Verbatim Content

Last week, I explained to the Sell Better audience that I think AI generated emails fit into 4 tiers. Use these buckets to brainstorm how it could apply to your business. 

We all want the perfect AI generated email but not every offer is capable of getting the best email. 

Top Tier - the famous Jordan Crawford Permissionless Value Prop email. An email so good, they would pay to receive it. Using your own internal data, data you scrape publicly, and AI to make a message that shows you did your research, uncovered a problem, and gave them a taste of the solution, while explaining how you can help more in the future. 

Second Tier - an email based on what you would research about a company for 10 minutes manually and using AI to change your messaging based on that. 

Not as in depth as Jordan's best following similar principles. Think about what you were to research manually about someone, keep track of what sources you check, and how it helps you make decisions on what to say. 

Use the literal messages you sent manually as the training data for your AI. 

Third Tier - Using AI to help someone imagine what it would be like if you were to work together. Simple, but still wildly effective. Basically a liner adding specificity about what you do and how it could specifically help them. 

Fourth Tier - Attention Hacking. Bottom of the barrel you probably stole it from someone on LinkedIn but everyone has to put food on the table so I get it. Especially in industries that don't receive very many sophisticated emails, these can still work. 

We've seen emailing local businesses and name dropping the top steakhouse in the area still increases replies over not doing it. 

So when making AI outbound workflows in Clay or other places. 

First think - is there anything I can send them that's inherently valuable and custom to their business without having to speak to them that I can automate. 

Second - what would I say if I were reaching out manually anyway?

Third - use AI to make the email more specific to their situation and business from information you can easily find on their website. 

Fourth - do what you gotta do haha

Any other ideas for tiers?

### 152. 7366850806832136193

- URL: https://www.linkedin.com/posts/outboundphd_heres-an-honest-to-god-way-to-increase-your-activity-7366850806832136193-Q3k5
- Author: 🦾Eric Nowoslawski (@outboundphd)
- Posted: 2025-08-28T15:15:01.818Z

#### Verbatim Content

Here's an honest to God way to increase your overall marketing lead conversion rates that takes some real work, but is always worth it. 

Now don't eye roll because you know what I'm going to say but having a lead magnet or a front end offer makes all marketing so much smoother. 

Clearbit famously made their API free to be able to convert company names into websites. 

We can launch campaigns for people for free

We have one customer making a suite of 10 videos for free 

These things of course take work but make outbound, inbound, referral, paid, whatever marketing so much easier. 

And if for whatever reason you think you can't make a lead magnet or a front end offer, I really think you can.

I believe that every business has some element of knowledge arbitrage to it. There's something that you know about running the business that your customers don't know. 

Because if they knew everything that you know, they probably wouldn't hire you, your product, buy your product or need a service. 

There has to be something that prospective customers wish that they knew that you could give away for free. 

And giving something great away for free can turn a demand capture campaign (a campaign that really only has a chance of getting positive responses from people who are already in market) into a demand generation campaign. 

Which is where you can get everybody who's on the fence and other people to learn about you and start engaging with them as well, too. 

I'd always rather have too many hand raisers that I have to qualify out than crickets on my messaging.

### 153. 7363566684739145729

- URL: https://www.linkedin.com/posts/outboundphd_the-new-york-times-paraphrased-me-saying-activity-7363566684739145729-5ELa
- Author: 🦾Eric Nowoslawski (@outboundphd)
- Posted: 2025-08-19T13:45:06.095Z

#### Verbatim Content

The New York Times paraphrased me saying Clay is a "powerful but unwieldy tool". That's not what I said and this post is for anyone that thinks it's "unwieldy."

When people get started with Clay, I recommend a crawl, walk, run framework because it's not that Clay is difficult to use. 

There are only 4 things you can do in Clay. 

Import Data, Enrich Data, Use Formulas, and Export Data. 

This isn't the unwieldy part. The fact that you have to onboard on to Clay and ALL the other integrations is where it gets confusing. 

So I tell everyone that they should Crawl, Walk, and then Run with Clay. 

Crawl looks like what I talk about in the video. 

There are workflows that I know you must be doing every day that can be easily automated like pulling leads, validating their emails, and cleaning the company names to be uploaded into a campaign. 

A manual workflow of the past now thanks to Clay. 

"Walk" is to add in some level of AI or if/then segmentation into your campaigns. 

"If VP of finance vs. head of finance, then change messaging this way" type workflows.

"Run" workflows are the workflows that you create when you watch yourself manually reach out to 20 people and think about how to automate the work you did when you were manually reaching out to people and capturing that into your workflow. 

Don't worry about doing workflows with triple double backflips or anything when getting started. Just automate away the low hanging fruit first.

### 157. 7364653831864913922

- URL: https://www.linkedin.com/posts/outboundphd_i-had-the-opportunity-to-teach-a-pavilion-activity-7364653831864913922-fugK
- Author: 🦾Eric Nowoslawski (@outboundphd)
- Posted: 2025-08-22T13:45:02.168Z

#### Verbatim Content

I had the opportunity to teach a Pavilion cohort with Yash Tekriwal 🤔 and here are 11 outbound tips we went over when automating your workflows!

I hit the character limit on this post so if you have any questions about the nuance, don't hesitate to comment as there's for sure a lot here. 

1. "Personalization" should = 10 minutes of what you would manually research
 
The most common mistake is asking “What are other companies doing with AI?” Instead, ask “What should we be doing with AI?”

Think about how you’d research a company for 10 minutes: what you’d check, how those checks change your message (hook, value, CTA), and then how to automate that same research.

Rule of thumb: Claygent can usually handle most of the research you need.

2. Generate AI snippets, not AI emails

Long, “all-in-one” prompts rarely nail everything. It’s much easier to create strong outputs in small pieces (e.g., 2–7 words or one short sentence at a time) and assemble the final message.

3. Don’t ever tell the model you’re writing a “cold email”

Describe the persona and tone you want instead. This avoids the generic “sales-blog” style many models default to.

4. One job per prompt

Every AI in your workflow should do one task at a time. Claygent does one research task. Your writing prompt does one writing task. Combining tasks creates errors and cost creep.

5. Feed actual data into the prompt

Don’t say “This is salesforce.com, write an email.” Give the model the specific facts you’d use yourself.

The more grounded the inputs, the less guessing.

6. Examples are the most important input for your prompts.

Great examples can turn an okay prompt—and an okay system prompt—into something excellent. Curate 2–3 gold-standard input→output examples and keep them fresh. Remove weak ones. Examples anchor style, brevity, and relevance.

7. Always use conditional formulas/run-conditions to protect spend

Only run research when required inputs exist; only run writing when research fields are filled. Guardrails reduce wasted credits and noisy outputs.

8. Dictate your prompts

If typing slows you down, speak your prompt, then ask the model to tighten and clarify it. You’ll capture more nuance, faster.

9. Ask AI to critique your prompt

Paste your draft prompt + goal and ask: “Do you have any follow-up questions to help make this more accurate?” Answer them, then have the model rewrite the prompt with fewer tokens.

10. Model + cost hygiene

We use GPT-4o mini for most tasks, and we lock our API key to that model so the team can’t accidentally run expensive ones. 

Keep prompts short and examples essential to control token spend.

11. Ban filler/jargon with a general system prompt

Keep a fifth-grade reading level, short sentences, and concrete verbs.

Ask ChatGPT to draft a reusable system prompt that bans words like “leverage,” “synergy,” “innovate,” etc., and apply that system prompt to any AI 
that writes email snippets.

### 158. 7371171364055035904

- URL: https://www.linkedin.com/posts/outboundphd_if-you-were-helping-someone-get-started-on-activity-7371171364055035904-5vis
- Author: 🦾Eric Nowoslawski (@outboundphd)
- Posted: 2025-09-09T13:23:22.932Z

#### Verbatim Content

If you were helping someone get started on Clay, what's a piece of *niche* advice you would give that most people don't talk about? Here's some of mine. 

1. Make it a rule across your team that no paid columns can run without a conditional formula. 

2. Replace your templates with their new functions feature. Functions allow you to run a workflow outside of your main Clay table so that if you ever need to change it, you don't need to change 300 Clay tables, you just change the function.

3. Save all paid data to our Supabase (or pick another tool/CRM) so that when you enrich data about a company or contact, you can keep it.

4. Everyone on the team is given practice assignments to scrape and integrate with Apify and ZenRows. These tools give you access to so many different ways of getting new data. 

5. Always have fallback campaigns or lines when we are creating outbound campaigns. Signals and intense filtering are great but outbound still is a numbers game. Keep great campaigns in your back pocket when people don't qualify for your signal campaigns. 

6. You can keep a campaign automatically turned on with a $15/month Hubspot account automatically syncing to Clay so you don't have to think about refilling the campaign as often. 

7. We have a whole set of other niche AI rules that the team has to follow when they do any kind of AI prompting as well which might be better for another post. 

Any other rules you pass of to your team or keep in mind for yourself?

### 160. 7365733444011606016

- URL: https://www.linkedin.com/posts/outboundphd_here-are-all-the-mistakes-i-made-on-my-first-activity-7365733444011606016-ErVU
- Author: 🦾Eric Nowoslawski (@outboundphd)
- Posted: 2025-08-25T13:15:01.762Z

#### Verbatim Content

Here are all the mistakes I made on my first outbound campaign that I thought sent 50,000 emails but no way it sent more than 2,000. 

Back in 2019, I was getting into outbound automation for the first time. 

As many people do, I was compiling a list of all of my target accounts and thought I was doing a great job. 

As soon as I finished, the hospitals reached out to the non-profit that I was working at and asked if there were any way to get the word out to get donations for N95 masks. 

We said we could help and it turned out that we solicited over 100 bulk donations of masks, but in doing so I made every mistake in the book. 

1) I thought I was sending 50,000 emails from my individual inbox on my main domain. 

If you look up the settings on outlook back in 2019, no way I got past 2,000 emails sent. 

2) I didn't validate my list so the bounces on my account were insane. 

For this reason and reason number 1, I couldn't actually email anyone for about a week because all my emails were going to spam. 

3) I had no idea who I was emailing so even though we were comparatively successful to the effort we put in, I emailed a ton of irrelevant people. 

Of course this was all for a good cause and I was only working in a non-profit. 

Funny to look back on this campaign with what I thought I had accomplished and now with what I know today, how much I could have improved and maybe even gotten more donations for the hospitals. 

One of my favorite stories from when I first got started in outbound automation!

### 161. 7358485717183832066

- URL: https://www.linkedin.com/posts/outboundphd_using-data-to-create-signals-in-outbound-activity-7358485717183832066-GsaX
- Author: 🦾Eric Nowoslawski (@outboundphd)
- Posted: 2025-08-05T13:15:09.030Z

#### Verbatim Content

Using data to create signals in outbound campaigns is great and should be done but isn't your silver bullet to a repeatable outbound machine. Refining the offer you use to a cold audience is much more impactful. 

As access to data becomes cheaper and cheaper (remember when you were a genius for automating targeting companies when they raised money) folks on LinkedIn and companies are talking/asking more about how to do outbound campaigns with better signals. 

Which is great, I think everyone should be doing that, but off the top of my head there's two places that signals fail. 

1. If you need more meetings than the signals can find for you in your market.
2. You're chasing a signal that isn't actually public information and you throw away good companies when you look for roundabout ways to get the data.

Just raised funding, new hires enroll, hiring for new team members or company news about a new product launch, etc, are very common signals that I see on LinkedIn and that our customers ask us for. 

Let's say your target market has 100,000 people in it that you can reasonably contact. How many of them at any given point are starting a new job or just got a promotion? 10%? 20%?

No where near enough that you can build a full outbound motion just off of signals. 

Same with point 2. We have one customer that has asked us in the past, "we want to target companies spending $40k+ per month on marketing."

Ok so there's no public way to get that information but I can tell you who has 5 people on their marketing team, is actively running Meta/Google/and LinkedIn ads. 

But what about the company that has 1 marketing person because they use a lot of contractors and is only running Meta ads but under the hood they are spending $100k a month on ads. 

Maybe the argument could be made that some kind of site traffic number should then be used as a proxy but isn't perfect yet again. 

1 thing that we have seen fix both issues is taking the time to figure out a front end offer that your audience resonates with. 

For us, our first campaign is free as a pilot. So we can tell everyone that before they pay us anything, we will prove we can get leads. 

For Clay, to a cold audience, finding case studies or the kinds of customers they sell to based on their website content and showing people how to easily build lookalike lists is the best performing outbound campaign hook.  

A video content agency we work with makes the first 10 videos and payments is collected only after the customer is satisfied. 

Of course, there's lots of ways to skin this cat. And I think more people should be talking about refining their offer to a dead cold audience vs. the next triple double backflip equivalent of an outbound workflow.

### 162. 7360663853354885120

- URL: https://www.linkedin.com/posts/outboundphd_my-gpt-5-nano-vs-4o-mini-analysis-for-cold-activity-7360663853354885120-uqLD
- Author: 🦾Eric Nowoslawski (@outboundphd)
- Posted: 2025-08-11T13:30:17.161Z

#### Verbatim Content

Tested GPT 5 nano vs. 4o mini this weekend and I’m disappointed but I hope I’m wrong. 

This is coming from a person who is:

 1. OpenAI's biggest fan
 2. Heavily a price-based shopper

The reason I'm so heavily price-based is because in my opinion, many of these models are actually getting too smart for the normal outbound use case. 

We don't need them to solve calculus. We just need them to be able to follow instructions as well.

I was doing some testing with Claygent and regular AI, and repeatedly I was getting extremely similar responses on 4o mini and 5 nano, and 5 nano was sometimes 2-3 times the cost. 

At worst, sometimes there's even a 10x the cost. 

The culprit is the fact that it's a reasoning model, and even though the input tokens are the same, the output tokens are completely different, jacking up the prices. 

Since making my YouTube video yesterday people have pointed out I need to lower the reasoning as a setting in the API which is fair. 

I think we will have to tinker with it and turn the reasoning to minimal but even if we do that, it seems in playground it still reasons a little bit which still will affect cost. 

I thought the release of GT 5 was going to help us reduce our cost, but it looks like the release of the open-source models will be even more important for doing so. 

Check out this video where I did a comparison across multiple tests to see what I'm talking about 

https://lnkd.in/eVgwrv2j

### 164. 7363936618447048706

- URL: https://www.linkedin.com/posts/outboundphd_fun-campaign-weve-sent-to-about-5460-folks-activity-7363936618447048706-3coS
- Author: 🦾Eric Nowoslawski (@outboundphd)
- Posted: 2025-08-20T14:15:05.164Z

#### Verbatim Content

Fun campaign we've sent to about 5,460 folks  with a 1 positive response per ~220ish people we contact rate and we are looking to scale up. 

This campaign requires a lot of filtering and tools to make happen. 

We needed to filter the campaign first with AI to make sure that we're reaching out to our customers' ICP. 

They have a pretty wide open target addressable market, but have a lot of companies that could easily be misdirected if you don't clean up with AI. For instance, they want to target e-commerce companies, jewelry stores, plastic surgeons, and a whole other slew of keywords that could very easily get mixed up with staffing and recruiting firms, marketing agencies, and tech companies if you don't clean your list. So first we clean our list with GPT 4o Mini and 7 trained examples in Clay

Then it is very important that they have a retail store so we are searching for the website and filtering for a match on Serper. 

Finally, the messaging hinges on an audit of their social media profiles. We use Open AI and Apify to pull in the data from their instagram/tiktok to audit their profile and make suggestions/offers depending on ranges of post frequency, like counts, and followers. 

Each email has 9 custom variables and is a pretty fun campaign for us to run.

### 165. 7361448963322904578

- URL: https://www.linkedin.com/posts/outboundphd_open-tracking-has-one-really-important-benefit-activity-7361448963322904578-YGpl
- Author: 🦾Eric Nowoslawski (@outboundphd)
- Posted: 2025-08-13T17:30:01.964Z

#### Verbatim Content

Open tracking has one really important benefit that we use it for and it's not knowing who actually opened our email.

Recently, we made the decision to start testing a couple of campaigns with open tracking turned on only on Thursdays and Fridays, and honestly, it's been a great decision. 

If you didn't know, open tracking is essentially a vanity metric. After an Apple iOS update, if somebody has the Mail app on their phone, every email that they receive that lands in their inbox pings the open pixel. 

Sometimes teams will tell us, "Can you get a list of all the people who open the email," and we always tell them, "Hey, let's score them on other things like their website content and other intent signals" because just because there's an open doesn't mean they actually opened the email. 

But what it does give us is data on whether or not our emails are landing in inbox for spam. 

Now I won't say open tracking is perfect, but you can be sure that if one of your inboxes is getting a 10% open rate and another inbox is getting a 75% open rate, that inbox with a 10% open rate can be tossed to the side and not used in any other campaigns. 

No other metric (such as watching for replies on the campaign or out of office replies) gives you enough of an inbox-level guide on this. They can tell you if the overall campaign is going well but not the immediate warning on the inbox.

Inbox spam placement tests are okay, but due to the nature of the test, if you start sending too many of them, the spam test is going to recognize the email it's being sent from and then keep it in the primary box. 

Some of the built-in spam testing tools are repairing your inbox health, and if they detect it going to spam, they're moving to the inbox which would negate the test the next time you try.

### 166. 7366099727731310594

- URL: https://www.linkedin.com/posts/outboundphd_i-see-a-lot-of-people-that-are-getting-started-activity-7366099727731310594-WduB
- Author: 🦾Eric Nowoslawski (@outboundphd)
- Posted: 2025-08-26T13:30:30.606Z

#### Verbatim Content

I see a lot of people that are getting started with outbound cold email who obsess over what their domains look like. Our data shows that your domains don't matter all that much. 

 We have two examples to be able to point to 

1) One of our biggest customers is a publicly traded company that everyone has heard the domain of. 

They will have us email some leads in their CRM to re-engage people, and they'll have us email cold leads. 

Even with a company with that much name recognition, the only people who ask questions about the domains are the people on the reengage campaign, and it doesn't happen often at all. 

Most of the time, people just respond, and we pass it to the sales team for booking.

2) Ever since we started launching free test campaigns for people, the only way that we can economically make the free test work and launch within 24 hours is we have a pool of domains that we rent out to people, and they just borrow them. 

We have repeatedly gotten leads for people from these alternative domains, even though they don't forward anywhere, and we don't mention the company that we're launching the free test on. (Ben Wright just passed a free test with us with flying colors). 

The only rule we have for buying domains is we only buy .com and .co domains and don't mess with any of the other riff raff of the TLDs. 

From our experience, as long as the domain forwards your main domain, you're going to be all set.

### 168. 7368643885092343808

- URL: https://www.linkedin.com/posts/outboundphd_come-watch-me-disagree-with-jordan-crawford-activity-7368643885092343808-AagI
- Author: 🦾Eric Nowoslawski (@outboundphd)
- Posted: 2025-09-02T14:00:04.989Z

#### Verbatim Content

Come watch me disagree with Jordan Crawford like a Wal-Mart customer looking to speak to the manager.

Jordan Crawford and I go further back than GPT-4. We connected over our agreement that so much of what people do in outbound sales is cookie cutter copies of what everyone else is doing. 

We also took advantage of being some first movers on two trends. The proliferation of AI and the amount of data providers moving into the sales space that had their own product specialization.

But then, a difference in opinion emerged. 

Jordan would come up with absolutely insane ideas to leverage data to find companies that the stars aligned on and take a Total Addressable Market of 4,000 people and find the most perfect 400 people to reach out to with a message the provided value without the prospect ever interacting with you. (this doesn't always happen, he just made one great message that everyone in his market could receive.) 

My main critique is what are you going to say to everyone else in the market which he has solutions for in his own way of course. 

I would see his ideas that push the boundaries of what people think is possible and take the most scalable idea and apply it to everyone in the market. 

We joke together that he hand paints Mona Lisa's with his "permission less value" philosophy, and I print them with warehouses filled with commercial grade printers opting for a simpler, "if you researched this person for 10 minutes, what are you looking for and how does it change your messaging?" philosophy.

At his even on Sept. 18th in San Francisco, we will be presenting on the pros and cons of our view points and if you're in town, you should drop by (registration QR code is in the image or message Jordan). 

Hope to see some folks there!

### 169. 7365797659414118400

- URL: https://www.linkedin.com/posts/outboundphd_leadmagic-dropped-a-feature-in-their-api-activity-7365797659414118400-q6Xg
- Author: 🦾Eric Nowoslawski (@outboundphd)
- Posted: 2025-08-25T17:30:11.907Z

#### Verbatim Content

LeadMagic dropped a feature in their API outputs that I think many other companies are going to follow suit on and it will highly increase the accuracy of your lists. 

We've talked about it before, and many email verification tools are essentially guessing common email permutations.

first@
first.last@
firstlast@

etc. 

And while everyone has their own proprietary blends of more secrets beyond this, which is why we use so many different partners, LeadMagic just started including an employment verification in their outputs, which might be one of the first that I've ever seen to do so. 

Basically included in the cost of a LeadMagic credit, they are checking the person's LinkedIn profile and seeing if they still work at the company with the domain that you just used in your input, and they won't give you an email if they've moved on to a different company. 

When people move on to different companies, a lot of times they don't completely disconnect the email just in case any customers or partners email them. They can see it and pass it on to the right person. 

So when an email finder tries to validate the email, it'll still come back valid, and this is where LeadMagic can help clean up your list and only get you people that are actually working at the company. 

And they don't charge you for emails that aren't validated so this process is just included. 

Really nice feature drop from Jesse!

### 171. 7366469636898435076

- URL: https://www.linkedin.com/posts/outboundphd_ill-say-it-once-a-month-for-as-long-as-im-activity-7366469636898435076-qqKW
- Author: 🦾Eric Nowoslawski (@outboundphd)
- Posted: 2025-08-27T14:00:23.824Z

#### Verbatim Content

I'll say it once a month for as long as I'm doing outbound, your best positive response rates are from Email 1, and open tracking isn't that bad. 

We now send a couple million emails a month on behalf of our customers and get a ton of data on this. 

We have started cutting our emails to two or three sequences for two big reasons. 

1) most of your positive responses come from email #1. 

The only campaign that I can think of where this isn't true is when people do some kind of joke or gimmick between email 1 and 2 that 

2) I would rather reach out to a new person at a new company than follow up with somebody who might have already seen my offer two times before and has still ignored it. The odds that they will mark you as spam is so much higher. 

And for those of you that love longer sequences, when you use your list, re-enrich everyone for your custom signals and such, rewrite the messaging and go again. 

On open tracking, it's just so powerful to be able to know exactly which inboxes are having issues. We've started turning it on every Thursday and Friday and removing low performing inboxes on Saturday with the Smartlead API.

### 172. 7367179270500032513

- URL: https://www.linkedin.com/posts/outboundphd_claygetcellstatusinput-your-column-activity-7367179270500032513-JUSC
- Author: 🦾Eric Nowoslawski (@outboundphd)
- Posted: 2025-08-29T13:00:13.659Z

#### Verbatim Content

Clay.getCellStatus({{input your column}}) is one of my favorite Easter eggs in Clay that I totally didn't find. Full credit to JEREMY 🪐 ROSS 

The biggest use case we have for it is sometimes when we need things to run and then rerun if there's an error, we will duplicate the columns and use this formula to run it again if necessary. 

Before N8N changed their pricing and some nerds thought you could replace Clay with it they forgot that Clay has built a lot of load balancing, concurrency,  throttling, etc. into their native integrations. 

That is very difficult to get in a tool like Zapier or Make or N8N. 

Basically if you have 10 tables calling 1 api key, Clay handles it for you instead of just tossing back errors. 

Despite the fact that they've built so much to handle this, issues still occur. 

Here's a quick way you could use this formula. I hope I'm not stepping on Jeremy's toes here because he found this and I've seen him publicly post about it, so I don't think it's a secret of his. 

We constantly run out of our email validation credits from providers here and there that don't have top ups...(different problem I know). 

So we use this so that if we run out the back up provider will run BUT if the provider runs successfully and the email is blank because it wasn't valid, we won't pay to revalidate it again. 

Really great for error handling and also when you need to write a formula for something like email validation!

### 173. 7354546698469134353

- URL: https://www.linkedin.com/posts/outboundphd_prompting-tips-from-someone-that-spends-probably-activity-7354546698469134353-1uFt
- Author: 🦾Eric Nowoslawski (@outboundphd)
- Posted: 2025-07-25T16:22:53.785Z

#### Verbatim Content

Prompting tips from someone that spends probably $13k+ per month on OpenAI API calls. 

I'll break the tips into chatGPT user interface tips as well as API tips. 

My bias is of course going to be about outbound sales and cold email because this is where we spend from and 100% of this spend is on 4o mini API calls. 

Chat GPT Prompting Tips

1. Use transcription as much as possible. Straight in the UI or use whisprflow(dot)ai (can't tag them for some reason). I personally get frustrated with a prompt when I'm typing it out vs. talking and can add so much more detail. 

2. Got this one from Yash Tekriwal 🤔 - When you're working on something complex like a deep research request or something you want o3 to run or analyzing a lot of data, ask chatgpt to give you any follow up questions it might have before it runs fully. Helps you increase your prompt accuracy like crazy. 

3. I've found that o3 is pretty good at building simple automations in make as well so we will ask it to output what we want in a format that we can input into make and often we can build automations just by explaining what we need and then plugging in our logins in Make.

API prompting tips

1. Throwing back to the Chat GPT UI, but we will often create our complex prompts in the user interface first and then bring it into the API via Clay asking ChatGPT along the way on how to improve the prompt and help us think of edge cases. This can take any team member to a prompting pro immediately. 

2. Examples are your best friend. Giving examples of what you would want the output to be is how we can get our outputs to be the same format and not put "synergies" in every email we are sending. 

I tell the team, minimum 2 examples for single line outputs. 4 examples for anything more complex than that. 6 examples for industry tagging because that gets so odd. Save on costs by putting some real examples in your system prompt. 

3. Request the output in JSON. It keeps everything more uniform in the format you need. 

4. Speaking of JSON, ask the API to prove to you why it thinks what it thinks and then output the answer. Especially for company category tagging, I find this works really well. I see this greatly increase the accuracy of our results for 2 reasons. 

I think if AI has to take the extra second to prove to you why a company is an ecommerce brand, the results are demonstrably better. 

This is just a guess, but I also think that because LLMs basically work by guessing what the next best word is, if you have it tell you why it thinks something is a certain industry and then it gives the output, I think it's much more likely to be correct. 

Anything else you've found?

### 174. 7353108301317869568

- URL: https://www.linkedin.com/posts/outboundphd_results-of-an-email-deliverability-experiment-activity-7353108301317869568-HFGx
- Author: 🦾Eric Nowoslawski (@outboundphd)
- Posted: 2025-07-21T17:07:13.180Z

#### Verbatim Content

Results of an email deliverability experiment we just ran: Inboxes sending 50-60 emails per day after a 2-week warmup have nowhere near the lifespan of 30 emails per day from our testing. 

I explain it more in this video.

We just had a customer that we're about to send a lot of volume for, and we took a fraction of their inboxes and said, "Hey, we want to learn as fast as possible while letting the other inboxes warm up past 2 weeks. So instead of sending 30 per day, let’s send 50-60 because in the past we used to be able to do this and then throttle it back down to 30.” 

We immediately were getting good results from their campaigns, and then after 2-3 days, the results completely dropped off. 

In the past, we used to be able to use this strategy and the inboxes would last for 2-3 weeks. 

This is an interesting change as this used to be something we would do all the time and we haven't come back to it. It was nice to be able to test it again so that we can tell other people that it doesn't work.

When you first start sending from new inboxes, I would definitely recommend not to go over 30 per day from what we just saw on regular Google inboxes. 

More thoughts in the video below

### 175. 7352034435757678592

- URL: https://www.linkedin.com/posts/outboundphd_these-are-three-mistakes-i-see-when-people-activity-7352034435757678592-pTyc
- Author: 🦾Eric Nowoslawski (@outboundphd)
- Posted: 2025-07-18T18:00:03.679Z

#### Verbatim Content

These are three mistakes I see when people are making lead magnets to use in their outbound campaigns. 

Mistake #1: It’s Not Tied to the Sales Process

The lead magnet should naturally lead into your sales conversation.

Ask: “If they say yes to this, are they more likely to buy?”

Mistake #2: It's Not Valuable Enough

Is this something your ICP would actually want?

Would they reply just to get it?

Mistake #3: You Don’t Qualify Leads Enough

Getting replies is great, but are they qualified?

We’ve seen this with our free test offer. It works and people reply, but sometimes those same leads don’t close because of an objection we didn’t uncover early. 

A lead magnet should help you disqualify bad fits as much as attract good ones.

### 176. 7358198799078420480

- URL: https://www.linkedin.com/posts/outboundphd_one-thing-i-never-thought-would-become-a-activity-7358198799078420480-BfKt
- Author: 🦾Eric Nowoslawski (@outboundphd)
- Posted: 2025-08-04T18:15:02.423Z

#### Verbatim Content

One thing I never thought would become a bottleneck in our workflows was API rate limits. LeadMagic is blowing away our normal validator just based on speed. 

Ever since we crossed over 2M email sends per month, we have started running into API rate limits that I didn't think would bottleneck us because we absolutely never had issues before. 

But running 2-300 Clay tables totaling 25+ million enrichments per week is taking a toll on our infrastructure. One of the most common email validation tools we use is Debounce, but we're starting to swap that out for LeadMagic just because of how fast and accurate it is. 

Just check out this video of it getting leads into our campaigns so much faster than an API column that had been running for about 10 minutes.

### 177. 7345805896905572352

- URL: https://www.linkedin.com/posts/outboundphd_im-getting-more-and-more-people-asking-me-activity-7345805896905572352-jiGG
- Author: 🦾Eric Nowoslawski (@outboundphd)
- Posted: 2025-07-01T13:30:04.296Z

#### Verbatim Content

I'm getting more and more people asking me about leveraging outbound signals which is great, but what are you going to say to everyone else? 

1. New hires in your TAM
2. New technology detected on site
3. Deanonymized web visitors
4. Open role
5. company headcount growth
6. fundraising
7. LinkedIn post about certain topics
8. Etc.

All great signals and all things that you should be doing but you also need to be thinking about an email or outbound message you can send to someone that still is in your ICP that doesn't have any of these signals. 

Maybe 5-15% of your target market is going to be in this window at any given time. What else are you preparing to send them?

### 178. 7370818241109323776

- URL: https://www.linkedin.com/posts/outboundphd_whenever-i-post-about-the-average-reply-rate-activity-7370818241109323776-yEkx
- Author: 🦾Eric Nowoslawski (@outboundphd)
- Posted: 2025-09-08T14:00:11.861Z

#### Verbatim Content

Whenever I post about the average reply rate that we see, agency owners I'm close with message me and tell me to stop because their customers then ask about getting that average reply. But often people are asking the wrong question. 

We get asked all the time, "What's a response rate that we can expect?"

The real answer is: If you were to take all of our customers and divide the amount of positive responses we get by how many emails we have to send out, the number right now is about 1 positive response out of 425 contacts that we enroll. (Up from 1 in 300 2 years ago I might add). 

But there's so much nuance underneath this. We have some people that get one positive response for every 78 people that they reach out to. 

Their LTV is $2,000.

Another customer, (who has happily worked with us for a year I might add) gets 1 positive response for every 2,000 leads.

Their LTV is $200k

 Asking what the average is fine, so that you can have an expectation of performance. But you also need to consider:

How many experiments have you run?
Have you truly thought through a lot of ideas and tested them?
How many times have you changed your copywriting and framing?

As long as you're constantly experimenting and trying new things, your response rate eventually is just what your response rate is in your market. And you have to make sure that that math works out for your business in particular.

### 179. 7356315198653177856

- URL: https://www.linkedin.com/posts/outboundphd_anytime-someone-says-we-want-to-hire-your-activity-7356315198653177856--ejQ
- Author: 🦾Eric Nowoslawski (@outboundphd)
- Posted: 2025-07-30T13:30:17.086Z

#### Verbatim Content

Anytime someone says "we want to hire your for email deliverability consulting." I always say no, and here's why. 

Email deliverability might feel like a moving target at times, but at the same time, there are very well-documented examples of things that work and how to do it. 

Smartlead / Instantly.ai both publish a tremendous amount of resources that basically both agree on the same exact things. Sending from multiple domains with 2-3 inboxes on each domain with 15-30 emails per day and 2 weeks of warming. 

People who are telling you that they have some other crazy email deliverability secret are either working off of a hack that isn't going to be around for a long time or is lying to you. 

So if you're unsure about what you should do next, about your email deliverability, the major platforms all have phenomenal guides. 

You can use a platform like Zapmail.ai or Hypertide.io to set up inboxes quickly so you never have to worry about that part of the process. 

And then always keep enough inboxes so that when something does go wrong, you have more inboxes to pivot to, and you're always keeping those warming up. 

Don't pay for an expensive email deliverability consultant for them to do all the same things as everybody else.

Make sure your DKIM, SPF, and DMARC are all aligned.

Use a number of free email testing tools in ChatGPT to troubleshoot your own domain.

When it comes to your outbound sending domains, set them up through the providers I mentioned and use some of the name-brand outbound sequencers.

You'll be 90% of the way towards what an email deliverability guru will tell you.

### 180. 7346636355793289220

- URL: https://www.linkedin.com/posts/outboundphd_two-signals-that-i-know-youve-heard-me-say-activity-7346636355793289220-h4j0

Two signals that I know you've heard me say before but you really have to set up a workflow to be checking for these across your TAM. 

New hires across your TAM in your ICP
And social signals like targeting people that engage with certain content or post content of their own through Trigify.io. (don't tell people you saw they liked the post...) 

Both can be set up easily in Clay so that you can automatically email them or reach out on with something like HeyReach.io.

### 181. 7358127124764917762

- URL: https://www.linkedin.com/posts/outboundphd_it-feels-like-there-are-so-many-variables-activity-7358127124764917762-KkSn
- Author: 🦾Eric Nowoslawski (@outboundphd)
- Posted: 2025-08-04T13:30:13.935Z

#### Verbatim Content

It feels like there are so many variables that can go wrong for an outbound campaign, but there's really only three things you need to worry about to create a positive reply.

1. Inbox Infrastructure
2. The list you are sending to (and how AI is bridging the gap to your number 3)
3. Campaign Strategy/Copywriting

And each of these have their own checklists as well. But if you can think through every success (or failure) you have in outbound through these 3 lenses, I find that it makes scaling (or fixing things) easier.

### 182. 7346175832152784896

- URL: https://www.linkedin.com/posts/outboundphd_this-company-was-sending-3000-emails-a-day-activity-7346175832152784896-9g5n
- Author: 🦾Eric Nowoslawski (@outboundphd)
- Posted: 2025-07-02T14:00:03.732Z

#### Verbatim Content

This company was sending 3,000 emails a day through HubSpot and getting zero replies despite a no brainer offer.

“If you have a negative Google review, we’ll try to get it removed. You only pay if we succeed.”

Great pitch. But no meetings.

So we made two big changes:

Switched from HubSpot to Smartlead

Deliverability was killing them. They were landing in spam. Not because the offer was bad, but because the infrastructure wasn’t built for cold outreach.
Used Clay to surface real signals

We found companies with actual negative Google reviews.

Then quoted the review directly in the email.

Subject line: Amelia’s Google Review

Body: “Hey, saw Amelia’s review and had a quick question…”

After those two changes, they went from 0 replies to getting one positive response per 70 emails.

Lesson:

Even the best offers fail when delivery is broken and messaging lacks context.
But when you fix both, you stand out fast.

### 184. 7350894445325611008

- URL: https://www.linkedin.com/posts/outboundphd_this-clay-table-took-me-4-hours-to-build-activity-7350894445325611008-SeKJ
- Author: 🦾Eric Nowoslawski (@outboundphd)
- Posted: 2025-07-15T14:30:08.780Z

#### Verbatim Content

This Clay table took me 4 hours to build live in front of my team but ended up being perfect training for them. Send it to someone looking to figure out Clay. 

I gave my team the list and these were the instructions. 

List clean up instructions

Make sure that the companies are B2B companies
Make sure that they are not in any of our blacklist industries
Have AI try to assume how many contacts on LinkedIn are there
Try to figure out if a customer is worth more or less than $5,000
Do they have a sales team already?

After we qualifed each lead for this criteria, we needed to make this message happen. 


Subject: {{subject line gex free test insert gex}} question

Hey {{first_name}}, 

{{firstline insert gex free test campaign}}

The reason I ask is because we run outbound campaigns for companies like Clay and Instantly and I was wondering if we could launch a campaign for free for you. 

We could do things like:

{{creative ideas}}

Let me know if you’d want to give this a shot. 

We just need to hop on a call for 15 minutes to make sure we nail your test. 

%signature%


{{subject line gex free test insert gex}} = change based on a case study being found on the site, a competitor, or no one. 

{{firstline insert gex free test campaign}} = if competitor found, 

It seemed people compare you to {{competitor}} and I was wondering how you think about acquiring customers cheaper than them.

If case study found, I saw the work you did with {{case study}} helping them {{insert what they do}} and I was wondering if you run outbound campaigns to get more customers like them.

{{creative ideas}} = First bullet point is targeting new ICP in role with messaging.
Second bullet is based on the case study or competitor or a fallback option.
Third bullet is about use social listening tools to be on the lookout for activity and posting that makes sense to them.

Check out this video on how we built the table. 

https://lnkd.in/eG6hRgzf

### 186. 7350583232821714944

- URL: https://www.linkedin.com/posts/outboundphd_heres-how-we-decide-to-use-clay-zenrows-activity-7350583232821714944-l1DL
- Author: 🦾Eric Nowoslawski (@outboundphd)
- Posted: 2025-07-14T17:53:29.937Z

#### Verbatim Content

Here's how we decide to use Clay, ZenRows, or Google Search (via Serper) when customers ask us to score websites for their ICP fit.

Claygent

Primary use-case

Grab one specific data point that could live anywhere across a website or across multiple domains.

Pros

Great for single-field extraction when the data’s location is uncertain.

Can bounce across multiple pages or websites to find an answer.

Quick setup—no need for manual scraping logic or selectors.

Cons

Falls short with multi-field extraction (4–7+ fields often leads to misses. It will pull 4-7 fields but might miss things here and there).

No training on examples: you can’t show it what a correct result looks like to improve accuracy.

Doesn’t self-verify: it doesn’t double-check that the result is correct or from the right page.

Prone to domain drift: can accidentally pull from external sources or irrelevant pages.

Google Search (site:, inurl:, "keyword", etc.)

Primary use-case

A precise keyword check: “Does this domain mention this thing?”

Especially useful for classification or competitor scans.

Pros

Highly accurate for keyword presence on specific sites.

Great for generating lead lists or checking competitor mentions.

Easy to validate matches via the preview text in results.

Cons

Most expensive tool in this stack (API/SERP scraping isn’t cheap).

Can be rate-limited or blocked for bulk querying.

Query operators can be unreliable—what you expect isn't always what you get.

Zenrows

Primary use-case

When you know the exact page and want to extract structured or unstructured data from it.

Especially helpful for multi-field scrapes, like pulling programmatic SEO pages, Crunchbase pages, or long-form content.

Pros

Comes with strong proxy support, helping avoid blocks.

Auto-parses full pages into JSON, which is ideal for AI-based keyword analysis or structured data extraction.

Great for reliable, repeatable pulls from pages with a consistent layout.

Cons

Cannot find the page for you—you must feed it the correct URL.

Occasionally fails to parse data you expect it to get.

Overkill for single data points (Claygent is better for those cases).

Anything you would add here?

### 187. 7346273978065252352

- URL: https://www.linkedin.com/posts/outboundphd_you-have-too-much-access-to-ai-tools-to-send-activity-7346273978065252352-xXKo
- Author: 🦾Eric Nowoslawski (@outboundphd)
- Posted: 2025-07-02T20:30:03.541Z

#### Verbatim Content

You have too much access to AI tools to send boring emails still...

In 2025, tools like Clay and Ocean.io give us so many filters we can use to build smarter lists.

But what I see too often is a spray-and-pray approach.

Someone will say:
 "I sell to the banking industry."
 "And I’m targeting companies with 20 to 500 employees."
 "And anyone with a Director of Marketing title or above."

The problem?
 
A Director of Marketing at a 20-person bank is a completely different person than one at a 500-person bank.

If your targeting doesn’t reflect that, your messaging won’t resonate.

Here’s a better approach:

 Look at your current customers.
 Understand what they actually value about your product or service.
 
Then ask yourself:
Where are the breakpoints in company size, seniority, or growth stage where your product becomes more relevant?

It’s usually not something as simple as:
 1–10 employees
 11–25 employees
 26–50 employees

You need to go deeper.

Use filters in Clay like:
Signals
Social Posts
Keywords on website
Past Case Studies
Competitors
Job title
Job focus (based on work experience and keywords) 
etc. 

Start building smarter lists and better outreach by mining for keywords and intent signals.

You don’t even have to use AI (yet).

But when you do start using AI, Clay becomes even more powerful.

Let’s take an example:

Say you’re selling for an accounting firm.

Sure, any company might need an accountant, but wouldn’t it make more sense to target:

- Companies that are new (they likely haven’t hired an accountant yet)
- Companies that have raised money (investors demand clean books)
- Companies where the CEO is a first-time founder (they’re likely to need more guidance)


Now you’ve got a far more compelling and relevant message. And the best part?

Clay lets you dynamically change your messaging based on:
When all 3 criteria are true
When 2 of them are true
When just 1 is true

Even when none are true (but they’re still worth reaching out to)

Smarter filters → Smarter lists → Better messaging → Better results.

Stop sending generic emails.

Start building systems that actually reflect who your best customers are.

Etc.

### 190. 7347987796378173441

- URL: https://www.linkedin.com/posts/outboundphd_heres-a-framework-we-use-internally-to-write-activity-7347987796378173441-uhXR
- Author: 🦾Eric Nowoslawski (@outboundphd)
- Posted: 2025-07-07T14:00:09.668Z

#### Verbatim Content

Here's a framework we use internally to write our email steps for a 3 email sequence. 

If you don't learn anything else from this post, just remember to not thread the whole sequence to just be follow up emails. You're just reminding someone that they didn't respond to your other emails...

Email 1 – Introduce Your Offer Clearly

- Start with a strong reason for reaching out — why you, why now
- Clearly explain your offer in as few words as possible
- Show social proof that others have seen success with your offer
- End with a direct call to action like asking if they want to chat next Thursday

(I know soft CTAs are all the rage on LinkedIn but my customers need meetings.)

Email 2 – Add Context

Use this email to include important supporting details that didn’t fit in email 1.

Because it's threaded, they can scroll up to see the original email, so you can dive deeper without repeating yourself.

The goal is to enrich the conversation and give them more reason to care.

Email 3 – Lower the Friction
 
If there’s still no response, it’s time to reduce the barrier to replying.

Instead of asking for a meeting, offer something with less commitment—like a lead magnet, a quick audit, or a Loom video.

The goal here is to get any sign of interest, not a booked call.

Make sure this has a new subject line and set as a net new thread so you don't remind someone that they already ignored you.

### 191. 7349074952257523713

- URL: https://www.linkedin.com/posts/outboundphd_i-had-a-call-with-manny-adelstein-to-help-activity-7349074952257523713-MyDI
- Author: 🦾Eric Nowoslawski (@outboundphd)
- Posted: 2025-07-10T14:00:07.828Z

#### Verbatim Content

I had a call with Manny Adelstein to help with Clay's email infrastructure and this is what we talked about. 

Most people underbuild their outbound infrastructure.

They buy 3–5 domains, warm them up, and hope deliverability holds.

But if you’re serious about scale, that’s not enough.

Here’s what we’ve learned running high-volume outbound:

If you want to send 1,000 emails per day, you need the capacity to send 3,000.

Why? Because domains burn. Even if your copy is clean and your targeting is perfect, some emails will hit spam. On average, a domain lasts ~2 months before you need to rotate it.

So we use a three-set system:

Set 1: actively sending
Set 2: fully warmed and on deck
Set 3: aging in the background

When Set 1 gets tired, you switch to Set 2, start warming Set 4, and keep going. The machine never stops.

Here’s the tip most people miss: buy domains early and let them age.

 Even if you’re not using them yet, aging them means when you need new inboxes, you’re not starting from scratch with a zero-trust domain.

We buy hundreds (sometimes thousands) of domains when there are sales.

Right now, Dynadot is running a promo: .coms for $7 and .cos for $2.

We’ll grab thousands and let them sit—no inbox cost until we need them.

Use Zapmail.ai and Hypertide.io for inbox setup. They give you admin access, don’t mix your domains with shady senders, and plug right into SmartLead.

Don’t wait until deliverability drops to prepare.

Stockpile aged domains now, and you’ll always be ready to scale or recover—without missing a beat.

I know some people will say, "If you send good emails, you don't need to do this."

We just had someone with literally an 8% reply rate which 80% of them being positive go from an 8% reply rate to 1% because of people still marking them as spam. 

I wish it wasn't this way either, but this is just what we have seen that we have to do.

### 193. 7351234198579421184

- URL: https://www.linkedin.com/posts/outboundphd_if-i-had-to-start-cold-email-all-over-again-activity-7351234198579421184-vAn7
- Author: 🦾Eric Nowoslawski (@outboundphd)
- Posted: 2025-07-16T13:00:12.268Z

#### Verbatim Content

If I had to start cold email all over again, here's exactly what I would do:

Start by understanding that cold email success comes down to just three things:

Sending, List Building, and Campaign Strategy. Nail those, and you'll win.

1. Sending
 Use a real outbound platform like Smartlead.ai or Instantly.ai — not MailChimp, HubSpot, or private SMTPs.

Send from Google or Outlook inboxes, not from sketchy servers.

Buy .com or .co domains only.

Don’t worry too much about how the domain looks, it doesn't matter nearly as much as you think.

Buy your domains from Porkbun or Dynadot, not GoDaddy. They often have domain sales. (one right now for .co domains for $2 and .com for $7).

Use a vendor like Zapmail.ai or Hypertide.io to spin up inboxes, you want to have admin console access.

Warm up your inboxes properly, especially for Outlook (sometimes for 4–6 weeks if you are targeting large enterprises.)

2. List Building

Realize that most list building platforms are just scraping LinkedIn and guessing emails. 

If you’re targeting local businesses, Google Maps scraping works well.

Generic emails like contact@ can work — especially with small businesses.

3. Campaign Strategy (What You Say)

Use a proven format that you can iterate on weekly.

Use ChatGPT to mimic styles like John Barrows, Josh Braun, or Will Allred.

If campaigns fail, adjust your template and list filters fast. Usually after sending to 1,000 contacts. 

Don’t rely on seven-step sequences. Two or three emails max — most replies come from email one.

Prioritize sending more net-new emails over sending tons of follow-ups.

Misc. Advice 

Ignore LinkedIn screenshots bragging about reply rates. Most of the people bragging are running campaigns they can't run for more than a week.

Use open tracking when you're unsure about deliverability. It's not the boogie man people make it sound. It can tell you within a day if you're having real issues with deliverability. Always use custom tracking domains. 

Don’t obsess over fake open rates from Apple devices — it still gives a good sense of inboxing.

Think about what's in it for the prospect. If they respond, what will they get in return.

### 195. 7348727650892365825

- URL: https://www.linkedin.com/posts/outboundphd_2-ai-workflows-that-we-used-in-the-last-year-activity-7348727650892365825-bK5J
- Author: 🦾Eric Nowoslawski (@outboundphd)
- Posted: 2025-07-09T15:00:04.730Z

#### Verbatim Content

2 AI workflows that we used in the last year. 1 was a terrible fail, and 1 is so good I get people emailing it to me all the time. 

I thought it was so smart to use company analogies to relate how a company helps their customers to how we help our customers. Someone actually gave me this idea but I won't tag them because it flopped so hard for us. 

In the past, I even made YouTube videos including it and how I thought it was such a good idea. 

Basically saying, hey a good way to think of us is we both help people save time (but more AI than that.)

We never got a bad reaction from it, but we never saw an improvement in reply rates either. 

BUT 

Using Claygent to research and find case studies on someone's site is a go to with my team at Growth Engine X.

You can easily prompt Claygent to find case studies on a prospect's website and how the company helped them so you can relate it to your own offer. 

How you can help them find more customers like that
How you can help them with their customer success on that deal
How you can help them with the pricing of that customer
Etc. 

We'll never do the analogy thing again but we do the case study AI all the time right now.

### 196. 7345439733214314497

- URL: https://www.linkedin.com/posts/outboundphd_two-reasons-why-companies-that-invest-heavily-activity-7345439733214314497-AzY9
- Author: 🦾Eric Nowoslawski (@outboundphd)
- Posted: 2025-06-30T13:15:04.069Z

#### Verbatim Content

Two reasons why companies that invest heavily in outbound automation aren't firing their SDRs and still investing in both. 

I get the privilege to speak to large companies that are doing some unbelievable email volume, and when I ask them if they ever think it will replace SDRs, they all tell me that outbound automation supplements what they're doing with SDRs; it doesn't replace what they're doing with SDRs. 

Two big ways that SDRs get an ROI in these teams despite having gigantic, multi-million dollar outbound automation programs are:

1. They are better at picking up on things that you would never automate with AI. For instance, there is a rep that I know is from Germany, and anytime he can connect the dots on any possible German prospect that he's reaching out to, he always does that. 

Never in a million years is anybody going to automate any kind of relationship, ethnicity, or geography thing like that.

2. When you're doing outbound automation, there's no marginal cost to send more emails, so the reply rate on a per-send basis could be higher with SDRs because you're including everyone - including office managers and maybe executive assistants and interns - into very large outbound automation programs. Just in case you get a person that could possibly still respond when it's still relevant.

There's just an account selection bias that humans can still do. Maybe something came up that the model wasn't trained on, but they know that it's a good reason to reach out, and they can reach out to better accounts because of that.

Keep in mind that despite what you might here on LinkedIn, one strategy might not be dead or alive. It all depends on your use case, your CAC, your LTV, and the way you can make the math work.

### 197. 7351596569344786433

- URL: https://www.linkedin.com/posts/outboundphd_delete-all-memories-youve-ever-had-about-activity-7351596569344786433-9hbG
- Author: 🦾Eric Nowoslawski (@outboundphd)
- Posted: 2025-07-17T13:00:08.191Z

#### Verbatim Content

Delete all memories you've ever had about breakup emails. 

I don't know who invented this and then who subsequently popularized it, but it's a waste of an email that's going to get you marked as spam and look desperate. 

Oren Klaff has a great point in his books that there are two kinds of sales people.

Those that are experts and have a solution to help their customers and those that are needy with commission breath begging for sales. I can't think of anything else that looks more needy than a breakup email. 

Either just cut your sequence short and email other people who are much more likely to be interested or use your last email to fact find/lower the friction of getting a hand raiser.

- URL: https://www.linkedin.com/posts/outboundphd_lookalikesjust-a-great-outbound-play-book-activity-7344390315002257408-Hb6S
- Author: 🦾Eric Nowoslawski (@outboundphd)

Lookalikes...just a great outbound play book that everyone should be using. We personally use Ocean.io for this. 

Many people, when they're first doing their outbound campaigns, could really benefit from doing lookalikes mainly because of the power it gives you to know what messaging to be using. 

When you use lookalikes, not only are you creating a better list, but you also know what the success has been for a customer that's in their shoes. 

You were successful with this customer already in the past, you know going forward what to use in that messaging to connect the dots for the person that you're sending to.
